articles.excerpt: Key due dates for filing ITR for FY 2025-26: 31 July for individuals, 31 October for audit cases, 30 November for transfer pricing cases. Miss them and you pay Section 234F late fees.
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

The due date for filing Income Tax Returns (ITR) is a critical compliance requirement under the **Income Tax Act, 1961**. For **Financial Year (FY) 2025-26** corresponding to **Assessment Year (AY) 2026-27**, the last date depends on the category of taxpayer and audit applicability.

Timely filing ensures eligibility for benefits such as loss carry forward, timely refunds, and avoidance of penalties.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

- Avoids late filing fees and interest
- Enables carry forward of losses
- Ensures faster processing of refunds
- Maintains compliance record
- Reduces risk of notices or follow-ups

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

- Missing deadlines leads to penalties under **Section 234F**
- Loss of certain tax benefits if filed late
- Additional compliance burden if delayed
- Interest liability on unpaid taxes

## 4) Applicability Across India

ITR due dates apply uniformly across India:

- Applicable in all States and Union Territories
- No regional variation
- Governed centrally under the Income Tax Act, 1961

## 5) Features

- Different due dates based on type of taxpayer
- Separate deadlines for audit and non-audit cases
- Possibility of extension by government notification
- Filing available through online portal

## 6) Documents Required

- Form 16 / income details
- Form 26AS / AIS
- Bank statements
- Investment proofs and deduction details
- Business financials (if applicable)

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Gather Details** — Collect income, deduction, and tax proofs.
2. **Select ITR Form** — Choose the appropriate form based on income type.
3. **Compute Income** — Calculate total taxable income.
4. **Verify Taxes Paid** — Cross-check TDS, advance tax, and self-assessment tax.
5. **Pay Remaining Tax** — Clear any balance before filing.
6. **File Return** — Submit online through the e-filing portal.
7. **Verify Return** — Complete e-verification or send signed ITR-V.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

- No fee for filing within due date
- Late filing fee applicable if deadline is missed
- Interest may apply on unpaid tax

## Due Dates for FY 2025-26 (AY 2026-27)

- **31st July 2026** — Individuals and non-audit cases
- **31st October 2026** — Businesses requiring audit
- **30th November 2026** — Cases involving transfer pricing

These dates may be extended by official notification if required.

> **Key Insight** — Filing before the due date is especially important for taxpayers with losses — late returns lose the right to carry forward losses against future income.

## Practical Interpretation

The filing deadline is not just procedural. It directly impacts tax outcomes, especially for taxpayers with losses, refunds, or complex income structures.

Filing early also reduces last-minute errors and system-related delays.

## Conclusion

The ITR filing last date is a key milestone in the tax compliance cycle. Meeting the deadline ensures access to benefits and avoids penalties.

A structured and timely approach aligned with the Income Tax Act, 1961 helps maintain accurate and efficient tax reporting.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: STCG (Short-Term Capital Gains Tax) \u2014 What Every Investor Must Know
articles.excerpt: Understand STCG tax in India: the new 20% rate on equity (post July 2024), asset-wise holding periods, set-off rules, FIFO method, and strategies to reduce your short-term tax bill.
articles.content:
## Overview

Short-term capital gains tax catches many investors off guard, particularly those who trade actively or redeem mutual funds before the holding period threshold. Unlike LTCG, which benefits from lower preferential rates and exemptions, STCG is taxed more aggressively — sometimes at rates equivalent to your income tax slab.

The defining factor is always the **holding period**. Sell too early and you forfeit the long-term benefit entirely. For equity investors who actively trade, STCG on listed shares can be a significant drag on returns that rarely gets modelled into investment decisions.

Budget 2024 increased the STCG rate on equity and equity-oriented assets from **15% to 20%**, effective July 23, 2024. This single change materially altered the math for short-term equity traders and those who exit mutual funds within a year.

## Tax Rates at a Glance

| Asset Type | Holding Period for STCG | STCG Tax Rate |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Listed Equity Shares | 12 months or less | 20% (post July 23, 2024) |
| Equity Mutual Funds | 12 months or less | 20% (post July 23, 2024) |
| Debt Mutual Funds | Any period (post Apr 2023) | Slab rate |
| Real Estate | 24 months or less | Slab rate |
| Gold (Physical) | 24 months or less | Slab rate |
| Unlisted Shares | 24 months or less | Slab rate |
| Listed Bonds / Debentures | 12 months or less | Slab rate |
| REITs / InvITs | 12 months or less | 20% (equity component) |

> [!NOTE] Cess on top, slab rate adds to income
> 4% health and education cess applies on top of all rates. For slab-rate taxation, gains are simply added to your total income for the year.

## How STCG Is Calculated

**STCG = Sale Price − Cost of Acquisition − Brokerage and Transfer Charges**

No indexation benefit is available for STCG on any asset class.

### Calculation Example: STCG on Listed Equity Shares

Priya bought 1,000 shares of a company at Rs. 150 in August 2024. She sold them in January 2025 at Rs. 210.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Sale Value | Rs. 2,10,000 |
| Cost of Acquisition | Rs. 1,50,000 |
| Brokerage (0.1%) | Rs. 210 |
| STCG | Rs. 59,790 |
| Tax at 20% | Rs. 11,958 |
| Add Cess at 4% | Rs. 478 |
| **Total Tax Payable** | **Rs. 12,436** |

Had Priya held beyond 12 months, her tax on the same gain would have been roughly Rs. 6,000 after the Rs. 1.25 lakh exemption. The cost of exiting early more than doubled her tax bill.

## Exemptions and Set-Off Rules

Unlike LTCG, there is no blanket exemption on STCG from equity. However, the following offsets apply:

### Set-Off Against Losses

- STCG can be set off against Short-Term Capital Losses (STCL) in the same year
- STCG **cannot** be set off against Long-Term Capital Losses (LTCL)
- Unabsorbed STCL can be carried forward for **8 assessment years**

### Securities Transaction Tax (STT)

The preferential STCG rate of 20% on equity applies only when STT has been paid at both the time of purchase and sale. Transactions on recognised stock exchanges automatically satisfy this condition.

## Strategy Tips

1. **Track your holding period obsessively.** A difference of even one day can shift you from LTCG to STCG. Most brokerage platforms now show holding-period data, but always double-check when planning exits.
2. **Bundle short-term losses with short-term gains.** If you have losing positions in your portfolio, consider booking them in the same financial year as your large short-term profits. This directly reduces your STCG liability.
3. **Avoid churning equity mutual funds within 12 months.** Switching between funds or redeeming within a year defeats the purpose of equity investing and generates STCG. SIPs use FIFO (first in, first out) for taxation, so older units are redeemed first.
4. **Consider futures and options taxation separately.** F&O income is not treated as capital gains at all. It is classified as business income and taxed at slab rates, with the advantage of deducting business expenses.
5. **Use advance tax planning.** If you realise significant STCG mid-year, pay advance tax by the June 15 installment to avoid interest under Sections 234B and 234C.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. I bought shares in two tranches at different prices. How does STCG get calculated when I sell only part of my holding?

**FIFO (First In, First Out)** is generally the method used for calculating gains when you partially sell a holding. The shares purchased earliest are assumed to be sold first. This can affect whether the gain is classified as short-term or long-term depending on when each tranche was purchased.

### Q2. Is there any way to legally reduce STCG on equity when I need to exit within 12 months?

There is no specific exemption for STCG on equity. Your best options are to offset it with STCL from other positions, delay the sale beyond the 12-month mark if feasible, or factor the tax cost into your exit price calculations so you are not caught off guard.

### Q3. My employer gave me ESOPs that vested and I sold immediately. Is that STCG?

ESOP taxation has two stages. At vesting, the difference between FMV and the exercise price is taxed as perquisite income under salary. When you subsequently sell the shares, any further gain or loss is treated as capital gains, with the holding period determined from the date of **exercise**, not the grant date.

### Q4. Can STCG on equity be offset against losses from selling physical gold?

Yes. STCG from equity can be set off against STCL from any asset, including gold. However, LTCL from gold cannot be set off against STCG from equity.

### Q5. If I am in the 30% tax bracket, is STCG from equity still taxed at 20% or at 30%?

STCG from listed equity and equity-oriented funds is taxed at the **flat 20% rate** regardless of your income tax slab. It is not added to your regular income. This is actually a relief for high-income earners compared to debt fund gains, which are added to income and taxed at up to 30%.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 80DDB of Income Tax Act: Deduction Limit, Diseases Covered, Claim Deduction & Certificate
articles.excerpt: Section 80DDB provides deduction for treatment of specified diseases — up to ₹40,000 for individuals and ₹1,00,000 for senior citizens. Requires a certificate from an authorised specialist.
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

**Section 80DDB** of the Income Tax Act, 1961 provides a deduction for expenses incurred on treatment of specified diseases for self or dependents. It is intended to reduce the financial burden in cases involving serious medical conditions that require prolonged and costly treatment.

The deduction is available to individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs), subject to limits and certification requirements.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

- Provides tax relief for high medical expenses
- Covers treatment of serious and specified illnesses
- Available for self and dependent family members
- Higher deduction limit for senior citizens
- Reduces overall taxable income

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

- Applicable only to specified diseases
- Deduction limited to prescribed amounts
- Requires medical certificate from authorized specialist
- Reduced by amount received from insurance or employer
- Documentation requirements must be strictly followed

## 4) Applicability Across India

Section 80DDB applies uniformly across India:

- Applicable in all States and Union Territories
- No regional variation
- Available to resident individuals and HUFs under the Income Tax Act, 1961

## 5) Features

- Deduction allowed for actual medical expenses incurred
- Maximum limit depends on age category
- Covers diseases such as **cancer, neurological disorders, chronic renal failure**, etc.
- Requires certification from a specialist doctor
- Deduction reduced by reimbursement received

## 6) Documents Required

- Medical certificate from prescribed specialist
- Bills and receipts for treatment
- Proof of payment
- Insurance claim details (if any)
- PAN and income details

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Verify Eligibility** — Check if disease and relationship qualify.
2. **Obtain Certificate** — Get a medical certificate from an authorised specialist.
3. **Calculate Expenses** — Total all medical expenses incurred.
4. **Deduct Reimbursement** — Reduce by any insurance/employer reimbursement.
5. **Apply Limit** — Use the lower of actual expenses or the prescribed cap.
6. **Claim in ITR** — Include the deduction in the income tax return.
7. **Retain Records** — Keep all documents for future verification.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

- No government fee for claiming deduction
- Benefit reflected as reduction in taxable income
- Non-compliance may lead to disallowance of deduction

## Deduction Limits

- Up to **₹40,000** for individuals below 60 years
- Up to **₹1,00,000** for senior citizens

The deduction is restricted to the lower of actual expenses or prescribed limit.

> **Key Insight** — Any amount reimbursed by an insurer or employer must be subtracted before claiming Section 80DDB. Double-claiming reimbursed amounts is a common reason for disallowance.

## Practical Interpretation

Section 80DDB is a targeted relief provision aimed at mitigating the financial impact of critical illnesses. While the deduction is beneficial, it is closely regulated through certification and specified disease lists.

Proper documentation and accurate reporting are essential to claim the benefit.

## Conclusion

Section 80DDB provides meaningful tax relief for taxpayers facing high medical expenses due to serious illnesses. By allowing deduction within defined limits, it reduces taxable income and supports financial planning during medical emergencies.

Understanding eligibility and maintaining proper records ensures effective utilization of this provision under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Employer's Contribution to NPS in Taxable Salary
articles.excerpt: Employer's contribution to NPS qualifies for deduction under Section 80CCD(2) — up to 10% of salary for private sector and 14% for government employees — outside the Section 80C limit.
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Employer's contribution to the **National Pension System (NPS)** is treated as a tax-efficient component of salary under the **Income Tax Act, 1961**. It is governed primarily by **Section 80CCD(2)**, which allows a deduction for contributions made by the employer on behalf of the employee.

While it forms part of the overall compensation structure, it receives preferential tax treatment, making it one of the more effective tools for optimizing taxable salary.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

- Provides additional deduction beyond Section 80C limits
- Reduces taxable income significantly for higher salary levels
- Encourages long-term retirement savings
- Available under both old and new tax regimes
- No requirement for separate investment by employee

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

- Contribution is subject to percentage limits of salary
- Funds are locked in until retirement, limiting liquidity
- Taxation applies at withdrawal stage as per rules
- Dependent on employer offering NPS as part of compensation

## 4) Applicability Across India

Employer contribution to NPS is applicable across India:

- Uniform treatment in all States and Union Territories
- No regional variation
- Applicable to salaried individuals under the Income Tax Act, 1961

## 5) Features

- Deduction available under **Section 80CCD(2)**
- Contribution up to **10%** of salary (Basic + DA) for private sector employees
- Higher limit (up to **14%**) for certain government employees
- Not included within ₹1.5 lakh limit of Section 80C
- Considered part of salary but allowed as deduction

## 6) Documents Required

- Salary structure or CTC breakup
- Employer contribution details
- NPS account statement
- Form 16 reflecting contribution
- PAN and employment details

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Employer Contributes** — Amount credited to employee's NPS account.
2. **Included in Salary** — Reflected in the salary structure on paper.
3. **Claim Deduction** — Apply Section 80CCD(2) while computing income.
4. **Form 16 Reference** — Contribution appears in Form 16 issued by employer.
5. **Report in ITR** — Disclose in the income tax return.
6. **Adjust Taxable Income** — Reduce the contribution from gross salary.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

- No fee for claiming deduction
- Tax benefit reduces taxable income
- Tax applicable at withdrawal stage as per NPS rules

## Practical Interpretation

Employer contribution to NPS stands out because it provides a deduction without requiring direct investment by the employee. For higher-income individuals, this can result in substantial tax savings while building a retirement corpus.

> **Key Insight** — Since 80CCD(2) sits outside the ₹1.5 lakh cap of Section 80C, it is one of the few ways to get additional tax benefit even after exhausting all other popular deductions.

It is often considered one of the most efficient components in salary structuring.

## Conclusion

Employer contribution to NPS offers a unique combination of tax efficiency and long-term financial planning. By providing an additional deduction outside standard limits, it helps optimize taxable income effectively.

Proper understanding and inclusion in salary planning ensures maximum benefit under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 44AD — Presumptive Scheme for Businesses
articles.excerpt: Section 44AD lets small businesses declare income at 6% (digital) or 8% (cash) of turnover instead of maintaining detailed books. Applicable for turnover up to ₹2 crore.
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

**Section 44AD** of the Income Tax Act, 1961 provides a presumptive taxation scheme for small businesses, allowing them to declare income at a fixed percentage of turnover instead of maintaining detailed books of accounts.

This provision is aimed at reducing compliance burden for small taxpayers while ensuring a standardized and simplified method of income reporting.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

- Eliminates requirement of detailed bookkeeping
- No mandatory tax audit if conditions are satisfied
- Simplifies income computation
- Reduces compliance and administrative effort
- Encourages digital transactions through lower presumptive rate

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

- Presumed income may be higher than actual profits
- Limited ability to claim actual expenses
- Restrictions on opting out once chosen
- Not applicable to certain businesses and entities
- Turnover limits restrict eligibility

## 4) Applicability Across India

Section 44AD applies uniformly across India:

- Applicable in all States and Union Territories
- No regional variation
- Applicable to resident individuals, HUFs, and partnership firms (excluding LLPs) under the Income Tax Act, 1961

## 5) Features

- Applicable where turnover does not exceed **₹2 crore**
- Presumptive income:
  - **8%** of turnover for cash transactions
  - **6%** of turnover for digital transactions
- No requirement to maintain detailed books of accounts
- Simplified advance tax payment (single installment)
- Option to declare higher income voluntarily

## 6) Documents Required

- Sales or turnover records
- Bank statements
- Basic invoices or receipts
- PAN and business details

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Check Eligibility** — Verify turnover limit and type of business.
2. **Calculate Turnover** — Total receipts for the financial year.
3. **Apply Presumptive Rate** — Use 6% or 8% based on mode of receipt.
4. **Compute Taxable Income** — Presumptive income = turnover × rate.
5. **Calculate Tax** — Determine liability using applicable slab rates.
6. **Pay Advance Tax** — Make payment in a single installment where applicable.
7. **File ITR-4** — Submit the return under the presumptive scheme.
8. **Maintain Basic Records** — Keep supporting records for verification.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

- No fee for opting for the scheme
- Tax payable on presumed income at applicable slab rates
- Interest or penalty may apply for non-compliance

> **Key Insight** — The 2% gap between cash (8%) and digital (6%) presumptive rates is a deliberate incentive to drive digital adoption and improve traceability of small-business income.

## Conclusion

Section 44AD offers a simplified taxation mechanism for small businesses by replacing detailed accounting with a fixed-rate income assumption. While it reduces compliance effort, it must be evaluated carefully based on actual profitability.

When used appropriately, it enables efficient compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961 while maintaining a balance between simplicity and accuracy.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Partner's Remuneration and How It is Calculated
articles.excerpt: Partner's remuneration is governed by Section 40(b) — on the first ₹3 lakh of book profit, higher of ₹1.5 lakh or 90%; on the remaining, 60%. Must be authorised in the partnership deed.
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Partner's remuneration refers to salary, bonus, commission, or any payment made by a partnership firm to its partners. Its tax treatment is governed by provisions of the **Income Tax Act, 1961**, particularly **Section 40(b)**, which lays down conditions and limits for allowability.

While remuneration is an expense for the firm, it is treated as **business income** in the hands of the partner. However, deduction to the firm is allowed only within prescribed limits and subject to specific conditions.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

- Allows firms to legitimately reduce taxable profits
- Provides structured compensation to working partners
- Clear limits reduce ambiguity in tax treatment
- Aligns profit distribution with partner involvement
- Recognized as a deductible expense (within limits)

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

- Deduction allowed only for working partners
- Must be authorized by partnership deed
- Subject to prescribed limits based on book profit
- Excess remuneration is disallowed
- Taxable in full in the hands of partner

## 4) Applicability Across India

Partner remuneration rules apply uniformly across India:

- Applicable in all States and Union Territories
- No regional variation
- Relevant for partnership firms and LLPs under the Income Tax Act, 1961

## 5) Features

- Deduction governed by **Section 40(b)**
- Applicable only to **working partners**
- Must be clearly defined in partnership deed
- Based on book profit of the firm
- Taxed as business income for partners

## 6) Documents Required

- Partnership deed specifying remuneration terms
- Books of accounts and profit computation
- Partner details and roles
- Financial statements of the firm
- Working of book profit

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Determine Book Profit** — Compute the firm's book profit.
2. **Identify Working Partners** — List eligible partners actively involved.
3. **Refer to Deed** — Check remuneration terms in the partnership deed.
4. **Apply Section 40(b) Limits** — Use the prescribed caps.
5. **Compute Allowable Amount** — Arrive at maximum deductible remuneration.
6. **Deduct in Firm's Return** — Reduce allowable portion from firm income.
7. **Tax in Partner's Hands** — Partner reports remuneration as business income.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

- No separate fee for remuneration calculation
- Tax payable by firm on remaining profits
- Partner pays tax on remuneration received
- Disallowed portion increases taxable income of firm

## How Remuneration is Calculated

The allowable remuneration is based on book profit:

- **On first ₹3 lakh of book profit** — Higher of **₹1.5 lakh** or **90%** of book profit
- **On remaining book profit** — **60%** of book profit

Any remuneration exceeding these limits is not allowed as deduction.

> **Key Insight** — Partner remuneration shifts tax liability from the firm (30% flat) to the partner (slab rates). For low-income partners, this can result in substantial overall tax savings.

## Practical Interpretation

Partner remuneration is both a compensation mechanism and a tax planning tool. While it reduces taxable income of the firm, it shifts the tax liability to partners.

Proper structuring through the partnership deed and adherence to limits is essential to ensure deductibility.

## Conclusion

Partner's remuneration must be carefully structured and calculated to comply with prescribed limits. When aligned with statutory provisions, it provides a legitimate way to distribute income and optimize tax liability.

Understanding its treatment ensures accurate compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961 and avoids disallowance during assessment.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Capital Gains on Property \u2014 The Complete Taxation Roadmap
articles.excerpt: Complete guide to capital gains tax on property sale in India: the 12.5% vs 20% with indexation choice post-Budget 2024, Section 54/54F/54EC exemptions, CGAS, and calculation examples.
articles.content:
## Overview

Selling a house or commercial property is often the largest financial transaction most Indians undertake in their lifetime, and the tax implications are proportionately significant. Property capital gains taxation is also one of the most misunderstood areas of Indian tax law — partly because the rules changed materially in Budget 2024, and partly because there are multiple exemptions available if you structure the transaction correctly.

Until July 22, 2024, sellers of real estate could claim indexation benefit and pay 20% LTCG. Post that date, the government moved to a flat **12.5% rate without indexation** for most property sales. A partial rollback followed: for properties purchased before July 23, 2024, individuals and HUFs can choose the more beneficial of the two options (20% with indexation **or** 12.5% without). For property bought after that date, only the 12.5% rate applies.

## Tax Rates and Holding Period

| Type of Property | Holding Period | Classification | Tax Rate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Residential / Commercial Property | Up to 24 months | STCG | Slab rate |
| Residential / Commercial Property | More than 24 months | LTCG | 12.5% (without indexation) |
| Property bought before July 23, 2024 | More than 24 months | LTCG | Option: 20% with indexation |
| Land | More than 24 months | LTCG | 12.5% |
| Under-construction property | From date of allotment | Depends on total hold | Slab / 12.5% |

Surcharge (if applicable) and 4% cess apply on top of these rates.

## Indexation and the 2024 Choice

The Cost Inflation Index (CII) is published annually by the government. It lets you inflate your purchase price to account for inflation, reducing your taxable gain.

### When is indexation worth it?

| Purchase Year | Purchase Price | Sale Price (2024) | Gain (No Indexation) | Indexed Cost (approx.) | Gain (With Indexation) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2005 | Rs. 20,00,000 | Rs. 1,20,00,000 | Rs. 1,00,00,000 | Rs. 56,00,000 | Rs. 64,00,000 |
| 2015 | Rs. 60,00,000 | Rs. 1,20,00,000 | Rs. 60,00,000 | Rs. 84,00,000 | Rs. 36,00,000 |

> [!IMPORTANT] Run both calculations before filing
> For very old properties, 12.5% without indexation might be better. For recently purchased properties, indexation often lowers the gain enough that 20% with indexation results in lower absolute tax. Always compute both scenarios.

## Full Calculation Example

**Scenario:** Sale of a residential flat purchased in 2012.

- Purchase price: Rs. 40,00,000 (March 2012)
- Sale price: Rs. 1,10,00,000 (October 2024)
- Registration and brokerage at sale: Rs. 2,00,000

### Option A: 12.5% without indexation

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Sale Price | Rs. 1,10,00,000 |
| Less: Acquisition cost | Rs. 40,00,000 |
| Less: Expenses on sale | Rs. 2,00,000 |
| **LTCG** | **Rs. 68,00,000** |
| Tax at 12.5% | Rs. 8,50,000 |
| Add Cess (4%) | Rs. 34,000 |
| **Total Tax** | **Rs. 8,84,000** |

### Option B: 20% with indexation

CII for 2011–12 = 200, CII for 2024–25 = 363 (approximate).

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Indexed Cost (40,00,000 × 363/200) | Rs. 72,60,000 |
| Sale Price | Rs. 1,10,00,000 |
| Less: Expenses on sale | Rs. 2,00,000 |
| **LTCG** | **Rs. 35,40,000** |
| Tax at 20% | Rs. 7,08,000 |
| Add Cess (4%) | Rs. 28,320 |
| **Total Tax** | **Rs. 7,36,320** |

**Option B is better here by over Rs. 1.47 lakh.** Always calculate both.

## Exemptions Available

### Section 54: Reinvest in Residential Property

If you sell a residential house and use the LTCG to buy or construct another residential house in India within specified timelines, the gains are exempt. Purchase must happen 1 year before or 2 years after sale. Construction must complete within 3 years of sale.

### Section 54F: Sell Any Non-Residential Asset, Buy a House

If you are selling a commercial property, land, or any non-residential asset, you can claim exemption by investing the **entire sale proceeds** (not just the gains) in a residential house.

### Section 54EC: Capital Gains Bonds

Invest up to Rs. 50 lakh of LTCG in bonds issued by NHAI, REC, or PFC within 6 months of the sale. The bonds carry a 5-year lock-in period and offer a modest fixed interest rate, but the tax saving is usually far larger than the opportunity cost.

### Capital Gains Account Scheme (CGAS)

If you cannot reinvest before your ITR filing deadline, deposit the gains in a CGAS account with a nationalised bank. This preserves the exemption claim while you arrange the investment.

## Strategy Tips

1. **Time the sale across two financial years if possible.** If the gain is very large and you cannot fully utilise exemptions, structuring the agreement and registration date near a financial year-end can split advance tax obligations.
2. **Register improvement costs.** If you spent money on renovation, extension, or structural improvement, these costs can be added to the cost of acquisition (with indexation where applicable). Maintain all receipts.
3. **Include stamp duty and registration in your acquisition cost.** The stamp duty paid when purchasing the property forms part of your cost of acquisition and reduces the taxable gain. This is commonly overlooked.
4. **Evaluate Section 54 vs 54EC.** If you do not want to buy another property, 54EC bonds may offer a cleaner exit. But the Rs. 50 lakh cap means large gains will still have a taxable portion.
5. **Use CGAS before the ITR deadline.** Do not wait to find a property and miss the ITR deadline. Park the funds in a CGAS account first, which preserves your exemption entitlement for up to 3 years.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. My property was inherited. How do I determine the cost of acquisition for capital gains?

For inherited property, you adopt the cost paid by the previous owner. If the property was acquired before April 1, 2001, you can use the **Fair Market Value as on April 1, 2001** as the cost, which is often significantly higher and reduces your gain.

### Q2. I am buying a property jointly with my spouse using the sale proceeds. Can both of us claim Section 54 exemption?

The exemption is linked to the seller. If you are the sole seller of the old property, the reinvestment in a joint property can still qualify for Section 54 exemption, provided your share in the new property corresponds to the gains reinvested.

### Q3. Can I claim Section 54 if I already own two houses at the time of sale?

Yes. Section 54 does not restrict the number of properties you can already own. The restriction is that the new property purchased for claiming Section 54 must be held for at least 3 years; selling it earlier results in the earlier exemption being reversed.

### Q4. What happens if I sell the property but the buyer pays in installments spread over two years?

Capital gains are taxed in the year of transfer (typically when a registered sale deed is executed), not in the year of receipt of consideration. You will need to declare and pay tax in the year of sale, even if you have not received the full payment yet.

### Q5. Is TDS applicable when I sell my property?

Yes. Under Section 194-IA, the buyer is required to deduct TDS at **1% of the sale consideration** if it exceeds Rs. 50 lakh. This is not the seller's final tax liability but a prepayment that is adjusted against the total tax due at filing time.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 10-IEA: Option to Choose Old Tax Regime | How to Fill & Submit Form 10-IEA Online
articles.excerpt: Form 10-IEA is used by taxpayers with business or professional income to opt out of the default new tax regime and choose the old regime. Step-by-step filing guide included.
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

**Form 10-IEA** is used by taxpayers to opt for or switch to the **old tax regime** under the Income Tax Act, 1961. With the new tax regime being the default for individuals, filing this form becomes necessary in specific cases, especially for those having **business or professional income**.

The form acts as a formal declaration to exercise the option of being taxed under the old regime, which allows deductions and exemptions.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

- Enables access to deductions like 80C, 80D, HRA
- Useful for taxpayers with significant investments and exemptions
- Allows flexibility in tax planning
- Ensures formal compliance with regime selection rules

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

- Restriction on frequent switching for taxpayers with business income
- Requires timely submission before filing ITR
- Incorrect selection may lead to higher tax liability
- Additional compliance step compared to salaried individuals without business income

## 4) Applicability Across India

Form 10-IEA applies uniformly across India:

- Applicable in all States and Union Territories
- No regional variation
- Relevant for individuals and HUFs, especially those with business or professional income under the Income Tax Act, 1961

## 5) Features

- Mandatory for opting out of default new tax regime (in applicable cases)
- Applicable primarily to business/professional income taxpayers
- Submitted online through income tax portal
- Linked with ITR filing process
- Once exercised, option may be restricted for future changes

## 6) Documents Required

- PAN and login credentials
- Income details
- Business or professional income records (if applicable)
- Previous year tax filing details

## 7) Process / Procedure

To opt for the old tax regime using Form 10-IEA:

1. **Log In** — Access the income tax e-filing portal.
2. **Navigate** — Go to e-File → Income Tax Forms → File Income Tax Forms.
3. **Select Form** — Choose Form 10-IEA.
4. **Choose AY** — Select the relevant assessment year.
5. **Enter Details** — Fill required information including nature of income.
6. **Submit** — File the form electronically.
7. **Proceed to File ITR** — Submit the return under the chosen regime.

The form must be filed before or along with the return, depending on the nature of income.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

- No fee for filing Form 10-IEA
- No direct cost involved
- Incorrect or delayed filing may result in loss of regime option

## Practical Interpretation

For salaried individuals without business income, switching between tax regimes is relatively flexible and may not require filing this form separately. However, for those with business or professional income, Form 10-IEA becomes essential to formally opt for the old regime.

> **Key Insight** — The decision should be based on comparative tax calculation, considering available deductions and overall income structure. Do a proper side-by-side comparison before filing.

## Conclusion

Form 10-IEA is a critical compliance requirement for taxpayers choosing the old tax regime in specific situations. Proper and timely submission ensures access to deductions and avoids complications in return filing.

Understanding when and how to file this form ensures accurate compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961 while optimizing tax outcomes.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Saving Capital Gains Tax \u2014 Practical Strategies That Actually Work
articles.excerpt: Actionable guide to reducing capital gains tax in India: tax loss harvesting, Section 54/54F/54EC, basic exemption adjustment, spreading gains across years, and combining multiple strategies.
articles.content:
## Overview

Capital gains tax is not inevitable in its full form. Indian tax law provides a surprisingly rich set of tools to legally reduce, defer, or eliminate it. The challenge is that most investors discover these options after the sale, when it is too late to use them optimally.

The strategies discussed in this guide apply to both short-term and long-term gains from different asset classes. Some require advance planning months before the sale, while others can be executed within the same financial year. The key principle throughout is that **tax planning is always more powerful when done before the transaction, not after**.

## Summary of Key Tax-Saving Mechanisms

| Strategy | Applicable Gain Type | Maximum Benefit | Time Sensitivity |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Section 54 (Buy Residential Property) | LTCG on Residential House | Full gain exempt | Must buy 1 yr before or 2 yrs after |
| Section 54F (Buy House from Any Asset) | LTCG on Any Asset (non-house) | Full gain if full proceeds reinvested | Same as above |
| Section 54EC (Capital Gains Bonds) | LTCG on Any Asset | Up to Rs. 50 lakh exempt | Must invest within 6 months |
| Annual Equity Exemption Limit | LTCG on Equity | Rs. 1.25 lakh per year | Each financial year |
| Tax Loss Harvesting | STCG and LTCG | Depends on losses booked | Same financial year or carry forward |
| Basic Exemption Limit | Any Capital Gain | Depends on total income | Annual |
| Joint Ownership | LTCG on Property | Splits tax between co-owners | At time of purchase |
| CGAS Parking | LTCG on Property | Preserves exemption claim | Before ITR filing |

## Deep Dive: Each Strategy

### 1. Tax Loss Harvesting

This is the most underused strategy for equity and mutual fund investors. The idea is simple: if you are sitting on unrealised losses in some stocks or funds, book them in the same year you realise a large capital gain. The loss offsets the gain and reduces the taxable amount.

**Example:** Kavita sold equity shares in November 2024 and made LTCG of Rs. 5,00,000. She also holds a fund that has fallen in value by Rs. 2,00,000 (unrealised). She redeems that fund in March 2025, booking a Long-Term Capital Loss of Rs. 2,00,000.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Gross LTCG | Rs. 5,00,000 |
| Less: Annual exemption | Rs. 1,25,000 |
| Less: LTCL booked | Rs. 2,00,000 |
| **Taxable LTCG** | **Rs. 1,75,000** |
| Tax at 12.5% | Rs. 21,875 |
| Tax without harvesting | Rs. 46,875 |
| **Saving** | **Rs. 25,000** |

She can then repurchase the same fund the next day. No wash sale rule applies in India.

### 2. Spreading Gains Across Financial Years

If you have flexibility on when to execute a sale, you can spread gains across two financial years to utilise the Rs. 1.25 lakh exemption twice.

> [!TIP] Split sales across years
> Instead of selling all equity shares in February 2025, sell half in February 2025 and half in April 2025. You claim Rs. 1.25 lakh exemption in each year — effectively doubling the tax-free amount.

### 3. Section 54EC Bonds

For property or land sales with large gains, capital gains bonds from NHAI, REC, or PFC are the most practical option when you do not want to buy another property. The bonds pay around 5% annual interest, are fully safe (sovereign-backed), and the gain invested is completely tax-exempt.

The cap is Rs. 50 lakh per financial year. If your gain exceeds Rs. 50 lakh, you can invest Rs. 50 lakh in the year of sale and another Rs. 50 lakh in the next financial year (if the 6-month window straddles two years). Some taxpayers have successfully used this to shield up to Rs. 1 crore in gains, though this is subject to specific conditions and legal interpretation.

### 4. Basic Exemption + Capital Gains

For individuals whose total income (excluding capital gains) is below the basic exemption limit of Rs. 2.5 lakh (Rs. 3 lakh for those aged 60 to 79, Rs. 5 lakh for those 80 and above), the shortfall can be adjusted against STCG from equity or LTCG.

**Example:** A retired individual has pension income of Rs. 1,80,000 and LTCG from equity of Rs. 2,00,000.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Shortfall in basic exemption (2,50,000 − 1,80,000) | Rs. 70,000 |
| LTCG | Rs. 2,00,000 |
| Less: Annual equity exemption | Rs. 1,25,000 |
| Less: Basic exemption adjustment | Rs. 70,000 |
| **Taxable LTCG** | **Rs. 5,000** |

### 5. Gifting to a Family Member in a Lower Bracket

Transferring assets to a family member in a lower tax bracket can reduce STCG liability. However, the **clubbing provisions** under Section 64 mean that income from assets gifted to a spouse or minor child can be clubbed with the donor's income. This strategy works better with adult children or parents who are in a lower bracket.

## Strategy Tips

1. **Always plan before the transaction, not after.** Most tax-saving opportunities must be set up before or within months of the sale. Calling a tax consultant after you have already registered the property sale limits your options dramatically.
2. **Combine strategies.** You can use Section 54 and Section 54EC together if only part of your gain is being reinvested in a house. There is no rule against combining these in the same year for the same transaction.
3. **Maintain a capital gains tracker.** Keep a year-by-year record of gains and losses across all asset classes. Unabsorbed capital losses can be carried forward for 8 years, and many investors lose this benefit simply because they do not track it.
4. **Do not ignore the new tax regime implications.** Under the new tax regime, deductions under Chapter VIA are not available, but capital gains exemptions under Sections 54, 54EC, and 54F remain available regardless of which tax regime you choose.
5. **For large property sales, get a registered valuer's opinion.** For properties sold at values that might attract scrutiny (below stamp duty value), having an independent valuer's report creates a defensible paper trail if the tax department raises queries.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. Can I simultaneously use Section 54 and Section 54F for the same sale?

No. Section 54 applies when the asset sold is a residential house, and Section 54F applies when the asset sold is anything other than a residential house. Both cannot apply to the same transaction since they cover mutually exclusive asset types.

### Q2. Is there any tax benefit available if I donate the capital gain amount to charity?

Donating gains to charity does not exempt the capital gains themselves. The capital gain arises on the sale and is taxable regardless of what you do with the proceeds afterwards. However, the donation separately qualifies for a deduction under Section 80G, which reduces your overall tax liability on other income.

### Q3. Can I use the basic exemption benefit against STCG on equity for which TDS has already been deducted?

The TDS deducted is only an advance tax. You file your ITR, calculate your actual liability considering the basic exemption, and receive a refund if TDS exceeds the actual tax due. So yes, the benefit is available — it is just claimed at the time of filing rather than upfront.

### Q4. My total LTCG from equity this year is Rs. 4 lakh. If I harvest losses of Rs. 2.75 lakh, will the remaining Rs. 1.25 lakh be fully exempt?

Yes. After adjusting the LTCL, your net LTCG is Rs. 1.25 lakh, which falls exactly within the exemption limit. Your tax liability on those equity gains would be zero. This is a common and effective strategy to combine loss harvesting with the annual exemption.

### Q5. Does reinvesting capital gains from property into a commercial property qualify for any exemption?

No standard exemption covers reinvestment into commercial property. Section 54 and 54F specifically require reinvestment in a residential house. Section 54EC works regardless of what you reinvest in, so that is your best option if you want to avoid buying residential property.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Liquid Funds Taxation \u2014 Everything You Need to Know
articles.excerpt: Clear guide to liquid fund taxation in India after the April 2023 rule change: slab-rate treatment, grandfathering for older units, post-tax comparisons with FDs, and arbitrage fund alternatives.
articles.content:
## Overview

Liquid funds are often described as the intelligent alternative to a savings account. They invest in very short-duration debt instruments (up to 91 days) and historically deliver returns modestly above FD rates with high liquidity. For individuals and businesses parking surplus funds, they have been a cornerstone of short-term treasury management.

The taxation rules for liquid funds changed fundamentally in **April 2023**. Before that date, liquid funds enjoyed a 20% LTCG rate with indexation after 3 years. Since April 1, 2023, all gains from liquid funds (regardless of how long you hold) are taxed at your applicable income tax slab rate. This single change made the tax treatment identical to a bank fixed deposit and removed the primary tax advantage that liquid funds previously held.

## Tax Treatment: Before and After April 2023

| Holding Period | Pre-April 2023 Treatment | Post-April 2023 Treatment |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Less than 3 years | Slab rate (STCG) | Slab rate |
| More than 3 years | 20% with indexation (LTCG) | Slab rate (same as STCG) |
| Dividend received | Slab rate | Slab rate |
| Units purchased before April 1, 2023 | Grandfathered at old rules | Old rules still apply to those units |

> [!IMPORTANT] Grandfathering matters
> Units of liquid funds purchased before April 1, 2023 are still governed by the old tax rules. Only units purchased on or after April 1, 2023 fall under the new slab-rate regime.

## Current Tax Rate Summary (Post-April 2023 Purchases)

| Investor Income Bracket | Effective Tax Rate on Liquid Fund Gains |
| --- | --- |
| Up to Rs. 3,00,000 (new regime) | Nil |
| Rs. 3,00,001 to Rs. 7,00,000 | 5% |
| Rs. 7,00,001 to Rs. 10,00,000 | 10% |
| Rs. 10,00,001 to Rs. 12,00,000 | 15% |
| Rs. 12,00,001 to Rs. 15,00,000 | 20% |
| Above Rs. 15,00,000 | 30% |

Add 4% health and education cess on all tax amounts. Add surcharge for income above Rs. 50 lakh.

## Calculation Example

**Scenario:** HNI investing in a liquid fund.

Vikram (income Rs. 40 lakh, 30% tax bracket) invests Rs. 20,00,000 in a liquid fund in June 2024. He redeems in October 2024 (4 months later) at Rs. 20,60,000.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Gain | Rs. 60,000 |
| Taxable at slab rate (30%) | Rs. 18,000 |
| Add Cess (4%) | Rs. 720 |
| **Total Tax** | **Rs. 18,720** |
| **Post-tax return** | **Rs. 41,280** |

### Comparison with other instruments (same amount, 4-month period)

| Instrument | Pre-Tax Return | Post-Tax Return (30% bracket) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Liquid Fund (current) | Rs. 60,000 (3.6% annualised) | Rs. 41,280 |
| Bank FD (7% pa) | Rs. 46,667 | Rs. 32,267 |
| Savings Account (4% pa) | Rs. 26,667 | Rs. 18,400 |

Liquid funds still outperform on returns but no longer offer a structural tax advantage.

## Exemptions

There are no specific exemptions available on liquid fund gains for post-April 2023 purchases. The gains are simply added to your total income and taxed accordingly. If your total income including liquid fund gains stays within the basic exemption limit, no tax is payable.

## Strategy Tips

1. **Liquid funds remain relevant for businesses in lower tax brackets.** For a partnership firm or private limited company, liquid funds offer returns competitive with FDs with better liquidity.
2. **Use overnight or ultra-short duration funds for extreme short-term parking.** For amounts needed within 7 to 15 days, overnight funds offer similar liquidity with marginally lower credit risk. Tax treatment is identical.
3. **Do not exit pre-April 2023 units prematurely.** If you still hold liquid fund units purchased before April 2023, those units retain the old tax treatment (LTCG with indexation after 3 years). Let them remain invested until needed.
4. **Consider arbitrage funds as a tax-efficient alternative.** Arbitrage funds are classified as equity funds for tax purposes. Gains held over 12 months attract only 12.5% LTCG tax with Rs. 1.25 lakh annual exemption. Short-term gains at 20% STCG are still better than slab-rate taxation for investors in the 30% bracket.
5. **Advance tax planning for large liquid fund redemptions.** If you redeem a large amount with significant gains, add these to your quarterly advance tax calculation to avoid interest penalties.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. I invested in a liquid fund in 2021. If I redeem today (2025), does the old LTCG rule apply since I have held it for more than 3 years?

Yes. Units purchased before April 1, 2023 are **grandfathered** under the old rules. Since you purchased in 2021 and are redeeming after more than 36 months, you qualify for LTCG at 20% with indexation under the pre-amendment rules. Only units you buy going forward attract slab-rate taxation.

### Q2. Are liquid fund returns guaranteed? If I lose money, can I set it off against other gains?

Liquid funds do not guarantee returns; they carry credit risk and minimal interest rate risk. If you exit at a loss (rare but possible in credit events), that capital loss can be offset against capital gains from other sources, subject to classification rules.

### Q3. How does TDS work on liquid fund redemptions?

Liquid funds and all mutual funds do **not deduct TDS on redemption proceeds** for resident individuals. You are responsible for self-assessment and advance tax payment. TDS applies only to NRI investors and on dividends above Rs. 5,000 per year from mutual funds.

### Q4. Is there a difference in taxation between the growth option and dividend option of a liquid fund?

Yes. In the growth option, tax arises only on redemption and is treated as capital gains. In the dividend (IDCW) option, each payout is treated as income from other sources and taxed at slab rate in the year of receipt. **Growth is generally more tax-efficient** for investors not needing regular cash flows.

### Q5. My company treasurer uses liquid funds for working capital parking. How is this treated for corporate taxation?

For companies, gains from liquid fund redemptions are added to business income and taxed at the applicable corporate tax rate (generally 22% for domestic companies under the concessional regime, plus surcharge and cess). The simplification post-April 2023 has less impact on corporates since they were never in a position to benefit from the indexation regime that applied to individuals.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 54EC \u2014 Capital Gains Bonds Explained
articles.excerpt: Complete guide to Section 54EC capital gains bonds: the 6-month window, Rs. 50 lakh cap, NHAI/REC/PFC options, 5-year lock-in, and how to combine 54EC with Sections 54 and 54F.
articles.content:
## Overview

Section 54EC is the Income Tax Act's most practical gift to property sellers. It allows you to invest your long-term capital gains in specified government-backed bonds and claim a complete exemption from capital gains tax on the amount invested, **up to Rs. 50 lakh per financial year**.

Unlike Section 54, which requires you to buy another property, Section 54EC requires only a straightforward bond investment. The trade-off is a 5-year lock-in period and a relatively modest interest rate. For most property sellers, this trade-off is entirely worth it.

> [!IMPORTANT] The 6-month window is strict
> You must make the investment within **6 months of the date of transfer (sale)** of the asset. Missing this window, even by a day, disqualifies the exemption entirely.

## Key Features of Section 54EC Bonds

| Feature | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Maximum Investment | Rs. 50 lakh per financial year |
| Lock-in Period | 5 years from date of investment |
| Eligible Bonds | NHAI, REC, PFC bonds (as currently notified) |
| Investment Window | Within 6 months of property sale |
| Interest Rate | Approximately 5% per annum |
| Taxability of Interest | Fully taxable as income from other sources |
| Asset Type Eligible | Long-term capital asset of any kind |
| Form of Investment | Demat or physical bonds |

## What Qualifies as a Long-Term Capital Asset for 54EC?

The exemption applies to the long-term capital gain from the sale of any long-term capital asset. This includes:

- Residential property (house, flat, villa)
- Commercial property (office, shop)
- Land and plots
- Unlisted shares held for more than 24 months
- Debt mutual funds held for more than 36 months (for pre-April 2023 investments)
- Gold and jewellery

> [!NOTE] Equity rarely uses 54EC
> LTCG from listed equity shares and equity mutual funds is not typically channelled through 54EC because the tax rate on equity is already lower, and the Rs. 1.25 lakh annual exemption usually provides adequate relief. The bond route is most relevant for property and other non-equity long-term assets.

## Calculation Example

**Scenario:** Sale of commercial property.

Suresh sold a commercial shop in September 2024 for Rs. 1,20,00,000. He had purchased it in 2010 for Rs. 18,00,000.

### LTCG Calculation (12.5% without indexation)

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Sale Price | Rs. 1,20,00,000 |
| Cost of Acquisition | Rs. 18,00,000 |
| Registration at purchase | Rs. 80,000 |
| Total acquisition cost | Rs. 18,80,000 |
| **LTCG** | **Rs. 1,01,20,000** |

### Tax with 54EC Investment

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Exempt Gain (bonds) | Rs. 50,00,000 |
| Taxable LTCG | Rs. 51,20,000 |
| Tax at 12.5% | Rs. 6,40,000 |
| Add Cess at 4% | Rs. 25,600 |
| **Tax Payable** | **Rs. 6,65,600** |

Without 54EC, his tax would have been approximately Rs. 13,14,200. **The bond investment saved him over Rs. 6.48 lakh in tax.**

## The Rs. 50 Lakh Per Year Argument

Some tax practitioners argue that if the 6-month window straddles two financial years (for example, property sold in October 2024 means the window runs until April 2025), the taxpayer can invest Rs. 50 lakh in the current financial year and another Rs. 50 lakh in the next financial year, effectively shielding Rs. 1 crore in gains.

This is a **contested position**. The Income Tax Act says the limit is Rs. 50 lakh during the previous year in which the asset is sold. Several tribunal rulings have allowed the two-year interpretation, but the matter is not entirely settled. Taxpayers relying on this should have strong legal backing.

## Exemption Conditions

| # | Condition |
| --- | --- |
| 1 | **6-Month Investment Window** — Investment must be made within 6 months of the date of sale. The registered sale deed date is generally considered the date of transfer. |
| 2 | **Hold for 5 Years** — If you sell, pledge, or convert the bonds before 5 years, the exempted gain is taxed as LTCG in the year of breach. |
| 3 | **Only Specified Bonds Qualify** — NHAI, REC, and PFC bonds are currently notified. Verify the list before investing. |
| 4 | **Investment from Sale Proceeds** — Bonds must be purchased from the capital gains amount. Making the investment from the sale proceeds account is the cleaner approach. |

## Strategy Tips

1. **Invest immediately, not at the deadline.** Waiting until the 5th or 6th month creates unnecessary risk. Bond availability can be limited at certain points. Invest as soon as the sale proceeds are received.
2. **Combine with Section 54 or 54F.** If you are reinvesting part of the proceeds in a house and part in bonds, you can claim both Section 54 (or 54F) and Section 54EC in the same year for the same sale. The total of both exemptions should not exceed the total gain.
3. **Account for the interest income.** The 5% interest you earn on the bonds is taxable each year. Include it in your advance tax calculations so you do not accumulate an interest liability under Sections 234B and 234C.
4. **Use CGAS as a bridge for Section 54 / 54F.** If you are planning to use 54EC for part of the gain and Section 54 for the remainder, but have not found a property yet, park the Section 54 portion in a CGAS account while the bond investment for 54EC goes through.
5. **Keep investment proofs meticulously.** In case of a future scrutiny assessment, having the bond certificate, ISIN, and investment date clearly documented makes the exemption claim watertight.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. Can I invest in 54EC bonds if I sell unlisted shares and make LTCG?

Yes. Section 54EC applies to any long-term capital asset, which includes unlisted shares held for more than 24 months. It is not restricted to immovable property. The 6-month investment rule and the Rs. 50 lakh cap remain the same.

### Q2. The property was jointly owned. Can each co-owner invest Rs. 50 lakh separately in 54EC bonds?

Yes. Each co-owner's LTCG is computed separately based on their ownership share. Each can independently invest their portion of the gains in 54EC bonds and claim the exemption up to **Rs. 50 lakh each**. This effectively doubles the maximum shield for jointly-owned property.

### Q3. What interest rate do NHAI and REC 54EC bonds currently offer, and is it competitive?

Current rates are approximately **5% per annum** (verify before investing, as rates are set at time of issue). This is not particularly competitive compared to FD or debt mutual fund returns, but the primary purpose is tax saving, not yield. The effective pre-tax equivalent yield, when accounting for the tax saved, is significantly higher.

### Q4. If I am unable to invest within 6 months due to a medical emergency, is there any relief?

No automatic extension is available under the Act. Some courts have granted relief in genuinely exceptional circumstances, but this is discretionary and not a reliable safety net. If there is any possibility of a delay, invest in whatever bonds are available before the window closes and sort out the specifics afterwards.

### Q5. The property I sold had a construction loan. Can I deduct the loan repayment from the sale proceeds before computing LTCG?

No. The loan repayment is not deductible from capital gains. LTCG is calculated based on sale price minus original cost of acquisition and improvement costs, not based on what you receive net of loan repayment. The cost of construction (which the loan financed) would have already been included in your cost of improvement.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: US Stocks Capital Gains \u2014 Tax Rules for Indian Investors
articles.excerpt: Comprehensive guide for Indian investors on US stock taxation: 24-month LTCG threshold, DTAA and Form 67, W-8BEN, Schedule FA reporting, LRS limits, and calculation examples.
articles.content:
## Overview

Indian investors have taken to US stocks enthusiastically over the past few years, driven by platforms like INDmoney, Vested, and Groww that make international investing accessible. What many do not fully appreciate until tax season is that US stock investments come with a **dual tax obligation** — one in the United States and one in India. Getting this wrong can mean paying taxes twice on the same gain or missing foreign tax credits that could have reduced your liability.

The good news is that the **India–US Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement (DTAA)** prevents genuine double taxation. The challenge is navigating both regimes correctly and filing in the right sequence.

This guide focuses on the Indian tax implications for resident Indian investors holding US stocks. NRI taxation is a separate topic with different rules.

## Tax Rates: Indian Tax Treatment of US Stock Gains

| Scenario | Holding Period | Classification in India | Indian Tax Rate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| US Stocks (NYSE / NASDAQ) | 24 months or less | STCG | Slab rate (up to 30%) |
| US Stocks (NYSE / NASDAQ) | More than 24 months | LTCG | 12.5% (without indexation) |
| US ETFs / Funds | Same as above | Same as above | Same as above |
| Dividends from US Stocks | N/A | Income from Other Sources | Slab rate |
| Dividend Withholding by US | N/A | Withholding (flat) | 25% (or 15% under DTAA) |

> [!IMPORTANT] US stocks use a 24-month threshold
> The classification as STCG or LTCG for US stocks uses a 24-month threshold, **not** the 12-month threshold that applies to Indian listed equity. US stocks are treated as unlisted securities for Indian tax purposes because they are not listed on a recognised Indian stock exchange.

## US Withholding Tax on Dividends

When a US company pays dividends to a foreign (Indian) shareholder, the US withholds tax at source. The standard rate is 30%, but under the India–US DTAA, this is reduced to **15%** if your broker has submitted the necessary W-8BEN form on your behalf. Most platforms do this automatically.

### What you receive

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Declared dividend | $100 |
| US withholding at 15% | $15 |
| **Net received in your account** | **$85** |

You then declare the **full $100** as income in India and claim a Foreign Tax Credit of $15 against your Indian tax liability. You must use **Form 67** to claim this credit before filing your ITR.

## Full Calculation Example

**Scenario:** Sale of Apple shares by a resident Indian.

Ananya invested Rs. 2,00,000 in Apple shares in January 2022 (when USD/INR was 74). She sold in March 2025 (when USD/INR was 84). Sale value: Rs. 3,80,000.

Holding period: Over 24 months, so LTCG applies.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Sale value (INR) | Rs. 3,80,000 |
| Cost of acquisition (INR) | Rs. 2,00,000 |
| **LTCG** | **Rs. 1,80,000** |
| Tax at 12.5% | Rs. 22,500 |
| Add Cess at 4% | Rs. 900 |
| **Tax Payable** | **Rs. 23,400** |

> [!NOTE] No Rs. 1.25 lakh exemption on US stocks
> The cost of acquisition is the rupee equivalent at the time of purchase. The gain in rupees automatically accounts for currency movement; there is no separate forex gain calculation. The Rs. 1.25 lakh annual exemption applies only to Indian listed equity, so the full gain is taxable here.

## Exemptions and Credits

### Foreign Tax Credit (FTC)

If US taxes have been deducted (typically on dividends), you can claim credit for those taxes in India using **Form 67**. The credit is the lower of the foreign tax paid or the Indian tax on that income.

### No LTCG Exemption for US Stocks

The Rs. 1.25 lakh annual exemption is specific to equity and equity-oriented mutual funds listed on recognised Indian exchanges. It does not extend to US stocks.

### Set-Off of Losses

Losses on US stocks can be set off against gains from other capital assets subject to the usual rules (STCL against any gain, LTCL only against LTCG).

## Reporting Requirements

### Schedule FA (Foreign Assets)

Every resident Indian who holds US stocks at any point during the financial year must declare them in Schedule FA of the ITR. This includes the purchase cost, fair market value as at December 31 (the US tax year end), and any income received. **The penalty for non-disclosure is Rs. 10 lakh per year** under the Black Money Act.

### Form 67

Must be filed before submitting the ITR if you are claiming Foreign Tax Credit. Many investors miss this deadline, which means they pay tax on dividend income both in the US (withheld) and in India (at slab rate) without any offset.

### FEMA Compliance

Investments in US stocks must be within the **Liberalised Remittance Scheme (LRS) limit of USD 2,50,000 per year** per resident individual. Brokers track this, but you are responsible for ensuring you stay within the limit.

## Strategy Tips

1. **Track your investment dates and cost in INR precisely.** Exchange rates on the purchase date matter for cost of acquisition. Keep a record of both USD amount and INR equivalent.
2. **File Form 67 before your ITR.** Form 67 must be filed on the income tax portal before you submit your ITR. Missing this means losing the foreign tax credit for that year. Set a reminder every March.
3. **Hold for 24 months to qualify for LTCG.** Short-term trading in US stocks is taxed at your full slab rate, which can reach 30% plus cess for high earners.
4. **Report fractional shares.** Many US platforms offer fractional share investing. Fractional shares still count as holdings and any sale is a taxable event. Track these meticulously.
5. **Consider rupee cost and dollar cost separately.** Rising INR–USD exchange rates amplify your returns in rupee terms. Falling rates can turn a USD profit into a rupee loss. Build currency assumptions into your return expectations.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. I made a loss selling US stocks. Can I set it off against STCG from my Indian equity trades?

If the US stock was held for 24 months or less, the loss is a Short-Term Capital Loss. STCL can be set off against both STCG and LTCG from any source, including Indian equity. If held for more than 24 months, it is an LTCL, which can only be offset against LTCG.

### Q2. My US broker automatically reinvests dividends (DRIP). Is that a taxable event in India?

Yes. When dividends are reinvested, the dividend amount is still considered income in the year it was declared and reinvested. You must report it as income from other sources and claim FTC for any US withholding. The reinvested amount then becomes your cost of acquisition for the new fractional shares.

### Q3. Do I need to pay advance tax on US stock gains during the year?

Yes. If your estimated tax liability from all sources (including US stock gains) exceeds Rs. 10,000 for the year, you must pay advance tax in quarterly installments. Missing this attracts interest under Sections 234B and 234C. If you sell US stocks mid-year with a large gain, calculate the advance tax liability immediately.

### Q4. My US stock portfolio is valued at USD 5,000. Does it still need to be reported in Schedule FA?

Yes. The reporting obligation under Schedule FA has **no minimum threshold**. Any foreign asset held by a resident Indian must be disclosed, regardless of value. Non-disclosure carries a minimum penalty of Rs. 10 lakh.

### Q5. Are US index ETFs listed in India treated differently from directly held US stocks?

Yes, significantly. Indian-listed ETFs that invest in US markets (like Mirae Asset NYSE FANG+ ETF) are treated as non-equity funds for Indian tax purposes. Gains from these are taxed at slab rates regardless of holding period, similar to debt funds. Directly held US stocks follow the 24-month LTCG rule as described above.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How to Save Capital Gains Tax on Sale of Land
articles.excerpt: Land held over 24 months qualifies for LTCG at 20% with indexation. Save tax through Section 54F (reinvest in house), Section 54EC (bonds), or CGAS (temporary parking).
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Capital gains arising from the sale of **land** are taxable under the **Income Tax Act, 1961**. The tax treatment depends on whether the land qualifies as a capital asset and the holding period.

If the land is held for **more than 24 months**, the gain is treated as **long-term capital gain (LTCG)** and taxed at concessional rates with indexation. The law also provides specific avenues to save or defer tax through reinvestment.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

- Availability of indexation benefit for long-term gains
- Multiple exemption options through reinvestment
- Ability to defer or eliminate tax liability
- Structured provisions for property and bond investments

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

- Strict timelines for reinvestment
- Lock-in period for certain investments
- Conditions must be fully satisfied to claim exemption
- Short-term gains taxed at higher slab rates
- Documentation requirements can be extensive

## 4) Applicability Across India

Capital gains taxation on sale of land applies uniformly across India:

- Applicable in all States and Union Territories
- No regional variation
- Governed centrally under the Income Tax Act, 1961

## 5) Features

- Classification based on **24-month** holding period rule
- LTCG taxed at **20%** with indexation
- Availability of exemptions under Sections 54F and 54EC
- Taxable in the year of transfer
- Applicable to both rural and urban land (subject to definition of capital asset)

## 6) Documents Required

- Purchase and sale deeds of land
- Cost of acquisition and improvement records
- Stamp duty valuation details
- Capital gains calculation
- Proof of reinvestment (property or bonds)
- Bank transaction records

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Verify Asset Qualification** — Check if land qualifies as a capital asset.
2. **Determine Holding Period** — Classify as short-term or long-term.
3. **Compute Gain** — Use sale value and indexed cost.
4. **Select Exemption Option** — Section 54F, 54EC, or CGAS.
5. **Reinvest Timely** — Complete investment within statutory window.
6. **Maintain Proof** — Retain documentation of investment.
7. **Report in ITR** — Disclose in the capital gains schedule.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

- **Short-Term Capital Gains (STCG)** — Taxed at slab rates
- **Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG)** — Taxed at **20%** with indexation
- Surcharge and cess applicable
- No fee for claiming exemptions

## Key Ways to Save Tax

### Section 54F

- Invest full sale proceeds in a residential property
- Applicable when selling land or other non-residential assets

### Section 54EC

- Invest capital gains in specified bonds within **6 months**
- Maximum limit of **₹50 lakh**

### Capital Gains Account Scheme (CGAS)

- Deposit unutilised gains temporarily to retain exemption eligibility
- Useful when reinvestment cannot be completed before ITR due date

> **Key Insight** — Section 54F requires reinvesting the **entire sale consideration**, not just the gain. If you only reinvest the gain, exemption is allowed only proportionately.

## Practical Interpretation

Tax saving on land sale depends heavily on **timely reinvestment decisions**. Since the exemption window is limited, planning must begin before or immediately after the transaction.

The choice between property investment and bonds depends on liquidity needs, risk preference, and long-term goals.

## Conclusion

Saving capital gains tax on the sale of land requires a clear understanding of holding period, available exemptions, and compliance timelines. Proper execution of reinvestment strategies can significantly reduce tax liability.

A structured approach ensures compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961 while optimizing post-sale financial outcomes.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: TDS Compliance for Enterprises in India: Checklist, Forms and Penalties
articles.excerpt: Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) is a core compliance mechanism under the Income Tax Act, 1961, designed to collect tax at the very origin of income. For enterprises in India, including startups, SMEs, and large corporations, TDS compliance…
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) is a core compliance mechanism under the Income Tax Act, 1961, designed to collect tax at the very origin of income. For enterprises in India, including startups, SMEs, and large corporations, TDS compliance is a statutory obligation linked to financial transactions such as salaries, contractor payments, rent, professional fees, interest, and more. Under this system, the payer or enterprise is responsible for deducting a specified percentage of tax before making a payment to the payee and depositing it with the government. This ensures a steady inflow of tax revenue and reduces tax evasion. TDS compliance involves multiple layers:

- Identifying applicable sections such as 192 for salary, 194C for contractors, 194J for professional services
- Deducting correct TDS rates
- Timely deposit of tax
- Filing periodic returns
- Issuing TDS certificates

Failure at any stage can attract penalties, interest, and in serious cases, prosecution.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Ensures Steady Revenue Collection

TDS enables the government to collect taxes throughout the year instead of waiting for annual filings. This supports fiscal stability.

### Reduces Tax Evasion

Since tax is deducted at the source, it minimizes the chances of underreporting income by recipients.

### Simplifies Tax Burden for Payees

For employees and vendors, TDS acts as advance tax and reduces year-end tax liability.

### Builds Financial Discipline

Regular compliance promotes structured accounting practices and better financial governance.

### Enhances Credibility

Companies that follow TDS norms are viewed as reliable by investors, auditors, and regulatory bodies.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Compliance Burden

Managing multiple sections, rates, deadlines, and filings can be complex, especially for small businesses.

### Frequent Changes in Law

TDS provisions are updated regularly in Union Budgets, requiring constant monitoring.

### Cash Flow Impact

Enterprises must deduct and deposit TDS even before settling full internal liabilities.

### Risk of Penalties

Errors such as incorrect rates, late filing, or wrong PAN details can lead to penalties.

### Administrative Costs

Many businesses require accountants or consultants to manage compliance effectively.

## 4) Applicability Across India (State-wise Context)

TDS is governed centrally by the Income Tax Department, so its applicability is uniform across India. However, its practical intensity varies depending on business activity in different states. Highly Industrial States

- Maharashtra including Mumbai and Pune with high corporate density
- Karnataka including Bangalore with strong IT sector presence
- Tamil Nadu including Chennai with manufacturing and services Commercial Hubs
- Delhi NCR including Delhi, Noida, and Gurgaon with consulting and startup ecosystems
- Gujarat including Ahmedabad and Surat with strong trading activity Emerging Startup Ecosystems
- Telangana including Hyderabad
- Kerala including Kochi and Trivandrum Other States

States such as Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Odisha, and Assam also require strict TDS compliance across:

- Government contractors
- Small and medium enterprises
- Educational institutions
- NGOs where applicable

TDS compliance is mandatory across India irrespective of state.

## 5) Features of TDS Compliance

### Threshold Limits

TDS applies only when payments exceed specified limits, such as ₹30,000 for professional fees under Section 194J.

### TAN Requirement

Every deductor must obtain a Tax Deduction and Collection Account Number.

### Quarterly Returns

### Returns must be filed using

- Form 24Q for salary
- Form 26Q for non-salary domestic payments
- Form 27Q for payments to non-residents

### TDS Certificates

- Form 16 for salary
- Form 16A for non-salary payments

### Online Compliance

All filings and payments are completed through the Central Board of Direct Taxes portal.

### PAN Linking

Incorrect or missing PAN leads to higher TDS, typically at 20 percent.

## 6) Documents List

For effective TDS compliance, enterprises should maintain the following: Basic Registration Documents

- PAN of the company
- TAN allotment letter
- GST registration if applicable Transaction Documents
- Salary and payroll records
- Vendor invoices
- Contract agreements
- Rent agreements
- Professional service agreements Compliance Documents
- Challans using Form 281
- TDS returns such as 24Q, 26Q, 27Q
- TDS certificates such as Form 16 and Form 16A Supporting Documents
- PAN details of employees and vendors
- Bank statements
- Ledger accounts

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Identify Applicable Section** — Determine the relevant TDS section based on the nature of payment.
2. **Deduct TDS** — Deduct tax at prescribed rates at the time of payment or credit, whichever is earlier.
3. **Deposit TDS** — Deposit the deducted amount using Challan ITNS 281:

- By the 7th of the following month
- For March, by 30th April

1. **File TDS Returns** — Quarterly deadlines:

- Q1: 31 July
- Q2: 31 October
- Q3: 31 January
- Q4: 31 May

1. **Issue TDS Certificates**

- Form 16 annually for employees
- Form 16A quarterly for others

1. **Reconciliation** — Match TDS data with Form 26AS and accounting records.
2. **Correction if Required** — Revise returns to fix errors such as incorrect PAN or mismatched entries.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### TAN Application Fees

Approximately ₹65 including GST.

### TDS Return Filing Fees

Free on government portals. Third-party software may charge between ₹500 and ₹5,000.

### Late Filing Fees under Section 234E

₹200 per day, capped at the total TDS amount.

### Interest on Late Payment

- 1 percent per month for delay in deduction
- 1.5 percent per month for delay in deposit

### Penalties

₹10,000 to ₹1,00,000 under Section 271H for incorrect or delayed filing.

### Prosecution in Severe Cases

Imprisonment ranging from 3 months to 7 years may apply in cases of willful default.

## Conclusion

TDS compliance is a critical responsibility for enterprises in India. It ensures legal adherence, supports government revenue, and reflects financial discipline. While the process involves multiple steps and strict timelines, maintaining proper systems and documentation can make compliance smooth and efficient. For businesses across sectors, especially startups and service-driven enterprises, strong TDS compliance practices help avoid penalties and build long-term credibility.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Updated List of GST Tax Slabs & Item-Wise Rates
articles.excerpt: The Goods and Services Tax (GST) is India’s unified indirect tax system introduced on 1 July 2017, replacing multiple taxes such as VAT, service tax, excise duty, and others. Goods and Services Tax is a destination-based, multi-stage tax…
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) is India’s unified indirect tax system introduced on 1 July 2017, replacing multiple taxes such as VAT, service tax, excise duty, and others. Goods and Services Tax is a destination-based, multi-stage tax levied at every point of value addition, with the final burden borne by the end consumer. As part of recent reforms often referred to as GST 2.0, the GST Council simplified the earlier multi-tier structure. The previous slabs of 0%, 5%, 12%, 18%, 28%, and 40% have now been rationalised into a simpler structure mainly comprising 0%, 5%, 18%, and 40%, with some special rates like 3% and 0.25% still applicable for specific goods. The key objective of this restructuring is:

- Simplification of tax compliance
- Reduction in classification disputes
- Lower tax burden on essential goods
- Higher taxation on luxury and harmful products

The new GST structure ensures that most goods fall under two primary slabs: 5% and 18%, making the system easier for businesses and consumers alike.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Simplified Tax Structure

The removal of 12% and 28% slabs reduces confusion and makes classification easier for businesses.

### Lower Cost of Essentials

Many daily-use goods such as packaged foods, medicines, and household items have shifted to lower slabs, reducing prices.

### Improved Compliance

Fewer slabs mean fewer errors in invoicing, HSN classification, and return filing.

### Boost to Consumption

Lower GST on common goods increases purchasing power and demand.

### Transparency and Uniformity

GST ensures the same tax rates across India, eliminating state-level tax differences.
