Milk has been retained as a fully exempt item due to its nutritional importance and its role as a primary protein and calcium source for all income groups. Paneer was reclassified along with other pre-packaged dairy products in the 2022 amendments to broaden the GST tax base for organised dairy.

### Q2. Is buttermilk (chhaas) taxable under GST?

Buttermilk sold loose or without pre-packaging is exempt from GST. Pre-packaged branded buttermilk (like tetra-packed chhaas sold under a brand) attracts **5% GST** under HSN 0403.

### Q3. Is there GST on milk sold by vending machines?

Milk dispensed through vending machines is generally treated as unpackaged milk if the consumer brings their own container and would be exempt. If pre-filled single-serve containers are dispensed, the packaging and labelling may trigger the 5% GST rule.

### Q4. How does a small paneer maker selling at a weekly market comply with GST?

If the annual aggregate turnover is below Rs. 20 lakh (Rs. 10 lakh in special category states), registration is not required. If they sell loose paneer without pre-packaging, it remains exempt regardless of turnover.

### Q5. Is GST applicable on organic ghee or A2 ghee sold by small farmers?

Ghee (including organic and A2 varieties) attracts **12% GST** regardless of the production method or marketing claims. Small farmers below the turnover threshold need not register. Registered businesses selling any ghee must charge 12% GST, irrespective of organic or A2 certification.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: GST on Medicines and Pharmaceuticals: Rates, HSN Codes & Compliance
articles.excerpt: Complete guide to GST on medicines and pharmaceuticals in India: rate table, HSN Chapter 30 breakdown, supply chain flow, NPPA pricing interaction, and compliance guidance for chemists and manufacturers.
articles.content:
## Introduction

India's pharmaceutical industry is the world's third-largest by volume and contributes substantially to global generic drug supply. With a sector of this scale and social significance, the GST treatment of medicines and pharmaceuticals directly affects millions of patients, thousands of manufacturers, lakhs of chemists, and complex hospital procurement chains.

The government has structured pharmaceutical GST to balance revenue generation with public health priorities. Most medicines attract either 5% or 12% GST, with a narrow category at 18%, and several critical drugs remaining exempt.

## GST Rate Table: Medicines and Pharmaceuticals

| Product Category | GST Rate | HSN Code | Examples |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Human blood and blood components | Nil | 3002 | Whole blood, plasma, platelets |
| Contraceptives (oral pills) | Nil | 3006 | Birth control pills |
| Vaccines | 5% | 3002 | Polio, MMR, COVID vaccines |
| Life-saving drugs | 5% | 3004 | Insulin, dialysis solutions |
| Medicines for rare diseases | 5% | 3004 | Orphan drugs |
| Anti-cancer drugs | 5% | 3004 | Chemotherapy agents |
| Ayurvedic medicines (branded) | 12% | 3004 | Patanjali, Dabur range |
| Homeopathic preparations | 12% | 3004 | All homeopathic medicines |
| Unani and Siddha medicines | 12% | 3004 | Traditional medicine systems |
| General prescription medicines | 12% | 3004 | Antibiotics, anti-hypertensives |
| OTC medicines (pain relievers etc.) | 12% | 3004 | Paracetamol, antacids |
| Medicated soaps | 18% | 3401 | Antiseptic specialty soaps |
| Cosmeceuticals (vitamin C serums etc.) | 18% | 3304 | If classified as cosmetics |
| Diagnostic kits (IVD reagents) | 12% | 3822 | ELISA kits, PCR reagents |
| Surgical sutures, absorbable | 12% | 3006 | Catgut, synthetic sutures |
| Dental cements and fillings | 12% | 3006 | Dental materials |

## HSN Chapter 30 Breakdown

| HSN Code | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 3001 | Glands and other organs for organo-therapeutic uses; heparin and its salts |
| 3002 | Human blood; animal blood for therapeutic use; vaccines, toxins |
| 3003 | Medicaments (not in measured doses or retail packs) |
| 3004 | Medicaments in measured doses for retail sale (the main pharmaceutical code) |
| 3005 | Wadding, gauze, bandages, and surgical dressings |
| 3006 | Pharmaceutical goods (surgical sutures, contraceptives, first-aid boxes) |

## The Supply Chain: Manufacturer to Patient

The pharmaceutical supply chain involves multiple GST transactions:

| Stage | GST Flow |
| --- | --- |
| Manufacturer → C&F Agent | GST collected at applicable rate (5% or 12%) on actual sale price |
| C&F Agent → Stockist / Distributor | GST continues to flow on actual transaction value |
| Stockist → Retail Chemist | GST charged at applicable rate |
| Retail Chemist → Patient | No additional GST above MRP; taxes embedded in supply chain |

The entire chain allows ITC credits to flow until the final consumer purchase.

## NPPA Pricing and GST Interaction

- NPPA ceiling prices are generally set **exclusive of GST**. Manufacturers may charge GST on top of the NPPA ceiling price.
- MRP printed on medicine packs must be **inclusive of GST**. Any excess charge is an offence.
- Price revisions under DPCO are periodically announced. GST rate changes trigger corresponding MRP changes.

> [!NOTE] MRP includes GST — always
> The Maximum Retail Price printed on medicine packaging must always be inclusive of all applicable taxes. Charging above MRP is a legal offence under the Legal Metrology Act, regardless of GST rate changes.

## Real-World Examples

### Example A: Insulin Manufacturer

A pharmaceutical company manufacturing insulin vials invoices a hospital supply chain at Rs. 100 per vial (10ml). GST at 5% = Rs. 5. Total invoice value = Rs. 105. The hospital, if GST-registered, can claim ITC of Rs. 5. If insulin is dispensed in-house as part of an exempt medical procedure, ITC is blocked and Rs. 5 becomes an embedded cost.

### Example B: Antibiotic Sales Through Chemist

A chemist purchases amoxicillin strips at Rs. 60 (inclusive of 12% GST) from a distributor. The MRP is Rs. 80. The chemist's margin is Rs. 20 before distribution costs. The chemist collects **no additional GST** from the patient since MRP is inclusive of all taxes.

### Example C: Ayurvedic OTC Brand

A branded Chyawanprash sold at Rs. 400 (MRP) includes 12% GST. Back calculation: GST component = Rs. 400 × 12/112 = **Rs. 42.86**. The manufacturer remits this GST through the supply chain.

## Industry Impact

- **Generic drugs:** The 12% GST on most medicines has been a concern for domestic generic manufacturers. The cost of compliance, especially for small-scale manufacturers, adds to the overall burden.
- **Export orientation:** India exports pharmaceutical products worth over USD 25 billion annually. Exports are zero-rated, and manufacturers claim ITC refunds on input taxes.
- **Cold chain requirements:** Biological medicines require temperature-controlled logistics which attract 18% GST, adding to the cost of biologics.
- **Jan Aushadhi stores:** PMBJP stores pay GST on purchases like any other chemist but pass on the low purchase price benefit to consumers.

## Key Insights

- The 5% rate on vaccines and life-saving drugs reflects the government's public health priorities
- Pharmaceutical companies must carefully classify their products under Chapter 30 to avoid classification disputes
- Transfer pricing and GST valuation rules both apply to pharmaceutical group company transactions
- E-pharmacies must ensure GST compliance including e-way bill requirements for medicines shipped across state lines

## FAQs

### Q1. Is there GST on medicines prescribed during a hospital visit?

If medicines are dispensed by the hospital as part of the treatment and included in the composite hospital bill, they may be part of the exempt healthcare service. If separately billed or purchased from an in-house pharmacy, standard pharmaceutical GST rates apply.

### Q2. Why do some medicines cost more after GST than before?

Pre-GST, many medicines were exempt from central excise but subject to state VAT at varying rates (0% to 6%). In states where VAT was low, the shift to 12% GST has increased the effective rate. However, manufacturers now benefit from ITC on inputs, which partially offsets this.

### Q3. Is there GST on nutritional supplements and protein powders?

Nutritional supplements and protein powders classified as food supplements (not medicines) attract **18% GST**. Products classified as medicines under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act and registered with CDSCO attract **12% GST** under Chapter 30.

### Q4. Can a patient get a refund of GST paid on medicines?

Individual patients cannot claim GST refunds directly — they are the end consumers who bear the tax. However, if medicines are purchased for business purposes by a registered company, the company may claim ITC subject to the nature of the supply.

### Q5. How does GST apply to free medicine samples given to doctors?

Free samples given to doctors are not a supply for consideration and do not attract GST on the transaction itself. However, the pharmaceutical company must **reverse ITC** on the inputs used to produce these samples. The cost is treated as a sales promotion expense with the embedded ITC reversal.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Rent Agreement for GST Registration in India: Rules, Format & Key Requirements (2026 Guide)
articles.excerpt: A rent agreement is one of the most important documents required for GST registration when a business operates from a rented property. Under the GST framework, every registered taxpayer must provide proof of their principal place of…
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

A rent agreement is one of the most important documents required for GST registration when a business operates from a rented property. Under the GST framework, every registered taxpayer must provide proof of their principal place of business, and a valid rent agreement serves as key evidence. As per provisions under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, businesses must declare and verify their place of operation during GST registration. If the premises are rented, the applicant must submit:

- A rent or lease agreement
- Supporting documents such as utility bills The rent agreement confirms:
- Legal occupancy of the premises
- Address authenticity
- Consent of the property owner It applies to:
- Startups
- Freelancers
- Small businesses
- Companies operating from rented offices or shops

Without a valid rent agreement or supporting proof, GST registration may be rejected or delayed.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Proof of Business Address

Ensures that the business location is legitimate and verifiable.

### Mandatory Compliance

Fulfills GST registration requirements.

### Easy Verification

Helps authorities validate the place of business quickly.

### Flexibility for Businesses

Allows businesses to operate from rented premises.

### Supports Legal Documentation

Acts as legal evidence in disputes or audits.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Documentation Errors

Incorrect or incomplete agreements may lead to rejection.

### Stamp Duty Requirement

Rent agreements may need to be stamped or notarized.

### Owner Consent Issues

Landlord may refuse to provide necessary documents.

### Renewal Requirements

Expired agreements may require updating in GST records.

### Additional Compliance

Businesses must maintain updated address proof.

## 4) Applicability Across India (State-wise Context)

Rent agreement requirements for GST registration apply uniformly across India. Major Business States

- Maharashtra, Karnataka, Delhi NCR

High number of startups and rented offices Commercial States

- Gujarat, Tamil Nadu Industrial and trading businesses Emerging Markets
- Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh Growing MSME sector

👉 While GST rules are uniform, stamp duty and rent agreement formats may vary by state laws.

## 5) Features of Rent Agreement for GST Registration

### Legal Document

Must be a valid agreement between tenant and landlord.

### Address Proof

Clearly mentions the property address.

### Duration

Specifies lease period.

### Signature Requirement

Signed by both parties.

### Supporting Documents

Usually accompanied by utility bill and owner’s ID proof.

## 6) Documents List

Mandatory Documents

- Rent agreement or lease agreement
- Utility bill (electricity, water, etc.)
- Owner’s PAN or Aadhaar Additional Documents
- No Objection Certificate (NOC) from owner
- Property ownership proof (in some cases) Business Documents
- PAN Card of applicant
- Business registration documents

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Draft Rent Agreement** — Include:

- Tenant and landlord details
- Property address
- Rent amount
- Duration

1. **Execute Agreement** — Sign by both parties and get it stamped if required.
2. **Collect Supporting Documents** — Obtain utility bill and owner’s ID proof.
3. **Apply for GST Registration** — Submit application on GST portal.
4. **Upload Documents** — Upload rent agreement and supporting documents.
5. **Verification by Authorities** — GST officer verifies details.
6. **Approval** — GST registration is granted if documents are valid. Sample Format (Key Elements) A rent agreement should include:

- Names of landlord and tenant
- Property address
- Rent amount and payment terms
- Lease duration
- Security deposit details
- Signatures of both parties

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### GST Registration

Free

### Stamp Duty

Varies by state

### Notary Charges

Optional but may apply

### Late Fees

Not applicable for registration but applies for GST returns

## Conclusion

A rent agreement is a crucial document for GST registration when operating from a rented premises. It serves as proof of business location and ensures compliance with GST requirements. For businesses, ensuring that the agreement is properly drafted, signed, and supported with required documents is essential to avoid delays or rejection. As GST compliance becomes increasingly digitized, maintaining accurate and updated documentation is key to smooth operations. Sale and Purchase of Securities in India GST Exemption on Sale and Purchase of Securities in India: Rules, Scope & Compliance (2026 Guide)

## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

The sale and purchase of securities in India occupy a unique position under the GST framework. Unlike goods and services, securities are specifically excluded from the scope of GST, meaning that transactions involving securities are not taxable under GST. As per the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, securities are neither treated as “goods” nor “services.” Therefore, buying and selling of securities such as shares, bonds, debentures, and derivatives do not attract GST. The definition of securities is derived from the Securities Contracts (Regulation) Act, 1956, which includes:

- Shares and stocks
- Bonds and debentures
- Derivatives
- Mutual fund units

However, it is important to note that while the transaction in securities is exempt, certain related services such as brokerage, transaction charges, and advisory services are taxable under GST. This distinction is crucial for investors, brokers, financial institutions, and businesses involved in financial markets.

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### No GST on Investment Transactions

Investors are not burdened with GST on buying or selling securities.

### Encourages Capital Market Participation

Lower transaction costs promote investment activity.

### Simplified Tax Treatment

No need to calculate GST on securities transactions.

### Alignment with Financial Systems

Maintains consistency with global practices.

### Boost to Financial Markets

Supports growth of stock markets and investment platforms.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### GST on Related Services

Brokerage and other services still attract GST.

### ITC Restrictions

Input tax credit may not be available for exempt transactions.

### Confusion Among Taxpayers

Distinguishing between securities and services can be complex.

### Compliance for Intermediaries

Brokers must manage GST on services separately.

### Limited Tax Revenue

Government does not earn GST directly from securities transactions.

## 4) Applicability Across India (State-wise Context)

GST exemption on securities applies uniformly across India. Major Financial Hubs

- Maharashtra (Mumbai)

Home to stock exchanges and financial institutions

- Delhi NCR, Karnataka (Bangalore) Investment firms and fintech companies Growing Investment Regions
- Increasing retail participation across Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities

👉 GST rules for securities are consistent nationwide.

## 5) Features of GST on Securities

### Not Considered Goods or Services

Securities are excluded from GST scope.

### No GST on Trading

Buying and selling securities is not taxable.

### GST on Ancillary Services

- Brokerage: 18% GST
- Advisory services: 18% GST

### ITC Limitations

ITC cannot be claimed on exempt supplies.

### Clear Legal Definition

Defined under SCRA for clarity.

## 6) Documents List

For Investors

- PAN Card
- Demat account details For Businesses
- GST Registration (if applicable for services)
- PAN Card Operational Documents
- Trade confirmations
- Brokerage statements Compliance Documents
- GST returns (for service providers)

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Identify Transaction Type** — Determine whether it is:

- Sale/purchase of securities (exempt)
- Service (taxable)

1. **Apply GST Rules**

- No GST on securities
- GST applicable on related services

1. **Issue Invoice (for services)** — Include GST for brokerage or advisory services.
2. **File GST Returns** — Service providers must report taxable services.
3. **Maintain Records** — Keep transaction and service records.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### GST on Securities

- Nil

### GST on Services

- 18%

### Late Fees

- ₹50 per day

### Interest

- 18% per annum

### Penalties

- Minimum ₹10,000 or 10% of tax Practical Examples

## 1) Buying Shares

- GST: Not applicable

## 2) Selling Mutual Funds

- GST: Not applicable

## 3) Brokerage Charges

- GST: 18% Industry Impact

### Growth of Investment Culture

No GST encourages more participation.

### Rise of Fintech Platforms

Simplified tax structure supports digital trading.

### Compliance for Brokers

Service providers must manage GST carefully.

### Market Expansion

Increased accessibility for retail investors.

## Conclusion

The GST exemption on the sale and purchase of securities ensures that investment transactions remain tax-efficient and accessible. While securities themselves are not taxed, related services such as brokerage and advisory fees are subject to GST. Understanding this distinction is essential for investors and businesses to ensure proper compliance. As India’s financial markets continue to grow, GST policies will play a supportive role in maintaining efficiency and transparency.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: GST on Photography Services: Rates, SAC Codes & Compliance Guide
articles.excerpt: Complete GST guide for photographers in India: 18% rate, SAC codes for photography, videography, stock licensing, and drone services, ITC eligibility, registration threshold, and real-world examples.
articles.content:
## Introduction

Photography has evolved from a simple portrait studio business into a sophisticated creative and commercial services industry encompassing wedding photography, commercial shoots, aerial drone photography, stock photography licensing, and cinematography. For professionals in this space, GST applicability covers not just the shoot itself but also post-production, equipment rental, and intellectual property aspects of the business.

Whether you are a freelance photographer, a studio owner, a corporate photography firm, or a videographer, this article provides clarity on your GST obligations, applicable rates, SAC codes, and how to structure your invoices correctly.

## GST Rate Table for Photography and Related Services

| Service Type | GST Rate | SAC Code | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Photography services (general) | 18% | 998387 | Standard creative service rate |
| Videography and film production services | 18% | 998387 | Includes wedding videography |
| Aerial / drone photography services | 18% | 998387 | Same SAC as ground photography |
| Studio rental (photography space) | 18% | 997212 | Immovable property rental for commercial use |
| Photo editing and retouching | 18% | 998319 | Post-production services |
| Stock photography licensing | 18% | 997337 | Intellectual property licensing |
| Photo printing services | 12% | 998912 | Physical output product |
| Photography equipment rental | 18% | 997319 | Camera, lens, lighting hire |
| Photography training and workshops | 18% | 999294 | Non-recognised institution |
| Event photography (corporate) | 18% | 998387 | B2B event coverage |
| Fashion and commercial photography | 18% | 998387 | Advertising photography |

## SAC Code Breakdown

| SAC Code | Description | Applicable To |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 998387 | Photography and videography services | Weddings, events, commercial, aerial shoots |
| 998319 | Other technical and trade services | Photo editing, colour grading, retouching |
| 997337 | Licensing of intellectual property | Stock photo sales, image rights licensing |
| 997319 | Rental services of machinery and equipment | Equipment hire without operator |
| 997212 | Rental services of other buildings | Studio space rental for shoots |
| 998912 | Printing and reproduction services | Photo printing, album production |

## Registration Threshold for Photographers

Individual photographers and small studios must register for GST when their aggregate annual turnover exceeds:

- **Rs. 20 lakh** for general category states
- **Rs. 10 lakh** for special category states (Manipur, Mizoram, Nagaland, Tripura)

Below these thresholds, registration is optional but may be advantageous if the photographer has significant ITC-eligible input costs (equipment, software, studio rent).

## ITC for Photographers

Registered photographers can claim ITC on:

- Camera bodies, lenses, and accessories purchased for business use
- Lighting equipment (strobes, reflectors, light stands)
- Editing software subscriptions (Adobe Creative Cloud, Lightroom)
- Studio rent paid to a GST-registered landlord
- Travel expenses incurred for outstation shoots (airfare, accommodation)
- Marketing and advertising costs

ITC is not available on goods or services used for personal purposes. A camera purchased for both personal and professional use should have ITC claimed only on the business-use portion.

## Real-World Examples

### Example A: Wedding Photographer

A wedding photographer in Mumbai charges Rs. 2,00,000 for a two-day wedding shoot including edited photos and a highlights video. GST at 18% = Rs. 36,000. Total invoice = Rs. 2,36,000. ITC can be claimed on camera maintenance, software subscriptions, and travel.

### Example B: Commercial Photography Agency

A Bengaluru agency provides product photography to an e-commerce company. Contract value: Rs. 5,00,000 for 1,000 product shots:

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Photography service | Rs. 5,00,000 |
| GST at 18% | Rs. 90,000 |
| **Total invoice** | **Rs. 5,90,000** |

The e-commerce company (GST-registered) claims full ITC of Rs. 90,000 against its output tax liability. Net GST cost to the e-commerce company is **zero**.

### Example C: Stock Photography

A photographer sells the right to use an image to a magazine for Rs. 10,000. This is licensing of intellectual property (SAC 997337) taxed at 18% = Rs. 1,800. If the photographer is registered, they must charge and remit the GST.

### Example D: Drone Photography

A real estate developer hires a drone photography firm for an aerial survey, billing Rs. 80,000. GST at 18% = Rs. 14,400. Total invoice = Rs. 94,400. The developer claims ITC since this is a business expense for a GST-registered construction firm.

## Industry Impact

- **Freelancer compliance burden:** The 18% GST and associated compliance (monthly/quarterly returns, GSTR-1, GSTR-3B, annual return) represents a significant administrative burden for individual photographers operating as sole proprietors.
- **ITC advantage for studios:** Photography studios with significant equipment investments benefit from ITC claims, partially offsetting the 18% GST charged to clients.
- **Corporate photography:** B2B photography contracts are GST-neutral since corporate clients claim full ITC. This has made corporate photography more competitive as effective costs have not risen.
- **Wedding photography pricing:** Consumer awareness of GST is increasing. Photographers who previously operated in the cash economy are now being asked for GST-compliant invoices, especially by corporate employees who want to claim reimbursements.
- **International clients:** Photographers providing services to foreign clients may qualify for export of service, making the supply zero-rated if payment is received in foreign exchange.

> [!TIP] Services to foreign clients may be zero-rated
> If you shoot for an overseas brand or agency and receive payment in foreign exchange, the service may qualify as an export of service — making it zero-rated with full ITC refund eligibility. Maintain proper documentation (FIRC, client contracts) to support the export claim.

## Key Insights

- Photography services and equipment are consistently taxed at 18%; no concessional rate applies
- The nature of the supply matters: photo prints are taxed at 12%, but the shooting service is at 18%
- Drone operators must hold a Remote Pilot Licence (DGCA) and separately comply with GST on their services
- Photographers providing services through gig platforms should verify how the platform structures its GST collection

## FAQs

### Q1. Does a part-time or hobby photographer who earns below Rs. 20 lakh need to register for GST?

No. If aggregate annual turnover from all supplies is below Rs. 20 lakh (or Rs. 10 lakh in special category states), registration is not mandatory. The photographer can operate without GST registration and should not collect GST from clients.

### Q2. Is there GST on photos sold as NFTs?

NFT transactions involving digital photographs likely attract 18% GST as electronically supplied services or as transfer of intangible goods. The regulatory classification is still evolving, and specific clarification from CBIC is awaited.

### Q3. How should a photographer handle GST when clients pay through wedding portals or aggregators?

The photographer remains the supplier and must issue a GST-compliant invoice to the client. The portal's commission is a separate service on which the portal charges GST to the photographer. Both transactions need to be accounted for separately.

### Q4. Can a photography school or institute claim exempt status like other educational institutions?

Only institutions affiliated with universities or providing recognised certificates/diplomas may claim the education exemption. Private photography schools and workshops generally do not qualify and must charge **18% GST** on course fees.

### Q5. Is GST applicable on photo albums and printed products delivered as part of a photography package?

When an album is part of a bundled photography service package, the principal supply (photography service) determines the rate, and the entire package is taxed at **18%**. If photo printing is invoiced separately without the photography service, the printing service attracts **12% GST** under SAC 998912.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 115BAC New Tax Regime: Slabs, Deductions, Exemptions & Benefits
articles.excerpt: Section 115BAC of the Income Tax Act, 1961 introduced the new tax regime to simplify income tax calculation for individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs). Initially optional, it has now become the default tax regime for taxpayers,…
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Section 115BAC of the Income Tax Act, 1961 introduced the new tax regime to simplify income tax calculation for individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs). Initially optional, it has now become the default tax regime for taxpayers, though individuals can still choose the old regime if beneficial. The new regime offers lower tax rates but removes most deductions and exemptions available under the old system. The idea is to:

- Simplify tax filing
- Reduce dependency on tax-saving investments
- Provide flexibility in income usage

### The regime is particularly beneficial for

- Salaried individuals without major deductions
- Young professionals
- Freelancers

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Lower Tax Rates

Reduced slab rates compared to old regime.

### Simplified Tax Filing

No need to track multiple deductions.

### Default Regime

Automatically applied unless opted out.

### Higher Rebate Benefit

Rebate under Section 87A extended up to ₹7 lakh income.

### Flexibility

No forced investments for tax saving.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### No Major Deductions

### Cannot claim

- Section 80C
- HRA
- LTA

### Not Ideal for High Deduction Taxpayers

People with home loans or investments may lose benefits.

### Limited Tax Planning

Fewer ways to reduce taxable income.

### Confusion Between Regimes

Taxpayers must compare both regimes.

### Switching Restrictions

Business income taxpayers have limits on switching.

## 4) Applicability in India (State-wise Context)

The new tax regime applies uniformly across India. Urban Taxpayers

- Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore

Prefer new regime due to higher salaries and fewer deductions Middle-Class Taxpayers

- Tier 2 cities like Jaipur, Lucknow Often compare both regimes Self-Employed Professionals
- Across all states Choose based on deduction eligibility

👉 No state-wise variation, purely central income tax.

## 5) Features

### Tax Slabs

- Up to ₹3 lakh: Nil
- ₹3–6 lakh: 5%
- ₹6–9 lakh: 10%
- ₹9–12 lakh: 15%
- ₹12–15 lakh: 20%
- Above ₹15 lakh: 30%

### Standard Deduction

₹50,000 available for salaried individuals.

### Rebate under Section 87A

No tax up to ₹7 lakh income.

### No Deductions

Most exemptions removed.

### Default Regime

Automatically applied unless opted out.

## 6) Documents List

Basic Documents

- PAN Card
- Aadhaar Card Income Documents
- Salary slips
- Form 16 Other Documents
- Bank statements
- Investment proofs (if comparing regimes)

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Calculate Income** — Determine total income from all sources.
2. **Compare Regimes** — Evaluate old vs new regime benefits.
3. **Choose Regime** — Opt for new regime or switch to old.
4. **Inform Employer** — Declare choice for TDS calculation.
5. **File ITR** — Select regime while filing return.
6. **Pay Tax** — Based on applicable slabs.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### Income Tax

As per slab rates

### Surcharge

Applicable on higher incomes

### Cess

4% Health and Education cess

### Penalties

Applicable for late filing

## Conclusion

Section 115BAC has significantly transformed India’s income tax system by offering a simplified and flexible approach. While it benefits taxpayers who do not rely heavily on deductions, it may not be suitable for those with significant investments or exemptions. Choosing between the old and new regime requires careful evaluation, but for many individuals, the new regime offers a straightforward and hassle-free taxation method.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Which ITR to File: Types of ITR Forms and Applicability
articles.excerpt: Filing an Income Tax Return (ITR) is a mandatory compliance requirement for individuals and entities whose income exceeds the prescribed limits under the Income Tax Act, 1961. However, one of the most common questions taxpayers face is:…
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Filing an Income Tax Return (ITR) is a mandatory compliance requirement for individuals and entities whose income exceeds the prescribed limits under the Income Tax Act, 1961. However, one of the most common questions taxpayers face is: “Which ITR form should I file?” The Income Tax Department provides multiple ITR forms, each designed for specific categories of taxpayers based on:

- Source of income
- Income level
- Type of taxpayer (individual, firm, company, etc.)

Choosing the correct ITR form is crucial because filing the wrong form can lead to:

- Defective return notices
- Processing delays
- Penalties The main ITR forms include:
- ITR-1 (Sahaj)
- ITR-2
- ITR-3
- ITR-4 (Sugam)
- ITR-5, 6, 7 (for entities)

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Simplified Filing

Each form is designed for specific taxpayer categories.

### Faster Processing

Correct form ensures smooth processing of returns.

### Reduced Errors

Pre-defined formats reduce mistakes.

### Better Compliance

Helps meet legal requirements efficiently.

### Easy Tax Calculation

Forms guide users based on income type.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Confusion in Selection

Multiple forms can confuse taxpayers.

### Frequent Changes

Forms may change every year.

### Complex for Multiple Income Sources

More income streams require detailed forms.

### Risk of Defective Return

Wrong form leads to notices under Section 139(9).

### Limited Awareness

Many taxpayers rely on professionals.

## 4) Applicability in India (State-wise Context)

ITR filing rules are uniform across India. Urban Areas

- Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore Salaried and business taxpayers Tier 2 & Tier 3 Cities
- Increasing number of taxpayers Rural Areas
- Growing awareness due to digital filing

👉 Income tax is centrally governed, so no state variation.

## 5) Features

### ITR-1 (Sahaj)

For salaried individuals with income up to ₹50 lakh and one house property.

### ITR-2

For individuals with capital gains or multiple properties.

### ITR-3

For individuals with business or professional income.

### ITR-4 (Sugam)

For presumptive income under Sections 44AD, 44ADA.

### ITR-5, 6, 7

For firms, companies, and trusts.

## 6) Documents List

Basic Documents

- PAN Card
- Aadhaar Card Income Documents
- Form 16
- Salary slips
- Business income records Other Documents
- Bank statements
- Investment proofs
- Capital gains statements

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Identify Income Sources** — Salary, business, capital gains, etc.
2. **Choose Correct ITR Form** — Based on income type and eligibility.
3. **Gather Documents** — Collect all required financial documents.
4. **Fill ITR Form** — Enter income, deductions, and tax details.
5. **Verify Details** — Check accuracy before submission.
6. **Submit Return** — File online through income tax portal.
7. **E-Verify** — Complete verification via Aadhaar OTP, bank, or DSC.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### Filing Fees

- Nil if income below threshold

### Late Filing Fees

- Up to ₹5,000 under Section 234F

### Interest

- Applicable on unpaid tax

### Penalties

- For incorrect or non-filing Conclusion

Choosing the correct ITR form is the first and most important step in income tax filing. Each form is designed for specific income categories, and selecting the right one ensures smooth processing and compliance. Taxpayers should carefully evaluate their income sources and consult professionals if needed to avoid errors. With increasing digitization, filing ITR has become easier, but accuracy remains critical.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Income Tax Form PDF Download: How to Download ITR-1, ITR-2, ITR-3, ITR-4, ITR-5 Forms from Income Tax Portal
articles.excerpt: Filing your Income Tax Return (ITR) in India requires selecting the correct form and submitting it through the official portal of the Income Tax Department. While most taxpayers file returns online, many still prefer or require offline…
articles.content:
## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

Filing your Income Tax Return (ITR) in India requires selecting the correct form and submitting it through the official portal of the Income Tax Department. While most taxpayers file returns online, many still prefer or require offline utilities and PDF forms for preparation and record-keeping. The Income Tax Department provides downloadable utilities and forms for:

- ITR-1 (Sahaj)
- ITR-2
- ITR-3
- ITR-4 (Sugam)
- ITR-5, ITR-6, ITR-7 These forms are available in:
- Excel utility
- JSON utility
- PDF format (mainly for reference and instructions)

### Downloading the correct form is essential for

- Accurate filing
- Offline preparation
- Avoiding errors

## 2) Advantages / Benefits

### Offline Preparation

Allows taxpayers to prepare returns without internet dependency.

### Better Accuracy

Forms include validations to reduce errors.

### Easy Record Keeping

Downloaded forms can be saved for future reference.

### Flexibility

Users can fill data at their own pace.

### Useful for Professionals

Chartered accountants prefer offline utilities for bulk filing.

## 3) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Not Fully PDF-Based Filing

Actual filing must be done online.

### Technical Complexity

Utilities may be confusing for beginners.

### Version Updates

Forms are updated frequently.

### Compatibility Issues

Excel/JSON utilities may require specific software.

### Manual Effort

Requires careful data entry.

## 4) Applicability in India (State-wise Context)

ITR form download and filing is uniform across India. Urban Users

- Prefer online filing Professionals
- Use offline utilities for multiple clients Rural Users
- Increasing adoption due to digital awareness

👉 The process is centralized and identical nationwide.

## 5) Features

### Multiple ITR Forms Available

ITR-1 to ITR-7 based on taxpayer category.

### Utility Formats

Excel and JSON utilities for offline filing.

### Pre-Filled Data

Forms can auto-fill using PAN details.

### Validation Checks

Built-in error detection before submission.

### Secure Filing

Data uploaded through official portal.

## 6) Documents List

Basic Documents

- PAN Card
- Aadhaar Card Income Documents
- Form 16
- Salary slips
- Business income records Other Documents
- Bank statements
- Capital gains reports
- Investment proofs

## 7) Process / Procedure

1. **Visit Income Tax Portal** — Go to official website.
2. **Navigate to Downloads** — Click on “Downloads” → “Income Tax Returns”.
3. **Select Assessment Year** — Choose relevant year.
4. **Choose ITR Form** — Select ITR-1, ITR-2, etc.
5. **Download Utility** — Download Excel or JSON utility.
6. **Fill Details** — Enter income, deductions, and tax details.
7. **Validate and Generate File** — Validate and generate JSON file.
8. **Upload on Portal** — Upload file and submit return.
9. **E-Verify** — Verify using Aadhaar OTP or bank method.

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### Download Charges

- Free

### Filing Fees

- Nil (within due date and threshold)

### Late Filing Fees

- Up to ₹5,000

### Interest

- Applicable on unpaid tax Conclusion

Downloading ITR forms from the Income Tax portal is a simple yet essential step in the tax filing process. While most taxpayers prefer online filing, offline utilities provide flexibility, accuracy, and better control over data. Understanding how to download and use these forms ensures smooth filing and reduces the chances of errors or notices. For professionals and individuals alike, mastering this process is key to efficient tax compliance. Difference Between TDS and TCS Difference Between TDS and TCS

## 1) Detailed Brief / Overview

In India’s tax system, both TDS (Tax Deducted at Source) and TCS (Tax Collected at Source) are mechanisms to collect tax at the point of transaction. They are governed under the Income Tax Act, 1961 and play a major role in ensuring steady tax collection and reducing evasion. While both aim to collect tax in advance, the key difference lies in who collects it:

- TDS → Deducted by the payer before making payment
- TCS → Collected by the seller at the time of sale

### These provisions apply to various transactions such as

- Salaries
- Interest payments
- Sale of goods
- High-value transactions

Understanding the difference is essential for businesses, professionals, and even individuals to ensure correct compliance.

## 2) Key Differences (Quick Comparison)

Basis TDS TCS Full Form Tax Deducted at Source Tax Collected at Source

## Who

deducts/collects Deducted by payer Collected by seller

When applied At time of payment/credit At time of sale Applicable on Salary, interest, rent, etc. Sale of goods, scrap, etc. Governing sections Various (192, 194C, etc.) Section 206C Responsibility Deductor Collector

## 3) Advantages / Benefits

### Early Tax Collection

Government receives tax throughout the year.

### Reduces Tax Evasion

Minimizes chances of underreporting income.

### Improves Compliance

Ensures transactions are tracked.

### Spreads Tax Burden

Tax is paid gradually instead of lump sum.

### Transparency

Reflected in Form 26AS for taxpayers.

## 4) Disadvantages / Limitations

### Compliance Burden

Businesses must handle deductions, filings, and certificates.

### Cash Flow Impact

Tax is deducted/collected before actual income realization.

### Complexity

Multiple sections and rates can confuse taxpayers.

### Risk of Errors

Incorrect deduction leads to penalties.

### Refund Delays

Excess deduction may take time to refund.

## 5) Features

### TDS Deduction

Applicable on payments like salary, rent, interest.

### TCS Collection

Applicable on sale of specified goods.

### PAN Requirement

Higher rates apply if PAN is not provided.

### Return Filing

Quarterly returns required for both.

### Certificates Issued

- TDS: Form 16 / 16A
- TCS: Form 27D

## 6) Documents List

For Deductor / Collector

- PAN Card
- TAN (Tax Deduction Account Number) For Transactions
- Invoices
- Payment records Compliance Documents
- TDS/TCS returns
- Challans

## 7) Process / Procedure

TDS Process

1. **Identify applicable transaction**
2. **Deduct tax at prescribed rate**
3. **Deposit with government**
4. **File TDS return**
5. **Issue certificate** — TCS Process
6. **Identify applicable goods**
7. **Collect tax from buyer**
8. **Deposit with government**
9. **File TCS return**
10. **Issue certificate**

## 8) Government Cost / Fees Payable

### Interest

- 1% or 1.5% per month for delay

### Late Filing Fees

- ₹200 per day

### Penalties

- ₹10,000 to ₹1,00,000 Real-Life Examples

TDS Example Salary of ₹50,000 → Employer deducts TDS before paying. TCS Example Car purchase above ₹10 lakh → Seller collects TCS.

## Conclusion

TDS and TCS are essential tools in India’s taxation system, ensuring that tax is collected at the source of income or transaction. While they serve a similar purpose, their application differs based on the nature of the transaction and the party responsible. For businesses, understanding both mechanisms is critical to avoid penalties and ensure compliance. For individuals, it helps track taxes paid and claim credits accurately.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How to Save Tax for Salary Above 10 Lakhs
articles.excerpt: Understanding the Problem First Once your salary crosses ₹10 lakh, you enter higher tax slabs under the Income Tax Act, 1961, which means a larger portion of your income gets taxed. If you don’t actively plan, you could end up paying…
articles.content:
Understanding the Problem First Once your salary crosses ₹10 lakh, you enter higher tax slabs under the Income Tax Act, 1961, which means a larger portion of your income gets taxed. If you don’t actively plan, you could end up paying significantly more tax than necessary. The good news is that Indian tax law provides multiple legal ways to reduce your taxable income. The key is knowing what actually works and what doesn’t.

1. **Choose the Right Tax Regime (Most Important** — Decision) Before doing anything else, decide between: Old Tax Regime

- Higher tax rates
- Multiple deductions available New Tax Regime
- Lower tax rates
- Almost no deductions

### 👉 Rule of thumb

- If you can claim deductions above ~₹2–2.5 lakh → Old regime is better
- If not → New regime is usually better

1. **Use the Big Deductions First (High Impact)** — Section 80C (₹1.5 Lakh Limit) This is your foundation. Best options:

- ELSS (high returns + tax saving)
- PPF (safe, long-term)
- EPF (already deducted for many)

👉 Don’t just invest randomly — choose based on your goals. Section 80CCD(1B) – NPS (Extra ₹50,000) This is over and above 80C.

- Helps reduce taxable income further
- Good for retirement planning

👉 One of the most underused deductions. Section 80D – Health Insurance

- ₹25,000 (self + family)
- ₹50,000 (parents, if senior citizens)

👉 This is both tax saving and financial protection.

1. **Reduce Taxable Salary Components** — HRA (House Rent Allowance) If you live on rent:

- Claim HRA exemption
- Or pay rent to parents (with proper documentation) Standard Deduction

Flat ₹50,000 deduction for salaried individuals 👉 Automatically reduces taxable income

1. **Use Loans Smartly (Advanced but Powerful)** — Home Loan Benefits

- ₹1.5 lakh (principal under 80C)
- ₹2 lakh (interest under Section 24)

👉 Huge tax saver if structured properly Education Loan (Section 80E)

- Interest fully deductible
- No upper limit

1. **Don’t Ignore These Extra Opportunities** — Donations (Section 80G)

- 50% or 100% deduction depending on fund

LTA (Leave Travel Allowance)

- Travel expenses can be exempt Capital Gains Planning
- Reinvest gains to save tax Common Mistakes to Avoid
- Investing only in March (bad planning)
- Ignoring NPS
- Choosing wrong tax regime blindly
- Not submitting proofs to employer
- Over-investing just to save tax

👉 Saving tax should not ruin your liquidity or goals Quick Example Salary: ₹12,00,000 If you use:

- 80C → ₹1.5L
- NPS → ₹50K
- 80D → ₹25K
- Standard Deduction → ₹50K

👉 Taxable income drops significantly 👉 You save ₹50,000–₹1,00,000+ in tax

What Actually Works (Simple Strategy) If you want a no-nonsense plan:

1. **Max out 80C**
2. **Add NPS (₹50K)**
3. **Take health insurance**
4. **Claim HRA or home loan**

## Compare regimes before filing

That alone covers 90% of effective tax saving

## Conclusion

Saving tax above ₹10 lakh salary isn’t about complicated tricks. It’s about:

- Choosing the right regime
- Using the right deductions
- Planning early

Do it right, and you won’t just save tax — you’ll build wealth at the same time.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 80DD: Tax Relief for Families Caring for Differently-Abled Dependents
articles.excerpt: Complete guide to Section 80DD deductions for taxpayers caring for disabled dependents: flat deduction of Rs. 75,000 or Rs. 1.25 lakh, qualifying disabilities, Form 10-IA, and required documentation.
articles.content:
## Overview

Section 80DD of the Income Tax Act is specifically designed to provide financial relief to individuals and HUFs who bear the expenses of maintaining and providing medical care for a dependent with a disability. **This is not a deduction for the disabled person themselves, but for the person supporting them financially.**

The provision recognises that caring for a disabled family member involves significant recurring expenditure — whether on medical treatment, rehabilitation, special training, or nursing. The deduction is available on a flat-rate basis, which means it does not require you to submit detailed bills for every rupee spent on the dependent's care.

It is one of the most compassionate provisions in Indian tax law, and yet it remains one of the least claimed — primarily because families are unaware of its existence or the documentation required.

## Eligibility

### Who can claim

- Individual taxpayers (resident in India)
- Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs)

### Who qualifies as a dependent

- **For individuals:** spouse, children (dependent), parents, brothers, and sisters who are wholly or mainly dependent on the taxpayer for support.
- **For HUFs:** any member of the HUF.

The dependent must be a person with a disability as defined under the Persons with Disabilities Act or specified diseases under Rule 11D of the Income Tax Rules.

### Qualifying disabilities

- Blindness
- Low vision
- Leprosy-cured
- Hearing impairment (40% or more)
- Locomotor disability
- Mental retardation
- Mental illness
- Autism, cerebral palsy, and multiple disabilities (under Section 80DD for severe disability)

> [!IMPORTANT] 80DD vs 80U
> The dependent should **not** have claimed a deduction under Section 80U for the same disability. 80DD is for the caretaker; 80U is for the disabled person themselves.

## Deduction Limits

| Category | Disability Percentage | Flat Deduction Allowed |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Normal disability | 40% to 79% | Rs. 75,000 |
| Severe disability | 80% or more | Rs. 1,25,000 |

> [!NOTE] Flat deductions, not reimbursements
> Even if your actual expenses are lower than Rs. 75,000, you get the full deduction. Conversely, even if expenses exceed Rs. 1,25,000, the cap applies.

## Required Documentation

| Document | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| Certificate of disability from medical authority | Mandatory to prove disability percentage |
| Self-declaration in Form 10-IA | Mandatory filing on the income tax portal |
| Insurance policy details (if applicable) | For policies taken for the disabled dependent |

Form 10-IA must be filed on the Income Tax portal. Disability certificates must be from a recognised medical authority such as a government hospital or authorised medical board.

## Example Calculation

Kavitha is a working professional with a gross income of Rs. 10,00,000 per year under the old tax regime. Her younger brother, who lives with her family and is wholly dependent on her, has a locomotor disability certified at 55%. Since the disability is between 40% and 79%, she qualifies for the **normal disability deduction of Rs. 75,000**.

### Without 80DD

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Taxable income (after SD and 80C) | Rs. 7,00,000 |
| Tax payable (approx at 20% slab) | Rs. 72,500 |

### With 80DD

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Taxable income after 80DD | Rs. 6,25,000 |
| Tax payable (approx) | Rs. 57,500 |
| **Tax saved** | **Rs. 15,000** |

## Optimization Tips

1. **Get the disability certificate updated if the condition is progressive.** Many families hold outdated certificates that show a lower disability percentage, causing them to claim a lower deduction when they may qualify for the severe disability category.
2. **Maintain the insurance policy receipt for disabled dependents.** If you have taken a life insurance policy or a maintenance deposit scheme for your disabled dependent through a registered insurer, the premium paid is also eligible under 80DD within the above limits.
3. **File Form 10-IA proactively** at the start of the year via the income tax portal, not just at the time of filing returns. This helps ensure your employer adjusts TDS accordingly if you are salaried.
4. **Families where both spouses work should decide who claims the deduction.** Only one person can claim for the same dependent. Typically, the one in the higher tax slab should claim it to maximise tax savings.
5. **Keep the disability certificate renewed.** Certificates have validity periods. Filing a return with an expired certificate can trigger a disallowance during scrutiny.

## Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Assuming actual expenses must equal the flat deduction amount.** This is a lump-sum deduction. You do not need to have spent exactly Rs. 75,000.
2. **Trying to claim both 80DD and 80U for the same person.** The taxpayer can claim 80DD for supporting a disabled dependent. The disabled person themselves can claim 80U. But the same disability cannot be claimed under both by the same taxpayer simultaneously.
3. **Claiming the deduction without filing Form 10-IA.** The form is mandatory. Without it, the deduction is likely to be disallowed during processing or scrutiny.
4. **Not distinguishing between dependent and non-dependent relatives.** A sibling or parent who has their own income source and is not wholly or mainly dependent on you does not qualify as a dependent for Section 80DD.
5. **Forgetting that the deduction is only available under the old tax regime.** Switching to the new regime means losing this relief entirely.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. Can I claim 80DD if my dependent's disability was not present at the start of the financial year?

Yes. As long as the disability exists during the financial year and is certified by a recognised medical authority, you can claim the deduction for that year. The disability does not need to have been present for the entire year.

### Q2. My child has autism. Which category of disability does that fall under?

Autism is classified under **severe disability** for Section 80DD purposes when certified at 80% or above, allowing a deduction of Rs. 1,25,000. For certifications between 40% and 79%, it would fall in the normal category at Rs. 75,000.

### Q3. Can an HUF claim 80DD if one of its members has a disability?

Yes. HUFs can claim the 80DD deduction if a member of the HUF has a qualifying disability and the HUF bears the maintenance and medical expenses. The same certificate and Form 10-IA requirements apply.

### Q4. Is there any TDS adjustment possible for 80DD benefits during the year?

Yes. If you are salaried, you can submit Form 10-IA and the disability certificate to your employer as part of your investment declaration (Form 12BB). Your employer can then reduce TDS accordingly.

### Q5. What happens if my dependent recovers from the disability before the end of the financial year?

If the disability no longer exists for any part of the year due to recovery or reclassification, you technically do not qualify for the flat deduction for that year. It is advisable to consult a tax professional in such situations to assess the correct claim position.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Personal Loan Tax Benefits: When Does a Personal Loan Qualify for a Deduction?
articles.excerpt: Complete guide to when personal loan interest is tax-deductible in India: home renovation, business use, investments, and documentation required to successfully claim deductions.
articles.content:
## Overview

Personal loans are commonly marketed as multi-purpose financial tools, and they are. But a frequent and important question is whether the interest paid on a personal loan is tax-deductible. **The short answer: it depends entirely on what the loan was used for.**

Unlike home loans or education loans, personal loans do not have a dedicated section in the Income Tax Act. There is no single provision that says "personal loan interest is deductible." Instead, the deductibility of interest is determined by the **end use** of the loan proceeds. If the loan was used for a purpose that qualifies for a tax deduction under some other provision of the Act, the interest may be deductible under that provision.

This makes personal loan tax planning a documentation and purpose-tracking exercise, not a straightforward claim process.

## When Personal Loan Interest Is Tax-Deductible

| Loan Purpose | Applicable Section | Deduction Available |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Purchase or construction of a house | Section 24(b) | Up to Rs. 2,00,000 (self-occupied) |
| Business or professional purpose | Section 37 or 36 | Full interest as business expense |
| Purchase of shares or investments | Section 57 | Against income from those assets |
| Home renovation / repair | Section 24(b) | Up to Rs. 30,000 (within 24(b) limit) |
| Education of self or family (if structured correctly) | Potentially Section 80E via separate loan | Requires separate education loan documentation |

## When Personal Loan Interest Is NOT Tax-Deductible

| Loan Purpose | Tax Treatment |
| --- | --- |
| Consumer goods or electronics | No deduction |
| Wedding expenses | No deduction |
| Vacation or travel | No deduction |
| Repayment of another personal loan | No deduction |
| Medical emergencies (personal) | No deduction under personal loan (may qualify under 80DDB with separate conditions) |

## Eligibility Conditions

For personal loan interest to be deductible, all of the following must be true:

1. The loan must be from a **recognised financial institution** (bank, NBFC, registered lender). Loans from friends or family without proper documentation rarely pass scrutiny.
2. The **end use of the loan must be clearly documented**. Bank statements showing the disbursement and end use are critical. You must be able to prove where the money went.
3. The deduction must be claimed under the **applicable section for that end use**, not as a standalone personal loan deduction.
4. The taxpayer must be under the **old tax regime** (since deductions under 24b, 57, etc. are relevant primarily there).

## Example — Personal Loan for Home Renovation

Suresh takes a personal loan of Rs. 5,00,000 at 12% per annum for renovating his self-occupied home. Annual interest: Rs. 60,000.

Under Section 24(b), interest paid for **repair or renovation of a self-occupied property** is deductible up to Rs. 30,000 per year.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Interest paid | Rs. 60,000 |
| Deduction allowed (renovation cap) | Rs. 30,000 |
| Tax saved at 20% slab | **Rs. 6,000** |

> [!NOTE] Renovation vs purchase limits differ
> If the loan had been for purchase or construction of the house, the full interest up to Rs. 2,00,000 would have been claimable. The renovation limit of Rs. 30,000 is a sub-limit within Section 24(b).

## Example — Personal Loan for Business

Deepa runs a consulting business and takes a Rs. 3,00,000 personal loan to buy a laptop and office equipment. She pays Rs. 36,000 in interest annually.

Since the loan is used for business purposes, the interest of **Rs. 36,000 is fully deductible** as a business expense under Section 37(1) of the Income Tax Act.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Interest deductible (no cap) | Rs. 36,000 |
| Tax saved at 30% slab | **Rs. 10,800** |

This makes business-purpose personal loans one of the most tax-efficient uses, since the deduction is uncapped for genuine business expenses.

## Optimization Tips

1. **Maintain clear evidence of end use.** At the time of taking the loan, note the purpose in writing. Transfer loan funds directly to the payee (contractor, seller, broker) rather than withdrawing cash, so the bank trail is clean.
2. **Consider a separate loan account for each purpose.** Mixing personal loan funds across uses (partly for home, partly for vacation) makes it almost impossible to claim the tax benefit cleanly.
3. **For business owners and freelancers**, a personal loan used fully for the business is often easier to justify and claim than attempting a formal business loan with collateral requirements.
4. **If you are taking a personal loan for investment in stocks or mutual funds**, note that the interest can be set off against income from the same investments under Section 57. Keep the investment statements and bank transfer records.
5. **Restructure loans if possible.** If you already have a home loan and want additional funds for home-related purposes, a **top-up on your existing home loan** qualifies under Section 24(b) more cleanly than a separate personal loan.

## Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Assuming all personal loan interest is automatically deductible.** This is the most widespread misconception. Without proof of end use, the claim will not hold.
2. **Mixing loan funds.** If you receive Rs. 5 lakh and use Rs. 2 lakh for home renovation and Rs. 3 lakh for a vacation, you cannot claim interest on the full loan. You can only claim interest proportionate to the eligible purpose amount.
3. **Not collecting a certificate or statement from the lender.** For any tax claim based on loan interest, a letter from the lender confirming the loan terms and interest paid is essential documentation.
4. **Claiming the interest under the wrong section.** For example, claiming renovation loan interest under 80C instead of 24(b). Know which section applies to your specific use case.
5. **Assuming the new tax regime allows interest deductions on personal loans.** Under the new regime, most deductions are eliminated. Personal loan interest deductions are largely unavailable for those who have opted for the simplified regime.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. If I take a personal loan and invest in an FD, can I claim the loan interest as a deduction?

Yes, under Section 57, interest paid on a loan taken to earn income can be deducted against that income. If your FD interest is Rs. 20,000 and you paid Rs. 15,000 in personal loan interest to fund it, your taxable interest income reduces to Rs. 5,000.

### Q2. Can I claim personal loan interest for purchasing agricultural land?

No. Agricultural income is generally exempt from tax in India, and expenses related to earning exempt income are not deductible against other taxable income.

### Q3. My friend lent me money informally for my business. Can I claim that interest?

The deductibility of interest paid to a friend depends on whether the transaction is genuine and whether you can prove it. A proper loan agreement, regular repayment via bank transfer, and evidence of business use are all required. Without documentation, such claims often get disallowed.

### Q4. Is the GST paid on personal loan processing fees deductible?

The processing fee itself is considered part of borrowing costs and may be deductible if the loan is for an eligible purpose (such as business or investment). GST embedded in it would be part of the overall cost and treated similarly.

### Q5. How do I show personal loan interest in my income tax return?

You do not report personal loan interest in a separate field. Instead, you claim it within the relevant section — such as entering it under "interest paid on borrowed capital" within **Schedule HP** (House Property) or **Schedule BP** (Business / Profession) depending on the use.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 80GGC: Deduction for Political Party Contributions
articles.excerpt: Complete guide to Section 80GGC tax deduction for individual donations to registered political parties and electoral trusts: eligibility, 100% deduction rule, banking channel requirement, and compliance tips.
articles.content:
## Overview

Section 80GGC of the Income Tax Act allows individual taxpayers to claim a deduction for contributions made to registered political parties or electoral trusts. In a democracy, encouraging citizen participation in political funding through transparency and tax incentives is a policy goal, and this section reflects that.

The provision is designed to encourage direct, traceable donations to political parties rather than opaque funding. **Contributions must be made through banking channels** — this is a strict requirement. Cash donations are entirely ineligible, and this rule has been in place and strictly enforced.

Section 80GGC applies specifically to individual contributors. A separate but related section, **Section 80GGB**, covers donations by companies to political parties and operates similarly.

## Eligibility

### Who can claim

- Individual taxpayers (salaried or non-salaried)
- Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs)
- Firms (though practically relevant mostly for individuals)
- Association of persons (AOP) or body of individuals (BOI)

### Who cannot claim

- Local authorities
- Artificial juridical persons wholly or partially funded by the government
- Companies (they fall under Section 80GGB)

The political party must be **registered under Section 29A of the Representation of the People Act, 1951**. Electoral trusts must be approved by the Central Government.

## Deduction Limits

| Parameter | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Maximum deduction | 100% of the amount contributed |
| Cash donations | Not eligible (zero deduction) |
| Mode of contribution | Cheque, DD, NEFT, RTGS, UPI, or other banking channels only |
| Applicable regime | Old regime only |
| Cap on deduction | No upper limit specified in the section |

> [!IMPORTANT] 100% deduction within taxable income
> There is no explicit rupee cap under 80GGC, but the deduction cannot exceed your total taxable income. Large donations may also attract scrutiny from the Income Tax Department.

## Comparison: 80GGC vs 80GGB

| Feature | Section 80GGC | Section 80GGB |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Applicable to | Individuals, HUFs, firms | Indian companies |
| Deduction amount | 100% of contribution | 100% of contribution |
| Cash donations | Not allowed | Not allowed |
| Cap | None (within taxable income) | None |
| Recipient | Registered political party or electoral trust | Registered political party or electoral trust |

## Example Calculation

Arun is a self-employed professional with a net taxable income of Rs. 8,00,000 under the old regime. He donates Rs. 1,00,000 to a registered national political party via bank transfer.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Taxable income before 80GGC | Rs. 8,00,000 |
| Less: Section 80GGC deduction | Rs. 1,00,000 |
| **Net taxable income** | **Rs. 7,00,000** |
| Tax saved at 20% slab | Rs. 20,000 |
| Additional 4% cess saving | Rs. 800 |
| **Total tax saved** | **Rs. 20,800** |

In effect, his Rs. 1,00,000 political contribution costs him only Rs. 79,200 after accounting for the tax benefit.

## Optimization Tips

1. **Always make political donations via banking channels.** A cheque or UPI transfer takes two minutes. A cash donation gives you zero tax benefit and also creates compliance problems.
2. **Obtain a receipt from the political party.** While the deduction does not require you to submit the receipt with your return, having proof is critical if your return is selected for scrutiny.
3. **Verify party registration.** The political party must be registered under Section 29A before donating. Donations to unregistered outfits or NGOs with political leanings do not qualify under 80GGC.
4. **Consider registered electoral trusts** if you are not committed to a single party. Electoral trusts pool donations from multiple contributors and distribute them to parties. Donations to approved electoral trusts can still qualify for 80GGC.
5. **Report in Schedule 80G of your ITR form.** Despite the name of the schedule, 80GGC claims are captured there as well. Ensure the political party's PAN is correctly entered.

## Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Donating in cash and expecting a deduction.** This is an absolute disqualification. Even a single rupee in cash is ineligible.
2. **Assuming all political entities qualify.** The party must be specifically registered under Section 29A. A donation to a politician's personal campaign fund or an unregistered advocacy group does not count.
3. **Confusing 80GGC with 80G.** Section 80G covers charitable donations and has different rules, limits, and qualifying organisations. Section 80GGC is exclusively for registered political parties and electoral trusts.
4. **Forgetting to report this in the ITR.** Many people make the donation, get a receipt, and then forget to reflect it in the correct schedule of their income tax return. The deduction is not automatic.
5. **Claiming 80GGC under the new tax regime.** Like most deductions, this benefit is available only under the old tax regime.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. Is there a minimum donation amount to qualify for 80GGC?

No minimum donation amount is specified under the law. Even small contributions via banking channels qualify. However, practically, it should make financial sense to claim it versus the compliance effort involved.

### Q2. Can I donate to multiple political parties in a year and claim 80GGC for all of them?

Yes. You can donate to multiple registered political parties in the same financial year, and the deduction is available for the **combined total** of all eligible donations.

### Q3. Do electoral bonds qualify for 80GGC?

Electoral bonds were a scheme where taxpayers could purchase bonds from SBI and donate them to political parties. The **Supreme Court struck down the electoral bond scheme in 2024**. As a result, electoral bond contributions are no longer a valid route. Banking channel contributions directly to parties remain the only eligible mode.

### Q4. I donated to a political party two years ago but forgot to claim it. Can I revise my return?

You can file a revised return within the relevant deadline. If the deadline has passed, you can still file a condonation of delay application. Speak to a tax consultant for assistance.

### Q5. Can a salaried person ask their employer to account for 80GGC while calculating TDS?

Yes. You can declare the donation on your **Form 12BB** submitted to your employer at the start of the year. The employer will then reduce TDS accordingly.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 194IA: TDS on Sale of Property
articles.excerpt: Section 194IA of the Income Tax Act, 1961 deals with Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on the purchase of immovable property. It places responsibility on the buyer to deduct tax before making payment to the seller. This provision was…
articles.content:
## Overview

Section 194IA of the Income Tax Act, 1961 deals with Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) on the purchase of immovable property. It places responsibility on the buyer to deduct tax before making payment to the seller. This provision was introduced to track high-value real estate transactions and ensure tax compliance. It applies to both residential and commercial property transactions, with certain thresholds and conditions.

When Does Section 194IA Apply TDS under this section is applicable when:

- The property value is ₹50 lakh or more
- The transaction involves immovable property (land, building, or both)
- The buyer is making payment to a resident seller

If the property value is below ₹50 lakh, TDS is not required. TDS Rate Under Section 194IA

- Standard rate: 1% of the transaction value Special cases:
- If seller does not provide PAN → 20% TDS may apply

The TDS must be deducted on the total sale consideration, not just the amount exceeding ₹50 lakh.

## Who is Responsible for Deducting TDS

- The buyer (purchaser) is responsible
- No requirement for the buyer to have a TAN
- PAN of both buyer and seller is mandatory Important Points to Note
- TDS must be deducted at the time of payment or credit, whichever is earlier
- Applies even if payment is made in installments
- Includes all charges such as:

○ Parking fees ○ Club membership ○ Maintenance charges These are considered part of the total transaction value.

## Features

### Threshold-Based Applicability

Applies only when property value is ₹50 lakh or more.

### Buyer Responsibility

Buyer deducts and deposits TDS.

### No TAN Required

Simplifies compliance for individuals.

### Mandatory PAN

Higher TDS applies if PAN is not available.

### Covers Total Consideration

Includes additional charges linked to property.

## Documents Required

- PAN of buyer and seller
- Property sale agreement
- Payment details
- Address details of both parties How to Deduct and Pay TDS (Process)

1. **Deduct 1% TDS at the time of payment**
2. **Fill Form 26QB online**
3. **Deposit TDS within 30 days from end of month**
4. **Download Form 16B (TDS certificate)**

## Provide Form 16B to seller

## Timeline to Remember

- TDS payment: Within 30 days
- Form 16B issuance: Within 15 days after filing Example Property value: ₹80,00,000
- TDS @1% = ₹80,000
- Buyer pays ₹79,20,000 to seller
- ₹80,000 deposited with government Penalties for Non-Compliance
- Interest for late deduction or payment
- Late filing fees
- Penalty for incorrect reporting

Non-compliance can lead to financial and legal consequences.

## Common Mistakes

- Deducting TDS only above ₹50 lakh
- Ignoring additional charges
- Missing deadline for Form 26QB
- Not issuing Form 16B Conclusion

Section 194IA ensures transparency in real estate transactions by requiring buyers to deduct TDS on high-value property purchases. While the process is relatively simple, strict adherence to timelines and documentation is essential. Understanding these requirements helps both buyers and sellers avoid penalties and ensures smooth compliance with tax laws.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Form 10IE: Option to Choose New Tax Regime
articles.excerpt: Form 10IE is a declaration form used by taxpayers to opt for the new tax regime under Section 115BAC of the Income Tax Act, 1961. While the new regime is now the default for individuals, certain taxpayers, especially those with business…
articles.content:
## Overview

Form 10IE is a declaration form used by taxpayers to opt for the new tax regime under Section 115BAC of the Income Tax Act, 1961. While the new regime is now the default for individuals, certain taxpayers, especially those with business or professional income, are required to formally submit this form to exercise or withdraw their option. The form ensures that the choice of tax regime is recorded officially with the Income Tax Department.

## Who Needs to File Form 10IE

### Form 10IE is mainly required for

- Individuals or HUFs having business or professional income
- Taxpayers who want to opt out of the new regime and choose the old regime

For salaried individuals without business income, the regime can generally be selected directly while filing the return, and separate submission of Form 10IE may not always be required.

When Should Form 10IE Be Filed The form must be submitted:

- Before filing the Income Tax Return (ITR)
- Within the due date for filing ITR

Failure to submit Form 10IE (where required) may result in the taxpayer being taxed under the default regime.

## Key Points to Understand

- The option exercised through Form 10IE is binding for business income taxpayers
- Switching between regimes is restricted once chosen
- The form must be filed online through the income tax portal
- It applies specifically to Section 115BAC Features

### Mandatory for Certain Taxpayers

Required for those with business or professional income.

### One-Time Option (with Restrictions)

Switching back is limited in future years.

### Online Filing

Submitted electronically on the portal.

### Linked to ITR Filing

Must be filed before return submission.

### Ensures Compliance

Records regime choice officially.

## Documents Required

- PAN Card
- Aadhaar details
- Business or professional income details
- Previous filing information (if applicable) How to Submit Form 10IE (Process)

1. **Log in to the income tax portal**
2. **Go to “e-File” → “Income Tax Forms”**
3. **Select Form 10IE**
4. **Choose the relevant assessment year**
5. **Fill required details**
6. **Submit the form**

## Verify using Aadhaar OTP or other methods

## Example

A freelancer earning professional income wants to opt for the new tax regime.

- They must submit Form 10IE before filing ITR
- Once submitted, the choice becomes binding with limited flexibility to switch later Common Mistakes
- Filing ITR without submitting Form 10IE (when required)
- Missing the due date
- Confusion between salaried and business taxpayers
- Assuming the default regime applies in all cases Conclusion

Form 10IE plays a crucial role for taxpayers opting into or out of the new tax regime, particularly those with business or professional income. Proper submission ensures that the chosen tax regime is valid and accepted by the Income Tax Department. Understanding when and how to file this form helps avoid incorrect tax treatment and ensures smooth compliance.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Defective Return Under Section 139(9): Meaning, Causes, and How to Fix It
articles.excerpt: A defective return notice under Section 139(9) gives you 15 days to correct your ITR before it is treated as never filed. This guide covers the 8 most common causes, consequences of not responding, a step-by-step correction process, and an example scenario.
articles.content:
## What Is a Defective Return?

Under Section 139(9) of the Income Tax Act, the Central Board of Direct Taxes has prescribed the conditions under which a return of income may be treated as **defective**. When the Assessing Officer or the CPC detects such defects, they issue a notice asking you to rectify the return within **15 days**.

If you do not respond within the time limit, the return is treated as if it was never filed — with serious downstream consequences.

## Common Reasons Why a Return Becomes Defective

| Reason | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Incomplete return | Return filed without all required schedules |
| Missing tax payment | Tax is payable but not paid before filing |
| Wrong ITR form | Wrong form used for income type (e.g., ITR-1 filed for capital gains income) |
| No balance sheet or P&L | Business income declared without attaching financial statements |
| Mismatch in gross receipts | Gross receipts in P&L differ from those in audit report |
| Incomplete Part B of return | Part B-TI or Part B-TTI not filled |
| No TAN or irregular TDS | TDS claimed but TAN of deductor not provided |
| Audit report not uploaded | Tax audit required but Form 3CA/3CB/3CD not uploaded |

## How the Defective Return Notice Is Issued

The notice under Section 139(9) is sent to your **registered email address** and is also available on the e-filing portal under **Pending Actions** or **e-Proceedings**. It specifies:

- The defect that has been identified
- The reference number of your original return
- The deadline to respond (15 days from the date of notice, extendable on request)

## Consequences of Not Responding

> [!WARN] Your return is treated as never filed
> - Return is treated as **invalid** — as if never filed
> - You are treated as a non-filer for that year, attracting penalty under Section 234F
> - Any refund due will not be processed
> - Carry-forward of losses (business, capital) is **denied**
> - TDS credit claimed in the return will not be processed

## Example: Karan, Freelance Developer — Audit Report Not Uploaded

> [!TIP] How it was resolved
> Karan filed ITR-4 for FY 2022-23 and declared gross receipts of Rs. 28 lakh under the presumptive scheme. However, since his turnover exceeded Rs. 1 crore in FY 2021-22, a tax audit was required — but he filed without uploading Form 3CB and 3CD.
> He received a defective return notice under Section 139(9). He immediately uploaded the audit report, **accepted the defect on the portal, and re-submitted** — and his return was treated as valid with the original filing date preserved.

## How to Respond to a Defective Return Notice

1. **Log in to the income tax e-filing portal** — incometax.gov.in
2. **Go to e-Proceedings** — locate the Section 139(9) notice
3. **Read the defect description carefully**
4. **Choose "Agree"** to correct the defect, or **"Disagree" with reasons** if you believe the notice is incorrect
5. **Make the necessary corrections** — upload a revised or corrected return
6. **Submit within the deadline** — request an extension if needed
7. **Download and preserve the submission acknowledgement**

## Prevention Tips

- Use the correct ITR form based on your income sources before filing
- Ensure all taxes are paid before pressing the Submit button on your return
- If your accounts are auditable, upload the audit report **before** filing the ITR
- Cross-check that gross receipts in your return match those in your audit report
- Do not leave any mandatory schedule blank, even if the amount is zero
