- Businesspersons with taxable profits
- Investors with substantial capital gains during the year

> [!NOTE] Senior citizens are exempt
> Senior citizens **above 60 years of age** who do not have business income are exempt from advance tax requirements.

## Advance Tax Payment Schedule

| Due Date | Minimum Cumulative Tax to Be Paid |
| --- | --- |
| By June 15 | At least 15% of estimated annual tax |
| By September 15 | At least 45% of estimated annual tax |
| By December 15 | At least 75% of estimated annual tax |
| By March 15 | At least 100% of estimated annual tax |

## How Is Section 234B Interest Calculated?

Interest is charged at **1% per month** (or part of a month) on the **shortfall** in advance tax. The shortfall is: 90% of assessed tax minus actual advance tax paid.

The interest runs from **April 1st of the assessment year** until the date of payment of tax or the date of assessment, whichever is earlier.

**Formula:** Interest = (Assessed Tax − TDS − Advance Tax Paid) × 1% × Number of Months

> [!WARN] Even a partial month counts as a full month
> If you pay on April 5, that counts as one full month. Delay another day to May 1 and it becomes two months. Pay early within each period.

## Example: Anand, Freelance Architect — Bengaluru

> [!TIP] What the interest worked out to
> Anand had a total tax liability of Rs. 3 lakh for FY 2023-24. TDS deducted: Rs. 50,000. Net advance tax required: Rs. 2.5 lakh.
> He paid Rs. 1.5 lakh by March 15 and the remaining Rs. 1 lakh when he filed his return in July 2024.
> Shortfall: Rs. 1 lakh | 234B Interest: 1% × Rs. 1 lakh × 4 months (April to July) = **Rs. 4,000**
> This is in addition to any Section 234C interest for late quarterly payments.

## Difference Between Sections 234A, 234B, and 234C

| Section | Triggered By | Interest Rate | Period |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 234A | Late filing of income tax return | 1% per month | From due date of filing to actual filing date |
| 234B | Shortfall in advance tax below 90% | 1% per month | April 1 of AY to date of payment or assessment |
| 234C | Short payment of advance tax in any instalment | 1% per month | 3 months each for first 3 instalments; 1 month for last |

## How to Respond and Rectify

1. **Pay the remaining tax** along with applicable interest before filing your return
2. **Use the self-assessment tax challan** (Challan 280) to pay the outstanding amount
3. **The ITR portal calculates 234B interest automatically** when you file — review it before submitting
4. **If interest has been wrongly computed** in an assessment order, file a rectification under Section 154

## Prevention Tips

- Estimate your total income at the beginning of the year, including expected capital gains
- Use the advance tax calculator on the income tax portal to plan quarterly payments
- If your income increases significantly mid-year due to a large capital gain or new contract, revise your estimate and pay accordingly
- Keep track of TDS deducted and reconcile with Form 26AS to ensure accurate advance tax computation
- If you are a senior citizen without business income, confirm your exemption status

## FAQs

### Can Section 234B interest be waived or reduced?

The Board has the power to waive or reduce interest in special circumstances, but this is rarely granted and requires a formal application. Paying the correct advance tax is the only reliable way to avoid this interest.

### Does Section 234B apply if all my income is from salary with TDS deducted?

If your employer deducts TDS correctly covering 90% or more of your total tax liability, Section 234B does not apply. However, if you have additional income from interest, capital gains, or freelance work on which TDS is not deducted, you need to pay advance tax on that portion.

### What if I paid advance tax on time but the AO later reassesses and increases my income?

In reassessment cases, interest under Section 234B runs from April 1 of the assessment year until the date of payment of the additional demand raised. The original advance tax paid is given credit.

### Does Section 234B apply to presumptive tax filers under Section 44AD?

Yes, but with a relaxation. Taxpayers under Section 44AD are required to pay the **entire advance tax by March 15 in a single instalment**. The graduated quarterly schedule does not apply to them.

### If I filed my return late and paid all taxes, do I pay both 234A and 234B interest?

Yes. Section 234A applies for late filing and Section 234B applies for shortfall in advance tax. They are independent charges and both apply simultaneously if applicable.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Children's Education Allowance: Tax Benefits for Parents
articles.excerpt: Complete guide to tax benefits for parents on education costs: Section 10(14) children education & hostel allowance, 80C tuition fees deduction, and Section 80E for education loan interest.
articles.content:
## Overview

The cost of educating children in India has risen sharply over the past decade. Recognising this, the government has incorporated multiple tax relief provisions for parents bearing education-related expenses. These benefits are available under different heads: **one as an exemption from salary income**, and **others as deductions** under the Chapter VI-A deductions framework.

Understanding the difference between an **exemption** (which reduces taxable salary directly) and a **deduction** (which reduces taxable income through investment or expense) is key to using both tools correctly.

## Applicable Provisions

| Provision | Section | Nature | Limit |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Children Education Allowance | Section 10(14) read with Rule 2BB | Exemption from salary | Rs. 100 per child per month |
| Children Hostel Allowance | Section 10(14) read with Rule 2BB | Exemption from salary | Rs. 300 per child per month |
| Tuition Fees Deduction | Section 80C | Deduction | Part of Rs. 1,50,000 80C limit |
| Interest on Education Loan | Section 80E | Deduction | Full interest (no limit) for 8 years |

## Children Education Allowance — Section 10(14)

This is a **salary exemption** available to employees who receive a children education allowance from their employer. It is not something you claim by investing; it flows from your salary structure.

### Limits

| Allowance Type | Per Child Per Month | Per Year Per Child | Max for 2 Children |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Children Education Allowance | Rs. 100 | Rs. 1,200 | Rs. 2,400 |
| Children Hostel Allowance | Rs. 300 | Rs. 3,600 | Rs. 7,200 |

> [!NOTE] Modest but worth claiming
> These limits have not been revised in many years and are small, but they are still worth claiming if your employer provides these allowances.

### Who qualifies

- Salaried employees receiving education or hostel allowance from employer
- Maximum benefit for **2 children** (additional children do not get further exemption)

## Tuition Fees Under Section 80C

This is a more meaningful benefit. The **actual tuition fees** paid to any school, college, or university in India for full-time education of up to two children are deductible under Section 80C, subject to the Rs. 1.5 lakh overall ceiling.

### Key conditions

- **Only tuition fees** count. Development fees, transport fees, library fees, and miscellaneous charges do not qualify.
- The institution must be **in India**.
- Fee must be for **full-time** education.
- Maximum for **2 children** only. If you have three children, fees for only two can be claimed.

In a family where both parents are taxpayers, each parent can claim fees for one child each, effectively covering fees for two children per parent (in theory), though practically, one parent's claiming is enough.

## Section 80E — Interest on Education Loan

If a child has taken an education loan (or if the parent has taken one for the child's higher education), the interest paid on that loan is **fully deductible under Section 80E with no upper limit** for up to 8 years starting from the year interest repayment begins.

| Feature | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Eligible borrowers | Individual (student, parent, spouse, or legal guardian) |
| Eligible education | Higher education in India or abroad (recognised institutions) |
| Duration | 8 consecutive assessment years |
| Deduction cap | **None** (full interest deductible) |

> [!IMPORTANT] 80E is separate from 80C
> Section 80E has its own unlimited deduction and does not interfere with the Rs. 1.5 lakh 80C ceiling. If your interest in Year 1 is Rs. 1,80,000, the entire amount is deductible.

## Example Calculation

Prakash has two children: one in Class 8 and one in an engineering college. He pays Rs. 60,000 per year as school tuition fees for the younger child and Rs. 1,20,000 as interest on an education loan taken for his older child's engineering course.

### 80C status

| Item | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| EPF | Rs. 30,000 |
| PPF | Rs. 60,000 |
| School tuition fees | Rs. 60,000 |
| **Total 80C** | **Rs. 1,50,000 (fully utilised)** |

### 80E deduction

| Item | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Education loan interest | Rs. 1,20,000 |
| Deduction under 80E (no cap) | Rs. 1,20,000 |
| Tax saved at 20% slab | **Rs. 24,000** |

The education allowance from his employer (if applicable) provides an additional nominal exemption on top of this.

## Optimization Tips

1. **Collect proper fee receipts** that specifically state "tuition fee" separately from other charges. A consolidated school fee receipt without itemisation may be questioned during scrutiny.
2. **Check if your employer offers education and hostel allowances** in your CTC structure. If not, ask HR whether they can restructure salary to include these components, even at the modest amounts allowed.
3. **Student-borrowers can claim 80E themselves.** If the education loan is large and in the student's name, the student (once employed) can claim the 80E deduction for up to 8 years. Parents who co-borrowed can also claim it proportionately.
4. **Plan which parent claims fees for which child.** Do not blindly combine two children's tuition fees under one parent's 80C if it conflicts with other investments already filling the ceiling.
5. **Donate strategically.** If total 80C is exceeded by tuition fees and other contributions combined, consider reducing other low-return 80C instruments rather than not claiming full tuition fees.

## Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Claiming hostel fees or transport fees under 80C as tuition.** Only pure tuition fees qualify.
2. **Claiming education allowance exemption for more than two children.** The law is clear: the exemption is limited to two children.
3. **Missing the education loan interest deduction entirely** because no one told you about it. This is remarkably common. First-generation college-goers, particularly from smaller towns, often repay education loans without knowing this benefit exists.
4. **Treating the education loan deduction as subject to the same 80C limit.** Section 80E is entirely separate from 80C.
5. **Assuming you cannot claim if the loan is in your child's name.** The parent or relative who repays an education loan (even if the original borrower is the student) can claim the 80E deduction.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. My child is studying abroad. Can I claim tuition fees under 80C?

No. Section 80C tuition fees are specifically for full-time education at institutions **in India**. Fees for foreign universities do not qualify under 80C.

### Q2. Can a single parent claim tuition fees for three children under 80C?

No. The law restricts the claim to a maximum of **two children**, regardless of how many children you have or your marital status.

### Q3. I repaid an education loan for my sibling. Can I claim 80E interest?

Section 80E is specifically available to an individual for a loan taken for their own higher education, or for the higher education of their **spouse, children, or a student for whom they are the legal guardian**. A sibling generally does not qualify unless you are the legal guardian.

### Q4. How many years can I claim 80E deduction for interest on an education loan?

You can claim for up to **8 consecutive financial years**, starting from the year in which you first start repaying the interest. If the loan is fully repaid before 8 years, the deduction stops at that point.

### Q5. Does the education loan need to be from a bank to qualify for 80E?

The loan must be from a **financial institution (bank or approved charitable institution)** to qualify. Loans from family members or informal sources do not qualify under Section 80E.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Income Tax Customer Care Number: Helpline, Toll Free Number & Email ID
articles.excerpt: Navigating tax compliance often requires direct interaction with the Income Tax Department—whether for return filing issues, refund delays, or notice clarification. To streamline this, the department provides centralized support systems,…
articles.content:
Navigating tax compliance often requires direct interaction with the Income Tax Department—whether for return filing issues, refund delays, or notice clarification. To streamline this, the department provides centralized support systems, including helplines and email-based assistance, integrated with its digital infrastructure under the Income Tax Act, 1961.

## Why the Helpline Matters

With most tax processes now digitized, support queries typically relate to:

- ITR filing errors
- Login or portal access issues
- Refund status or delays
- PAN and Aadhaar linkage
- Notices and compliance requirements

The customer care system acts as a first-level resolution mechanism, reducing the need for physical visits.

## Key Helpline Numbers

The Income Tax Department provides multiple contact numbers based on the type of query:

- e-Filing Helpline: 1800-103-0025 / 1800-419-0025
- PAN/TAN Helpline: 020-27218080
- TIN (NSDL) Services: 020-27218080

These numbers are typically available during working hours, with extended support during peak filing seasons. Email Support For written queries or documentation-related issues, email support is available:

- e-Filing Support: efilingwebmanager@incometax.gov.in Email communication is useful for:
- Detailed issue descriptions
- Attaching supporting documents
- Tracking responses in a structured manner Types of Issues Handled

The support system covers a broad range of queries, including:

- Errors during ITR submission
- Problems with digital signature or OTP verification
- Refund reissue or bank account validation
- Rectification requests
- AIS/Form 26AS discrepancies

For more complex matters, escalation may be required.

## How to Use the Helpline Effectively

### Before contacting support, it is advisable to

- Keep PAN and registered mobile number ready
- Note down error messages or reference IDs
- Ensure login credentials are accessible
- Clearly define the issue

This helps in faster resolution and avoids repeated follow-ups. Alternative Support Channels Apart from helplines, the department offers:

- e-Filing portal helpdesk (ticket-based support)
- FAQs and guides available online
- Grievance redressal through CPGRAMS

These channels are particularly useful for non-urgent or documentation-heavy queries.

## Practical Context

During peak filing periods, such as July deadlines, helplines may experience high call volumes. In such cases, email or portal-based queries often provide more reliable tracking. For refund-related issues, verification through Form 26AS and bank details should be completed before raising a query.

## Conclusion

The Income Tax Department’s customer care system is an integral part of the digital tax ecosystem, providing accessible support for a wide range of compliance issues. While most queries can be resolved through these channels, effective use depends on clarity of information and proper documentation. Leveraging these resources ensures smoother interaction with the tax system and supports compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Debt Mutual Fund Taxation \u2014 The Post-2023 Landscape
articles.excerpt: Complete guide to debt mutual fund taxation post the April 2023 change: affected fund types, pre/post purchase treatment, tax-efficient alternatives like arbitrage funds, and real calculation examples.
articles.content:
## Overview

Debt mutual funds were once the poster child of tax-efficient investing for individuals in higher income brackets. The combination of indexation benefit and a 20% LTCG rate after 3 years meant that a 7% pre-tax return from a debt fund easily outpaced a 7% bank FD on a post-tax basis for anyone in the 30% bracket. **That advantage is gone for new investments.**

The Finance Act 2023 fundamentally altered the taxation of debt mutual funds effective April 1, 2023. For units purchased on or after that date, all gains, regardless of holding period, are taxed at the investor's slab rate. The distinction between STCG and LTCG no longer applies to these funds for tax computation purposes.

## The Pre vs Post April 2023 Tax Split

| Category | Units Purchased Before April 1, 2023 | Units Purchased April 1, 2023 Onwards |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Holding less than 3 years | STCG at slab rate | Slab rate (same) |
| Holding more than 3 years | 20% LTCG with indexation | Slab rate (no LTCG benefit) |
| Dividend (IDCW) payout | Slab rate at receipt | Slab rate at receipt |
| TDS on dividend | 10% if over Rs. 5,000 | 10% if over Rs. 5,000 |

## Which Funds Are Affected?

| Fund Type | Affected by April 2023 Change? |
| --- | --- |
| Liquid Funds | Yes |
| Overnight Funds | Yes |
| Ultra Short Duration Funds | Yes |
| Short Duration / Medium Duration Funds | Yes |
| Corporate Bond Funds | Yes |
| Gilt Funds | Yes |
| Dynamic Bond Funds | Yes |
| Conservative Hybrid Funds (equity less than 65%) | Yes |
| Gold ETFs / Gold Fund of Funds | Yes |
| International Fund of Funds | Yes |
| Equity Funds (equity 65% and above) | No (old rules apply) |
| Aggressive Hybrid Funds | No |

## Calculation Example

**Scenario:** Same fund, two investors with different purchase dates.

A corporate bond fund currently shows a return of 7.5% per annum.

### Investor A — Invested March 2020, redeems March 2025 (5 years)

Old rules apply. Held more than 3 years → LTCG with indexation.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Simple gain | ~Rs. 4,35,000 |
| Indexed cost (~25% inflation adjustment) | Reduces gain to ~Rs. 2,85,000 |
| Tax at 20% + cess | Rs. 59,280 |
| **Post-tax gain** | **Rs. 3,75,720** |

### Investor B — Invested May 2023, redeems May 2028 (5 years)

New rules apply. Slab rate regardless of holding period.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Simple gain | ~Rs. 4,35,000 |
| Tax at 30% + cess | Rs. 1,35,720 |
| **Post-tax gain** | **Rs. 2,99,280** |

> [!IMPORTANT] The gap is meaningful
> Difference in post-tax wealth after 5 years: **Rs. 76,440**. The old rules deliver meaningfully better outcomes for those holding pre-April 2023 units.

## Alternatives That Still Offer Tax Efficiency

| Alternative | Tax Treatment | Key Consideration |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Arbitrage Funds | Equity taxation (12.5% LTCG after 12 months) | Returns similar to liquid funds; highly tax-efficient for 30% bracket |
| Equity Savings Funds | Equity taxation | Mix of equity, arbitrage, and debt; moderate risk |
| Bank Fixed Deposits | Slab rate | Now on equal footing with debt funds; simpler |
| RBI Floating Rate Savings Bonds | Slab rate | Sovereign safety; 7–8% returns |
| PPF | Exempt-Exempt-Exempt | 15-year lock-in; not suitable for all needs |
| REITs / InvITs | Mixed (capital gains + interest) | For sophisticated investors; complex taxation |

## Exemptions

There are no special exemptions on debt mutual fund gains for post-April 2023 purchases. The gains are taxed as income and the investor's basic exemption limit is the only relief. For pre-April 2023 units, the Section 54EC exemption is technically available if the gain qualifies as LTCG after 3 years.

## Strategy Tips

1. **Hold pre-April 2023 units for the full 3 years before redeeming.** If you have debt fund units from before April 2023, do not redeem them prematurely. The indexation benefit on a 4 or 5-year holding can be substantial.
2. **Shift new surplus to arbitrage funds if you are in the 30% bracket.** For high-income investors needing quasi-debt returns with high liquidity, arbitrage funds now offer a clear structural advantage. The tax difference can be 15 to 17 percentage points on gains.
3. **Use the IDCW option only if you need periodic cash flow.** Under the growth option, tax is deferred to the year of redemption. This is generally more efficient than receiving taxable dividends annually.
4. **Do not churn debt funds unnecessarily.** Even post-April 2023, transaction costs and tax on each redemption compound negatively when you switch frequently. Align investments with your actual holding horizon.
5. **Evaluate FD vs debt fund on a post-tax, post-liquidity basis.** Debt funds still offer better liquidity than FDs (no premature withdrawal penalty, no tax on interest unless redeemed). Even the same post-tax return with better liquidity makes debt funds worthwhile.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. I switched from one debt fund to another within the same AMC in February 2023. Does the April 2023 change affect the new units?

Yes. When you switched, the old units were redeemed and new units were created in the target fund. The date of the switch determines whether the new units fall under the old or new tax regime. Units created through a switch in February 2023 (before April 1, 2023) are governed by the old rules.

### Q2. Are gilt funds also affected by the April 2023 change?

Yes. Gilt funds are classified as debt-oriented funds with less than 65% equity, so they are fully affected. Gains from gilt fund units purchased after April 1, 2023 are taxed at slab rates regardless of how long you hold them.

### Q3. If I receive a dividend from a debt mutual fund, is it taxed differently than if I redeem and take growth gains?

Both are taxed at your slab rate now for post-April 2023 units. However, dividends are taxed in the year of receipt (with TDS applicable), whereas growth-option gains are taxed only upon redemption, giving you more control over timing. Growth is generally the better option for tax deferral.

### Q4. Can I use Systematic Withdrawal Plans (SWPs) from debt funds to reduce my tax burden?

For post-April 2023 units, each SWP instalment triggers taxation at slab rate on the gain portion. For pre-April 2023 units, SWPs after 3 years still benefit from LTCG with indexation, making them more efficient.

### Q5. My debt fund shows a negative NAV movement due to interest rate hikes. If I book this loss, what can I offset it against?

Capital losses from debt fund redemptions can be set off against capital gains from other assets. STCL (held less than 3 years under old rules, or any holding for new units) can be set off against both STCG and LTCG from any source. LTCL (from pre-April 2023 units held more than 3 years) can only be set off against LTCG. Carry forward for up to 8 assessment years is allowed.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 87A Rebate — How to Pay Zero Tax on Income Up to Rs. 12 Lakh
articles.excerpt: Section 87A makes income tax zero for resident individuals with taxable income up to Rs. 12 lakh under the new regime. This guide explains the rebate mechanism, the marginal relief cliff, what income types are excluded (LTCG, STCG, lottery), and the salaried Rs. 12.75 lakh zero-tax threshold.
articles.content:
## Introduction

If you have heard that individuals earning up to Rs. 12 lakh pay no income tax in India under the new regime, **Section 87A** of the Income Tax Act is the provision that makes it possible. Yet a surprising number of salaried individuals do not know how this rebate works, what its exact limits are, whether it applies to all types of income, and when it does not apply despite income being below the threshold.

Understanding Section 87A completely prevents missed tax savings and avoids the unpleasant surprise of receiving a tax demand you thought you were exempt from.

## What Is Section 87A?

Section 87A provides a **rebate** (not a deduction) from the income tax payable by a resident individual, provided their total income does not exceed a specified threshold. Unlike a deduction (which reduces taxable income), a rebate **directly reduces the tax payable** — making it far more powerful dollar-for-dollar.

| Aspect | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Applicable to | Resident individuals only (not NRIs, HUFs, firms, companies) |
| Income threshold — new regime, FY 2025-26 | Up to Rs. 12,00,000 (total income after deductions) |
| Income threshold — old regime, FY 2025-26 | Up to Rs. 5,00,000 (total income) |
| Rebate amount — new regime | Equal to income tax payable (i.e., full rebate) |
| Rebate amount — old regime | Up to Rs. 12,500 |
| Result | Effective zero tax for qualifying individuals |

## How Section 87A Works — The Mechanism

The rebate is applied after computing income tax on taxable income but **before** adding cess:

1. **Compute gross total income**
2. **Apply deductions** (if old regime) or standard deduction
3. **Arrive at net taxable income**
4. **Compute income tax** at applicable slab rates
5. **Check if income is within 87A threshold**
6. If yes: **apply rebate** (reduce tax to zero or by Rs. 12,500)
7. **Add cess @ 4%** on remaining tax (cess on zero = zero)
8. **Final tax payable = Rs. 0** (if full rebate applies)

## Section 87A Under the New Regime (FY 2025-26)

| Total Income (After Standard Deduction) | Tax Before Rebate | Rebate | Tax After Rebate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Rs. 7,00,000 | Rs. 15,000 | Rs. 15,000 | Rs. 0 |
| Rs. 9,00,000 | Rs. 45,000 | Rs. 45,000 | Rs. 0 |
| Rs. 11,00,000 | Rs. 75,000 | Rs. 75,000 | Rs. 0 |
| Rs. 12,00,000 | Rs. 80,000 | Rs. 80,000 | Rs. 0 |
| Rs. 12,10,000 | Rs. 81,500 | Rs. 0 (exceeds threshold) | Rs. 81,500 + cess |

> [!WARN] The cliff effect at Rs. 12 lakh
> Moving from Rs. 12 lakh to Rs. 12,10,000 of taxable income suddenly results in a **Rs. 81,500 tax bill**. Marginal relief (see below) softens this, but be aware of the cliff if your income hovers near the threshold.

## Marginal Relief: Preventing a Disproportionate Tax Jump

To address the harsh cliff at Rs. 12 lakh, marginal relief ensures that **tax payable does not exceed the income exceeding Rs. 12 lakh**.

**Example — Taxable income of Rs. 12,10,000:**
- Normal tax (no rebate): Rs. 81,500 + cess
- Income exceeding Rs. 12 lakh: Rs. 10,000
- With marginal relief: Tax capped at Rs. 10,000 + 4% cess = **Rs. 10,400**

This relief tapers off as income rises further above Rs. 12 lakh.

## Section 87A Under the Old Regime

| Total Income (Old Regime) | Tax Before Rebate | Rebate | Tax Payable |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Rs. 3,00,000 | Rs. 2,500 | Rs. 2,500 | Rs. 0 |
| Rs. 4,00,000 | Rs. 7,500 | Rs. 7,500 | Rs. 0 |
| Rs. 5,00,000 | Rs. 12,500 | Rs. 12,500 | Rs. 0 |
| Rs. 5,50,000 | Rs. 22,500 | Rs. 0 (exceeds threshold) | Rs. 22,500 + cess |

## What Income Is Excluded From the 87A Rebate?

This is the most critical — and most often misunderstood — aspect of Section 87A. The rebate does **not** apply to income taxed at special flat rates:

| Type of Income | Flat Rate | Eligible for 87A Rebate? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Long-term capital gains on equity (Section 112A) | 12.5% | No |
| Short-term capital gains on equity (Section 111A) | 20% | No |
| Lottery / gambling income (Section 115BB) | 30% | No |
| Online gaming winnings (Section 115BBJ) | 30% | No |
| All other income taxed at slab rates | Slab | **Yes** |

> [!IMPORTANT] Special-rate income is excluded even if total income is below Rs. 12 lakh
> **Example:** Suresh has Rs. 8 lakh salary (taxable) + Rs. 2.5 lakh LTCG from equity MFs. Total income Rs. 10.5 lakh (below Rs. 12L).
> Tax on salary: Rs. 30,000 → **fully rebated to zero** under 87A.
> LTCG tax: 12.5% on Rs. 1.25 lakh (above the Rs. 1.25L LTCG exemption) = Rs. 15,625 → **no rebate applies**.
> Cess on Rs. 15,625: Rs. 625. **Total tax payable: Rs. 16,250.**

## Practical Example: Pooja at Rs. 12.75 Lakh Gross Salary

| Computation | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Gross salary | Rs. 12,75,000 |
| Less: Standard deduction (new regime) | Rs. 75,000 |
| Taxable income | Rs. 12,00,000 |
| Tax at new regime slabs on Rs. 12 lakh | Rs. 80,000 |
| Less: 87A rebate | Rs. 80,000 |
| Net tax | Rs. 0 |
| Health and education cess | Rs. 0 |
| **Total Tax Payable** | **Rs. 0** |

Pooja pays **zero income tax on a Rs. 12.75 lakh gross salary** — one of the most significant benefits introduced in Budget 2024, still in force for FY 2025-26.

## Who Is NOT Eligible for Section 87A?

| Category | Reason |
| --- | --- |
| NRIs (Non-Resident Indians) | Section 87A is for resident individuals only |
| HUFs (Hindu Undivided Families) | Not an individual |
| Companies and firms | Not an individual |
| Resident individuals above Rs. 12 lakh income (new regime) | Above threshold |
| Resident individuals above Rs. 5 lakh income (old regime) | Above threshold |
| Special-rate income (STCG, LTCG, lottery) | Income type excluded |

> [!NOTE] The rebate is applied automatically
> Section 87A is applied automatically by the ITR filing system once you enter your income correctly and select the applicable regime. However, the system does not always handle LTCG and STCG exclusions correctly in all scenarios — **cross-verify your tax computation sheet before submitting**.

## FAQs

### Q1. I earned Rs. 11.5 lakh in salary and Rs. 80,000 in FD interest. Does 87A apply?

Gross salary Rs. 11.5L − Rs. 75,000 standard deduction = Rs. 10.75L + FD interest Rs. 80,000 = **total income Rs. 11.55 lakh**. Since Rs. 11.55 lakh is below Rs. 12 lakh, the full 87A rebate applies and **your tax is zero**.

### Q2. If 87A makes my tax zero, does my employer still need to deduct TDS?

Your employer must deduct TDS based on the projected full-year tax computation. If the full-year calculation shows zero tax after the rebate, the employer should deduct zero TDS. Submit your investment declarations to your employer's payroll team at the start of the year to ensure TDS is computed correctly from the beginning.

### Q3. I am 75 years old with Rs. 4 lakh pension income. Do I get the 87A rebate?

Under the old regime, citizens aged 60-80 have a basic exemption of Rs. 3 lakh, and the 87A rebate of Rs. 12,500 covers any tax up to Rs. 5 lakh income. Under the new regime, the basic exemption is Rs. 3 lakh for all ages, but the 87A rebate covers full tax up to Rs. 12 lakh income — effectively making Rs. 4 lakh of pension tax-free under either regime.

### Q4. I sold my ancestral residential property and made an LTCG of Rs. 8 lakh. My total income is Rs. 9 lakh. Is the LTCG rebatable?

LTCG on immovable property is taxed at 12.5% under Section 112 (without indexation, post July 2024). Whether 87A applies to LTCG on property is an area of ongoing interpretation — since it is taxed at a special flat rate, the rebate may not apply to this portion. **Consult a CA for your specific computation.**

### Q5. My income is exactly Rs. 12 lakh but the portal shows a small tax even after the rebate. Is that an error?

This typically happens when a small amount of income — such as interest on an income tax refund — pushes your total income marginally above Rs. 12 lakh, or when the system computes cess before fully applying the rebate. Review the tax computation sheet carefully. If total income is exactly Rs. 12 lakh or below and all income is at slab rates, the rebate should reduce tax to exactly zero. Raise a feedback or grievance on the portal if the error persists after correctly entering all income details.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How To Save Tax For Salary Above 30 Lakhs?
articles.excerpt: At income levels above ₹30 lakhs, taxpayers are firmly positioned in the highest tax bracket, where a large portion of income is taxed at 30% plus surcharge and cess under the Income Tax Act, 1961. At this stage, tax planning is no longer…
articles.content:
At income levels above ₹30 lakhs, taxpayers are firmly positioned in the highest tax bracket, where a large portion of income is taxed at 30% plus surcharge and cess under the Income Tax Act, 1961. At this stage, tax planning is no longer about basic deductions—it requires structured optimization, strategic allocation, and careful timing of income and expenses. The focus shifts from “saving tax” to managing taxable exposure efficiently within legal limits.

Why Planning Changes at ₹30 Lakhs Beyond ₹30 lakhs:

- Surcharge becomes applicable, increasing effective tax rate
- Standard deductions alone have limited impact
- Errors or missed deductions result in significant tax leakage

The margin for inefficiency becomes more expensive.

## What Actually Works at This Level

Tax reduction here depends on combining multiple small optimizations rather than relying on a single large deduction.

## Maximize All Core Deductions First

Before exploring advanced strategies, ensure full utilization of available deductions:

- Section 80C – ₹1.5 lakh (EPF, PPF, ELSS, home loan principal)
- Section 80D – Health insurance (self + parents)
- Standard deduction
- Section 80CCD(1B) – ₹50,000 (NPS additional benefit)

At higher incomes, even these basic deductions contribute meaningful savings.

## Use Employer-Linked Benefits

### Salary structuring becomes critical

- HRA exemption (if living on rent)
- LTA claims
- Employer contribution to NPS (Section 80CCD(2))
- Reimbursements structured within policy

These components reduce taxable salary without additional investment.

## Leverage Home Loan Structuring

Home loans provide one of the most consistent tax advantages:

- Interest deduction up to ₹2 lakh
- Principal under Section 80C

For let-out properties, interest deduction can be higher (subject to set-off limits).

## Plan Capital Gains Efficiently

At this income level, capital gains planning becomes important:

- Utilize ₹1 lakh LTCG exemption on equity annually
- Offset gains with carried-forward losses
- Avoid unnecessary short-term gains taxed at higher rates

Strategic timing of asset sales can reduce tax impact.

## Evaluate Old vs New Tax Regime Carefully

### At ₹30+ lakh income

- Old regime works better if deductions are substantial
- New regime may be beneficial if income is mostly fixed salary with minimal deductions

A side-by-side comparison is essential every year.

## Consider Income Structuring (Where Applicable)

### For individuals with variable income

- Split income across family members (where legally valid)
- Use joint ownership structures for investments
- Allocate income to lower tax brackets within the family

This must be done carefully to avoid clubbing provisions. Step-by-Step Planning Approach

1. **Compute total annual income**
2. **Apply all eligible deductions and exemptions**
3. **Structure salary components efficiently**
4. **Optimize investment allocations**
5. **Plan capital gains timing**
6. **Compare both tax regimes**

## Adjust advance tax or TDS accordingly

## Key Considerations

- Surcharge increases effective tax rate beyond 30%
- Not all deductions are available under the new regime
- Documentation and compliance are critical
- Tax planning should align with long-term financial goals Practical Insight

At this level, tax planning is about precision, not shortcuts. There are limited avenues to drastically reduce tax, but proper structuring can still lead to meaningful savings annually. Ignoring planning often results in avoidable tax outflow, especially due to missed deductions or poor income timing.

## Conclusion

For salaries above ₹30 lakhs, effective tax management requires a holistic approach combining deductions, structuring, and timing. While the tax rate remains high, disciplined planning ensures that liability is optimized within the framework of the Income Tax Act, 1961. The emphasis should remain on compliance-driven efficiency, rather than aggressive tax minimization.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How To Save Tax For Salary Above 1 Crore?
articles.excerpt: At income levels exceeding ₹1 crore, taxation enters a different zone altogether. Apart from the base rate of 30%, surcharge slabs significantly increase the effective tax rate, making structured planning essential under the Income Tax…
articles.content:
At income levels exceeding ₹1 crore, taxation enters a different zone altogether. Apart from the base rate of 30%, surcharge slabs significantly increase the effective tax rate, making structured planning essential under the Income Tax Act, 1961. At this level, tax saving is not about finding new deductions—it is about optimizing structure, timing, and classification of income while remaining fully compliant.

## Why Tax Planning Becomes More Complex

### For high-income individuals

- Surcharge can go up to 25% or more, increasing effective tax burden
- A large portion of income is taxed at the highest marginal rate
- Standard deductions have limited relative impact
- Errors in structuring can lead to substantial additional tax outflow

Even marginal improvements in planning can result in significant absolute savings.

## What Actually Makes a Difference

At this level, tax efficiency depends on layered planning, combining multiple strategies rather than relying on isolated deductions.

## Fully Utilize Available Deductions (Baseline Layer)

Even at ₹1 crore income, foundational deductions remain relevant:

- Section 80C – ₹1.5 lakh
- Section 80D – Health insurance
- Section 80CCD(1B) – ₹50,000 (NPS)
- Standard deduction

While these may seem small relative to income, they still reduce taxable exposure.

## Employer Contribution to NPS (High-Impact Tool)

### One of the most effective strategies at this level

- Employer contribution up to 10% of salary (14% for certain employees)
- Deduction available under Section 80CCD(2)
- Not counted within 80C limits

This becomes a meaningful tax-saving lever for high-income salaried individuals.

## Salary Structuring and Compensation Design

At senior compensation levels, structuring plays a major role:

- Optimizing HRA vs company-leased accommodation
- Use of reimbursements and allowances
- Performance bonuses structured with tax implications in mind

These decisions are often made at the compensation negotiation stage.

## Capital Gains Optimization

A significant portion of income at this level may come from investments:

- Use ₹1 lakh LTCG exemption on equity annually
- Prefer long-term holding over short-term transactions
- Offset gains with carried-forward losses
- Plan exit timing to avoid bunching of gains in a single year

## Income Diversification and Structuring

### Where applicable and legally valid

- Use family-based investment structures
- Allocate investments in names of family members in lower tax brackets
- Consider joint ownership of assets

Care must be taken to comply with clubbing provisions.

## Old vs New Tax Regime – Strategic Choice

### At ₹1 crore+

- Old regime works if structured deductions and benefits are substantial
- New regime may still be efficient if income is largely fixed and unstructured

A detailed comparison is necessary—there is no one-size-fits-all answer.

## Advance Tax Planning

### High-income individuals must

- Estimate income accurately during the year
- Pay advance tax in installments
- Avoid interest under Sections 234B and 234C

Cash flow planning becomes as important as tax calculation.

## Practical Interpretation

At this level, tax planning shifts from reactive to proactive structuring:

- Decisions taken at the beginning of the year matter more than year-end adjustments
- Compensation design, investment timing, and income classification drive outcomes
- The goal is efficiency, not avoidance Common Real-World Pattern

A senior executive earning ₹1.2 crore typically reduces tax liability through:

- Employer NPS contribution
- Optimized salary structure
- Full utilization of deductions
- Planned capital gains realization

Even then, a substantial portion remains taxable—planning ensures it is not higher than necessary.

## Conclusion

For salaries above ₹1 crore, tax efficiency depends on discipline, structure, and timing rather than additional deductions. The system offers limited avenues, but when used correctly, they can significantly influence final tax liability. A well-planned approach aligned with the provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961 ensures that taxation remains optimized, predictable, and compliant.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How to Save Tax for Salary Above 7 Lakhs?
articles.excerpt: For individuals earning above ₹7 lakhs, tax planning begins to depend heavily on the choice of tax regime and effective use of available deductions under the Income Tax Act, 1961. This income level is particularly important because it…
articles.content:
For individuals earning above ₹7 lakhs, tax planning begins to depend heavily on the choice of tax regime and effective use of available deductions under the Income Tax Act, 1961. This income level is particularly important because it sits around the rebate threshold under Section 87A (new regime), making even small planning decisions impactful. The objective here is to either reduce taxable income to ₹7 lakh or optimize tax beyond that level.

Why ₹7 Lakhs is a Key Threshold Under the new tax regime:

- Income up to ₹7,00,000 results in zero tax due to rebate
- Even a small increase above ₹7 lakh leads to tax liability on the entire applicable slab portion

This creates a planning opportunity to manage taxable income around this threshold.

## What Needs to Be Focused On

### At this level, tax planning revolves around

- Reducing taxable income below ₹7 lakh (if possible)
- Or optimizing deductions under the old regime

The strategy depends on income composition and flexibility. Strategy 1: Bring Taxable Income Below ₹7 Lakhs If income is slightly above ₹7 lakh, reducing it can eliminate tax completely. This can be done through:

- Standard deduction
- NPS contribution (₹50,000 under Section 80CCD(1B))
- Health insurance (Section 80D)
- Other eligible deductions (if using old regime)

Even small adjustments can make a disproportionate difference at this level. Strategy 2: Choose the Right Tax Regime New Regime

- Lower tax rates
- Limited deductions
- Best suited if income structure is simple Old Regime
- Allows deductions like 80C, 80D, HRA
- Useful if total deductions are significant

A comparison is essential before deciding. Strategy 3: Use Section 80C Efficiently

- Limit: ₹1.5 lakh
- Includes EPF, PPF, ELSS, insurance, home loan principal

This remains one of the most effective ways to reduce taxable income under the old regime. Strategy 4: Health Insurance (Section 80D)

- Up to ₹25,000 (self/family)
- Additional for parents

Provides both financial protection and tax benefit. Strategy 5: NPS Contribution

- Additional deduction of ₹50,000 beyond 80C
- Particularly useful for crossing below ₹7 lakh threshold Step-by-Step Approach

1. **Compute total annual income**
2. **Apply standard deduction**
3. **Identify possible deductions (80C, 80D, NPS)**
4. **Check if income can be reduced to ₹7 lakh**
5. **Compare tax under both regimes**
6. **Choose the more efficient option**
7. **Adjust TDS or investments accordingly** — Practical Insight At this income level, tax planning is highly sensitive to small changes. A deduction of even

₹50,000 can shift the taxpayer from paying tax to zero tax liability under the new regime. This makes timing of investments and declaration of deductions particularly important.

## Conclusion

For salaries above ₹7 lakhs, tax optimization depends on understanding the ₹7 lakh rebate threshold and aligning deductions accordingly. The choice between old and new regimes plays a decisive role.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How to Save Tax for Salary Above 12 Lakhs?
articles.excerpt: For individuals earning above ₹12 lakhs, tax planning requires a more structured approach. A significant portion of income falls into higher tax slabs, making it important to use available deductions, allowances, and planning strategies…
articles.content:
For individuals earning above ₹12 lakhs, tax planning requires a more structured approach. A significant portion of income falls into higher tax slabs, making it important to use available deductions, allowances, and planning strategies effectively under the Income Tax Act, 1961. At this level, the goal is not to eliminate tax entirely but to reduce taxable income in a disciplined and consistent manner.

Why Planning Matters at ₹12 Lakhs At this income range:

- Income moves into the 20% and 30% tax slabs
- Basic deductions alone may not be sufficient
- Missed deductions can lead to noticeable tax outflow differences

Even moderate optimization can lead to meaningful savings. Start with the Right Tax Regime The first decision is choosing between: Old Tax Regime

- Allows deductions like Section 80C, 80D, HRA
- Suitable if you actively invest and claim benefits New Tax Regime
- Lower slab rates
- Limited deductions
- Suitable if income structure is simple

A comparison should be done based on actual numbers before finalizing. Use Section 80C Completely

- Maximum deduction of ₹1.5 lakh
- Includes EPF, PPF, ELSS, life insurance, home loan principal

At this income level, not utilizing the full limit leads to avoidable tax. Health Insurance Deduction Under Section 80D:

- Up to ₹25,000 for self and family
- Additional benefit for parents

This deduction serves both financial protection and tax efficiency. National Pension System (NPS)

- Additional deduction of ₹50,000 under Section 80CCD(1B)
- Over and above Section 80C

This is one of the few ways to extend deductions beyond the standard limits. Salary Structuring Opportunities If your employer allows flexibility, consider:

- HRA exemption if living on rent
- LTA claims where applicable
- Reimbursements structured within company policy

These reduce taxable salary without additional investment. Home Loan Benefits If applicable:

- Interest deduction up to ₹2 lakh
- Principal repayment under Section 80C

Housing loans provide combined tax benefits and long-term asset creation. Step-by-Step Planning Approach

1. **Calculate total income from all sources**
2. **Apply standard deduction**
3. **Identify deductions under 80C, 80D, and NPS**
4. **Evaluate HRA and other allowances**
5. **Check home loan benefits if applicable**
6. **Compare tax under both regimes**
7. **Finalize and adjust TDS or investments** — Practical Insight At ₹12 lakh income, tax planning is about consistent use of available provisions rather

than complex structuring. The difference between planned and unplanned tax outcomes can be substantial over time. A disciplined approach also ensures smoother compliance and avoids last-minute adjustments.

## Conclusion

For salaries above ₹12 lakhs, tax efficiency depends on combining deductions, structuring salary components, and choosing the appropriate tax regime. While the scope for large reductions is limited, proper planning ensures that tax liability remains optimized. Applying these strategies within the framework of the Income Tax Act, 1961 leads to consistent and compliant tax outcomes.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How To Save Tax For Salary Above 50 Lakhs?
articles.excerpt: At income levels above ₹50 lakhs, taxation is significantly impacted by surcharge, which increases the effective tax rate beyond the base 30% under the Income Tax Act, 1961. This makes tax planning more sensitive, as even small…
articles.content:
At income levels above ₹50 lakhs, taxation is significantly impacted by surcharge, which increases the effective tax rate beyond the base 30% under the Income Tax Act, 1961. This makes tax planning more sensitive, as even small inefficiencies can result in large absolute tax outflows. At this stage, tax optimization is driven by structure, timing, and disciplined use of provisions, rather than relying on basic deductions alone.

Why ₹50 Lakhs Changes the Equation Once income crosses ₹50 lakhs:

- Surcharge becomes applicable, increasing total tax liability
- Effective tax rate rises noticeably above 30%
- Limited deductions mean fewer opportunities for large reductions

This makes it important to ensure that no available benefit is left unused.

## What Actually Helps at This Level

Tax planning here is layered. No single strategy creates a large impact, but a combination of structured decisions does.

## Fully Utilize All Available Deductions

### Even at high income, baseline deductions remain relevant

- Section 80C up to ₹1.5 lakh
- Section 80D for health insurance
- Section 80CCD(1B) additional ₹50,000 for NPS
- Standard deduction

These form the foundation of tax reduction.

## Employer Contribution to NPS

### One of the most effective tools at this level

- Deduction under Section 80CCD(2)
- Up to 10% of salary contributed by employer
- Not part of 80C limit

This can create meaningful tax savings for high-income salaried individuals.

## Salary Structuring

### Compensation design plays a critical role

- Optimizing HRA or company-leased accommodation
- Using structured reimbursements
- Managing bonus payouts and timing

These decisions influence taxable salary directly.

## Home Loan Planning

### If applicable

- Interest deduction up to ₹2 lakh
- Principal repayment under Section 80C

For let-out properties, interest treatment may differ within set-off limits.

## Capital Gains Strategy

### Investment income often becomes significant at this level

- Utilize ₹1 lakh LTCG exemption on equity
- Offset gains with losses
- Avoid frequent short-term trading taxed at higher rates
- Spread gains across financial years where possible

## Regime Selection

### Choosing between tax regimes remains important

- Old regime works if deductions and structuring are strong
- New regime may suit simplified income structures

A detailed comparison is necessary every year.

## Advance Tax Discipline

### High-income individuals must

- Estimate income accurately
- Pay advance tax in installments
- Avoid interest under Sections 234B and 234C

Cash flow planning becomes closely linked to tax planning.

## Practical Insight

At ₹50 lakhs and above, tax planning is about efficiency rather than reduction. There are limited avenues to significantly lower tax, but proper structuring ensures that no excess tax is paid. Ignoring planning at this level can lead to avoidable tax leakage, especially due to missed deductions or poor timing of income.

## Conclusion

For salaries above ₹50 lakhs, effective tax management depends on combining deductions, optimizing compensation structure, and planning investment income carefully. While the tax rate remains high, disciplined planning ensures it is not higher than necessary. A structured approach aligned with the provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961 ensures both compliance and efficiency in managing tax liability.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How To Save Tax For Salary Above 15 Lakhs?
articles.excerpt: For individuals earning above ₹15 lakhs, a substantial portion of income falls into the highest tax slab. At this level, tax planning is about structured use of deductions, correct regime selection, and efficient salary design under the…
articles.content:
For individuals earning above ₹15 lakhs, a substantial portion of income falls into the highest tax slab. At this level, tax planning is about structured use of deductions, correct regime selection, and efficient salary design under the Income Tax Act, 1961. Unlike lower income brackets, the focus here is not on eliminating tax but on optimizing taxable income and avoiding unnecessary outflow.

Why Planning Matters at ₹15 Lakhs At this income range:

- Income enters the 30% tax slab
- Basic deductions alone have limited impact
- Inefficient planning leads to noticeable tax differences

A disciplined approach ensures that available benefits are fully utilized. Choosing the Right Tax Regime This is the first and most important decision. Old Tax Regime

- Allows deductions such as Section 80C, 80D, HRA
- Suitable if you actively claim benefits New Tax Regime
- Lower tax rates
- Limited deductions
- Suitable for simplified income structures

At ₹15 lakhs, the old regime often works better if deductions are substantial, but this must be evaluated case by case. Core Deductions to Use Section 80C

- Up to ₹1.5 lakh
- Includes EPF, PPF, ELSS, insurance, home loan principal Section 80D
- Health insurance for self and family
- Additional benefit for parents

NPS (Section 80CCD(1B))

- Additional ₹50,000 deduction beyond 80C

These form the foundation of tax planning. Salary Structuring If flexibility is available:

- Claim HRA exemption where applicable
- Use LTA benefits
- Structure reimbursements within employer policies

These reduce taxable salary without requiring additional investment. Home Loan Benefits If applicable:

- Interest deduction up to ₹2 lakh
- Principal repayment under Section 80C

This provides both tax efficiency and asset creation. Managing Additional Income If you have income beyond salary:

- Plan capital gains timing
- Use available exemptions and set-off provisions
- Avoid unnecessary short-term gains

Proper classification and timing can reduce tax impact. Step-by-Step Planning

1. **Calculate total income**
2. **Apply standard deduction**
3. **Use deductions under 80C, 80D, and NPS**
4. **Evaluate HRA and other allowances**
5. **Include home loan benefits if applicable**
6. **Compare both tax regimes**
7. **Adjust TDS or investments accordingly** — Practical Insight At ₹15 lakhs, the difference between planned and unplanned tax outcomes can be

significant. The key lies in consistent use of available provisions rather than complex strategies. Small inefficiencies, when applied to income taxed at 30%, result in higher absolute tax.

## Conclusion

For salaries above ₹15 lakhs, tax optimization depends on combining deductions, structuring salary effectively, and choosing the appropriate regime. While the scope for large reductions is limited, disciplined planning ensures efficient outcomes. Applying these strategies within the framework of the Income Tax Act, 1961 helps maintain compliance while minimizing unnecessary tax burden.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 69 of the Income Tax Act: Unexplained Investments
articles.excerpt: Section 69 turns unexplained investments into fully taxable income at an effective rate of ~78%. This guide covers the three conditions that trigger it, the tax rate under Section 115BBE, a worked example, and how to document gifts, loans, and inheritance properly.
articles.content:
## What Is Section 69?

Section 69 of the Income Tax Act deals with **unexplained investments**. If the income tax department finds that you have made investments during a financial year and those investments are either not recorded in your books, or the explanation you offer for the source of those investments is not satisfactory, the value of those investments is deemed to be your income for that year.

The law essentially says: if you cannot explain where the money for an investment came from, we will assume it was income you earned but did not declare — and that income will be taxed accordingly.

## When Does Section 69 Apply?

Section 69 is triggered when **all three** of the following conditions are met:

- An investment has been made by the taxpayer during a financial year
- The investment is either not recorded in the books of accounts, or no books are maintained
- The taxpayer cannot satisfactorily explain the nature and source of the investment to the AO

> [!NOTE] Any type of asset can trigger Section 69
> Section 69 can apply to jewellery, gold, fixed deposits, property, shares, or any other asset. **The source of funds is what matters, not the type of asset.**

## Tax Rate on Unexplained Investments

Under Section 115BBE, income deemed under Section 69 is taxed at a flat rate of **60%** plus a surcharge, with no deductions, exemptions, or allowances available.

| Component | Rate |
| --- | --- |
| Flat tax on unexplained income | 60% of deemed income |
| Surcharge on tax | 25% of the tax amount |
| Effective tax rate | 75% of deemed income |
| Health and Education Cess | 4% on tax + surcharge |
| **Final effective rate (approx.)** | **~78% of unexplained investment** |
| Penalty under Section 271AAC | 10% of tax paid under 115BBE |

## Example: Meera's Property Purchase — Delhi

> [!WARN] What happened when documentation was missing
> Meera, a homemaker, purchased a flat worth Rs. 80 lakh in FY 2021-22. Her declared income was only Rs. 4 lakh per year with no recorded savings to fund the purchase. She claimed the money came from her mother as a gift, but there was no documentary evidence of the gift or her mother's capacity to give it.
> The AO invoked Section 69 and treated Rs. 80 lakh as unexplained investment.
> Tax at 60% = Rs. 48 lakh | With surcharge and cess: **total demand exceeded Rs. 62 lakh**, plus penalties.
> Section 69 can be applied to any year in which the investment is traced, even if it was made several years ago. Proper documentation of gifts, loans, and inheritance is not optional.

## How to Respond to a Section 69 Notice

1. **Gather all documents** that prove the legitimate source of funds for the investment
2. **Collect gift deeds, loan agreements, bank transfer records, and inheritance documents** if applicable
3. **Show that the source of funds** is either your declared income, gifts received from relatives, or proceeds from sale of other assets
4. **File a written reply with all supporting documents** before the AO
5. **If the AO is not satisfied**, appeal to CIT(A) with complete documentation

## Prevention Tips

- Always maintain a clear paper trail for large investments: bank transfers, cheque records, gift deeds
- If you receive a cash gift from a relative, execute a proper gift deed and ensure the giver has capacity to give
- Avoid making large investments from funds that are not traceable to declared income
- If you are making investments from savings over several years, maintain year-wise records
- Ensure inheritance is documented through a will, legal heirship certificate, or settlement deed

## FAQs

### Can Section 69 apply to investments made in the name of family members?

Yes. If the AO finds that investments in the name of a spouse, children, or parents cannot be explained by them and appear to be funded by you, the deemed income can be attributed to you under related sections like 69A or 64.

### I inherited ancestral property and sold it for Rs. 50 lakh. How do I prove the source?

For inherited property, you need a legal heirship certificate or will, along with evidence of the original acquisition by the ancestor. If all documents are in place, Section 69 should not apply since the source is explainable.

### Is a cash gift from a non-relative acceptable as source of unexplained investment?

Gifts from non-relatives exceeding Rs. 50,000 are taxable as income under Section 56(2). So if you use such a gift to invest, you first need to declare it as income, pay taxes on it, and only then can it serve as an explained source.

### What if I had savings in hand that were not in a bank?

Cash savings are the most difficult to explain since they leave no paper trail. You would need to demonstrate a cash withdrawal pattern over years, past ITRs showing sufficient income, and logical reasons for holding cash.

### Does Section 69 expire after a certain number of years?

No. However, it can only be applied within the time limit for reassessment under Section 148/149 — generally up to **10 years** in cases of income exceeding Rs. 50 lakh that has escaped assessment.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 269SS: No Cash Loans or Deposits Above Rs. 20,000
articles.excerpt: Section 269SS prohibits accepting loans, deposits, or advances above Rs. 20,000 in cash. Violations attract a 100% penalty under Section 271D — even for friendly, innocent arrangements. This guide covers what is covered, exceptions, and how to respond to a penalty notice.
articles.content:
## What Is Section 269SS?

Section 269SS of the Income Tax Act **prohibits any person from accepting loans, deposits, or any specified sum of Rs. 20,000 or more in cash**. The restriction applies equally to both the giver and the receiver. If you lend someone Rs. 50,000 in cash or receive it as a deposit, both parties are in violation.

The rule was introduced to curb black money circulation through informal cash lending and to ensure that all significant financial transactions are routed through banking channels, creating a traceable audit trail.

## What Transactions Does It Cover?

| Transaction Type | Covered Under 269SS? |
| --- | --- |
| Loan received in cash above Rs. 20,000 | Yes |
| Deposit received in cash above Rs. 20,000 | Yes |
| Advance against property sale received in cash above Rs. 20,000 | Yes |
| Salary paid in cash above Rs. 20,000 | No (separate rules apply) |
| Gift received in cash below Rs. 50,000 from non-relative | No (covered under Section 56) |
| Cash payment for purchase of goods | No (covered under Section 40A(3)) |
| Cheque/NEFT/RTGS transactions above Rs. 20,000 | Not prohibited by 269SS |

## Exceptions to Section 269SS

- Loans or deposits taken from or given to a bank, post office, or government
- Transactions between persons where both have agricultural income and neither has taxable income
- Loans taken from or given to a cooperative society
- Transactions with the Reserve Bank of India
- Other persons as notified by the Central Government from time to time

## Penalty for Violation

| Aspect | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| Penalty provision | Section 271D |
| Penalty amount | **100% of loan/deposit received in cash** |
| Who bears the penalty | The person who accepted the cash |
| Authority | Joint Commissioner or higher |
| Appeal | CIT(A) within 30 days of penalty order |
| Reasonable cause defence | Available if genuine hardship can be proven |

## Example: Ramesh's Friendly Cash Loan — Coimbatore

> [!WARN] Innocent intent does not protect you
> Ramesh needed urgent funds for inventory. His friend Suresh handed him Rs. 1.5 lakh in cash as a short-term loan. Both thought it was a simple friendly arrangement.
> During a survey, the IT officer found the cash transaction. Ramesh received a **penalty notice under Section 271D for Rs. 1.5 lakh** — equal to the full loan amount.
> It does not matter if the intention was innocent. If cash changes hands as a loan or deposit above Rs. 20,000, Section 269SS is violated and Section 271D penalty follows automatically.

## How to Respond If a Penalty Notice Is Issued

1. **Check whether the transaction** genuinely falls under the definition of loan or deposit
2. **Identify if any specific exemption** applies to your case
3. **Prepare a written explanation citing reasonable cause** if the cash transaction was unavoidable
4. **Gather evidence** — urgency of transaction, lack of banking access, recipient's circumstances
5. **Submit a response to the Joint Commissioner** before the deadline in the notice
6. **If penalised, file an appeal before CIT(A)** with complete documentation

## Prevention Tips

- Always use cheque, NEFT, RTGS, or UPI for any loan or deposit transaction above Rs. 20,000
- Even for family loans, execute a simple loan agreement and transfer funds via bank
- Do not accept or give advances for property in cash above the threshold
- Educate your relatives and business associates about this restriction
- If banking services are genuinely not accessible, document the circumstances carefully

## FAQs

### Does Section 269SS apply to loans between husband and wife or parent and child?

Yes. The restriction applies to all persons including close family members. Even a cash loan from a parent to an adult child of Rs. 25,000 technically violates Section 269SS.

### What if I repay an old loan in cash? Does that attract penalty?

Repayment of loans in cash is governed by the companion provision **Section 269T**, not Section 269SS. Section 269T similarly prohibits cash repayment of loans or deposits above Rs. 20,000 and carries the same **100% penalty under Section 271E**.

### If I received multiple cash loans each below Rs. 20,000 but together above Rs. 20,000, is there a violation?

Each individual transaction is evaluated independently. If each cash loan is below Rs. 20,000, there is technically no violation of Section 269SS. However, the AO may view multiple small transactions as structured to circumvent the limit, which can trigger other provisions.

### Is there a time limit for the AO to initiate penalty proceedings under Section 271D?

Yes. The penalty order must be made within the time prescribed — generally **within 6 years** from the end of the relevant financial year.

### Can a company or firm also violate Section 269SS?

Yes. Section 269SS applies to all persons, including companies, partnerships, LLPs, and trusts. If a company receives a cash deposit from a director or promoter exceeding Rs. 20,000, it is equally liable.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Income from House Property and Taxes
articles.excerpt: Income from house property is one of the five heads of income under the Income Tax Act,
articles.content:
Income from house property is one of the five heads of income under the Income Tax Act,

1. **It applies to income earned from ownership of buildings or land attached to them,** — whether residential or commercial. The taxation here is structured and does not always

follow actual cash flow, since it is based on the concept of annual value.

## What gets taxed

The key condition is ownership, not receipt of rent. Income is taxed under this head when:

- You own the property
- It is not used for your own business or profession
- It is rented, or capable of generating rent

Even if a property is vacant, it may still be taxed under certain conditions. Types of property and how they are treated A property can fall into different categories, and the tax treatment changes accordingly. A self-occupied property has no taxable annual value. Its value is taken as nil, but you can still claim interest deduction on a home loan, subject to limits. A let-out property is taxed on the rent earned. The law considers either actual rent or expected rent, whichever is higher. If you own more than one house, only one can be treated as self-occupied. The others are treated as deemed let-out, even if you do not actually rent them.

## How income is calculated

### The computation follows a fixed method

- Start with gross annual value, which is the higher of expected rent or actual rent
- Deduct municipal taxes paid
- The result is net annual value
- From this, apply deductions:

○ 30% standard deduction ○ Interest on borrowed capital The final figure after these adjustments is the taxable income. Interest and deductions Interest on a home loan is a major benefit:

- Up to ₹2 lakh deduction for self-occupied property
- For let-out property, interest can be claimed fully, but overall loss set-off is restricted

The flat 30% standard deduction applies regardless of actual expenses, which simplifies calculation. Loss and set-off It is common for house property to show a loss, especially when there is a home loan.

- Loss up to ₹2 lakh can be set off against other income in the same year
- Remaining loss can be carried forward for 8 years

This makes housing loans a significant tax planning tool.

## Practical understanding

The system does not always match real income. A property may be taxed even if it is vacant, and deductions are allowed even if actual expenses are lower. At the same time, the structure provides built-in relief through standard deduction and interest benefits, which often reduce taxable income substantially.

## Conclusion

Income from house property is governed by a formula-driven approach rather than actual earnings. The focus is on ownership, expected income, and predefined deductions. A clear understanding of how annual value and deductions work ensures accurate reporting and efficient tax planning under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 44B and Section 172 of Income Tax Act
articles.excerpt: Income arising from shipping operations involving non-residents is governed by specific provisions under the Income Tax Act, 1961. Sections 44B and 172 deal with taxation of such income, but they operate in different contexts and with…
articles.content:
Income arising from shipping operations involving non-residents is governed by specific provisions under the Income Tax Act, 1961. Sections 44B and 172 deal with taxation of such income, but they operate in different contexts and with different compliance mechanisms. Understanding the distinction is important for businesses engaged in international shipping or dealing with foreign shipping companies. The Context of Shipping Income Shipping is inherently cross-border. Foreign shipping companies may earn income from:

- Transporting goods from Indian ports
- Carrying passengers to or from India
- Chartering vessels for operations connected to India

Since such entities may not have a permanent presence in India, special provisions exist to ensure tax collection. Section 44B: Presumptive Taxation for Non-Resident Shipping Businesses Section 44B provides a presumptive taxation scheme for non-resident shipping companies. Instead of computing actual profits, the law assumes that:

- 7.5% of specified receipts is treated as taxable income These receipts include:
- Amounts received for carriage of goods or passengers from Indian ports
- Amounts received outside India for such carriage

This simplifies taxation where maintaining detailed accounts in India may not be practical. Section 172: Voyage-Based Taxation Section 172 applies in cases where:

- A non-resident shipping company operates occasionally in India
- There is no regular presence or permanent establishment

In such situations, tax is collected on a voyage-by-voyage basis.

### Key aspects

- Tax is calculated on 7.5% of freight for that voyage
- Payment is required before the ship leaves the Indian port
- The responsibility often falls on the ship’s agent in India

This ensures immediate tax collection from occasional operators.

Key Difference Between Section 44B and Section 172 The distinction lies in the nature of operations:

- Section 44B applies to regular shipping businesses with ongoing operations
- Section 172 applies to occasional or isolated voyages

Section 44B operates through normal return filing, while Section 172 is a self-contained recovery mechanism.

## Practical Illustration

A foreign shipping company regularly transporting goods from Indian ports will be taxed under Section 44B, declaring income based on 7.5% of total receipts. On the other hand, a vessel that makes a one-time trip to India to carry cargo would fall under Section 172, with tax collected before departure. Interaction Between the Two Provisions If a non-resident is already assessed under regular provisions such as Section 44B, Section 172 may not apply. However, where no such assessment exists, Section 172 ensures that tax is still collected. This creates a dual framework that covers both regular operators and occasional participants.

## Practical Interpretation

These provisions reflect a policy approach focused on ease of administration and assured tax collection. Given the mobile nature of shipping businesses, traditional assessment methods may not be effective. By using presumptive income and voyage-based taxation, the law balances compliance with practicality.

## Conclusion

Sections 44B and 172 together form a specialized taxation framework for non-resident shipping income. While one provides a simplified method for regular operators, the other ensures immediate tax collection in isolated cases. Understanding their scope and application is essential for businesses dealing with international shipping, ensuring compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961 while avoiding procedural gaps.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Presumptive Taxation for Business and Profession
articles.excerpt: Presumptive taxation is a simplified method of computing taxable income under the Income Tax Act, 1961, designed for small businesses and professionals. Instead of maintaining detailed books and calculating actual profits, income is…
articles.content:
Presumptive taxation is a simplified method of computing taxable income under the Income Tax Act, 1961, designed for small businesses and professionals. Instead of maintaining detailed books and calculating actual profits, income is presumed at a fixed percentage of turnover or receipts, reducing compliance burden. This framework is particularly relevant for taxpayers seeking ease of filing without complex accounting requirements.

## What Presumptive Taxation Means

Under this scheme, the taxpayer is not required to compute actual income. Instead:

- Income is assumed at a prescribed rate
- No need to maintain detailed books of accounts
- Audit requirements are generally avoided

However, once opted, the taxpayer must comply with specific conditions.

## Key Sections Covered

### Presumptive taxation operates mainly through

- Section 44AD for small businesses
- Section 44ADA for professionals
- Section 44AE for transport businesses

Each section has its own scope, limits, and rates. Section 44AD (Business) Applicable to small businesses with turnover up to ₹2 crore.

- Presumed income:

○ 8% of turnover (cash transactions) ○ 6% of turnover (digital transactions) Eligible taxpayers include individuals, HUFs, and partnership firms (excluding LLPs). Section 44ADA (Profession) Applicable to specified professionals such as doctors, lawyers, engineers, architects, and consultants.

- Gross receipts limit: ₹50 lakh
- Presumed income: 50% of receipts

This simplifies compliance for professionals who may otherwise need detailed expense tracking. Section 44AE (Transport Business) Applicable to businesses engaged in plying, hiring, or leasing goods vehicles.

- Income is calculated per vehicle per month, based on prescribed rates What You Gain Presumptive taxation offers:
- Reduced compliance burden
- No requirement to maintain detailed books
- No mandatory tax audit (subject to conditions)
- Simplified income computation

This makes it attractive for small taxpayers.

## Important Conditions

- If income is declared lower than prescribed rates, books and audit may become mandatory
- Under Section 44AD, opting out after choosing the scheme may restrict re-entry for a specified period
- Advance tax requirements are simplified (usually a single installment) Practical Illustration

A small trader with turnover of ₹80 lakh opting for Section 44AD:

- Income presumed at 6% or 8% depending on transaction mode
- No need to track every expense
- Tax calculated on presumed income

Similarly, a professional earning ₹40 lakh can declare ₹20 lakh as income under Section 44ADA.

## Practical Interpretation

Presumptive taxation is not necessarily about reducing tax, but about reducing compliance effort. In some cases, actual profits may be lower than presumed income, making the scheme less beneficial. The decision should therefore balance simplicity versus accuracy.

## Conclusion

Presumptive taxation provides a structured and simplified approach for small businesses and professionals to comply with tax requirements. By replacing detailed accounting with fixed-rate income estimation, it reduces administrative complexity. Used appropriately, it enables efficient compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961 while allowing taxpayers to focus on business operations rather than extensive record-keeping.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How to File Income Tax Returns for Freelancers?
articles.excerpt: Freelancers and independent professionals operate outside the traditional salary framework, which makes tax compliance more self-driven. Unlike salaried individuals, there is usually no TDS deducted regularly, and the responsibility to…
articles.content:
Freelancers and independent professionals operate outside the traditional salary framework, which makes tax compliance more self-driven. Unlike salaried individuals, there is usually no TDS deducted regularly, and the responsibility to compute and pay tax rests entirely on the individual under the Income Tax Act, 1961. Filing Income Tax Returns (ITR) as a freelancer involves correctly classifying income, tracking expenses, and choosing the right method of taxation. Understanding How Freelancer Income is Treated Freelance income is generally classified as “Profits and Gains from Business or Profession”. This means:

- Income includes payments from clients, both domestic and international
- Expenses incurred to earn that income can be deducted
- Net profit becomes the taxable amount

Unlike salary, there is no standard structure, so accuracy depends on proper tracking.

## What Needs to Be in Place

### Before filing, a freelancer should have

- Total income received during the financial year
- Record of business-related expenses
- Bank statements and invoices
- TDS details (if any) from clients
- Form 26AS or AIS for verification

Maintaining basic records throughout the year simplifies filing significantly. Choosing the Right Method Freelancers can file using two approaches: Regular Method

- Maintain accounts
- Deduct actual expenses
- Tax paid on net profit Presumptive Taxation (Section 44ADA)
- Applicable if receipts are within specified limits
- 50% of receipts treated as income
- No need to maintain detailed books

The choice depends on whether actual expenses are higher or lower than the presumed percentage. Step-by-Step Filing Process

1. **Compute total income** — Add all receipts from freelance work
2. **Deduct expenses** — Include costs such as software, internet, office expenses, professional tools
3. **Determine taxable income** — Either actual profit or presumptive income
4. **Check TDS and advance tax** — Adjust tax already paid
5. **Select correct ITR form** — Typically ITR-3 or ITR-4 depending on method used
6. **Report income and deductions** — Fill relevant schedules in the return
7. **Pay balance tax, if any** — Through self-assessment

## Submit and verify the return

## Important Conditions

- Advance tax must be paid if total tax liability exceeds ₹10,000
- GST may apply separately based on turnover and nature of services
- Foreign income must be reported with proper conversion
- Presumptive scheme has eligibility limits and conditions Practical Situations

A freelance designer earning ₹18 lakh annually with moderate expenses may opt for presumptive taxation and declare ₹9 lakh as income. Another freelancer with higher expenses, such as equipment and team costs, may prefer the regular method to reduce taxable income.

## Common Areas of Confusion

### Freelancers often

- Mix personal and business expenses
- Ignore advance tax requirements
- Underreport small payments received across platforms
- Fail to reconcile income with AIS or bank records
