These issues can lead to mismatches and notices.

## Conclusion

Filing ITR as a freelancer requires a structured approach to income tracking and expense management. The flexibility offered under business income provisions also comes with increased responsibility. A clear understanding of classification, method selection, and compliance ensures accurate reporting under the Income Tax Act, 1961 while avoiding unnecessary complications.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Income Tax Notice: Types, Meaning, and What to Do When You Get One
articles.excerpt: Getting an income tax notice is not the end of the world. This guide explains all 8 common notice types (143(1), 143(2), 142(1), 148, 156, 139(9), 131, 133(6)), how to verify a notice is genuine, and a step-by-step response process.
articles.content:
## Why Do People Get Income Tax Notices?

The income tax department sends notices for many reasons. Sometimes it is because your return has a mismatch with data the department already has. Sometimes it is because you filed late. Sometimes it is because your income or spending pattern triggered an algorithm. And sometimes it is simply because your case has been selected for routine verification.

The important thing to understand is that **receiving a notice does not mean you have committed any offence**. It means the department has a question. Your job is to answer it clearly.

## Common Types of Income Tax Notices

| Section | Type of Notice | Why You Get It | Time Limit to Respond |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 143(1) | Intimation | Arithmetic error or mismatch in return | 30 days |
| 143(2) | Scrutiny Notice | Return selected for detailed examination | Typically 15 to 30 days |
| 142(1) | Inquiry Notice | AO wants information before assessment | As specified in notice |
| 148 | Reassessment Notice | Income has escaped assessment in past years | As specified |
| 156 | Demand Notice | Tax, penalty, or interest has been determined as due | 30 days to pay |
| 139(9) | Defective Return Notice | Return has errors or is incomplete | 15 days |
| 131 | Summons | Your personal presence or documents required | As specified |
| 133(6) | Information Notice | Third-party information being sought | As specified |

## How to Identify a Genuine Income Tax Notice

All genuine income tax notices are issued through the official **Income Tax e-filing portal (incometax.gov.in)** or sent physically through registered post or speed post.

To verify:

1. Log in to your income tax e-filing account
2. Navigate to **e-Proceedings** or **Pending Actions**
3. All official notices will appear there
4. Cross-check the **Document Identification Number (DIN)** mentioned on the notice

> [!WARN] Beware of fake notices
> If a notice arrives by WhatsApp, email from a non-official address, or through an unknown agent, treat it as suspicious. Official communications come **only from @incometax.gov.in** or through your e-filing portal.

## Step-by-Step Guide to Responding

1. **Read the notice carefully** — identify the exact section under which it is issued
2. **Note the deadline** — set a reminder well before it
3. **Gather all relevant documents** for the query raised
4. **Log in to the e-filing portal** — navigate to the specific proceeding
5. **Draft a clear, factual response** — upload supporting documents
6. **Submit and download the acknowledgement**
7. **Follow up after 30 to 45 days** if no reply is received from the AO

## Example: Priya's TDS Mismatch — Mumbai

> [!TIP] How a simple mismatch was resolved
> Priya, a salaried professional, received an intimation under Section 143(1) stating an arithmetic difference — a demand of Rs. 18,500. On checking, she realised she had incorrectly entered her TDS figure from Form 16.
> She logged into the portal, filed a **rectification request under Section 154** with the correct TDS figure from Form 26AS, and the demand was withdrawn within 45 days.
> Most Section 143(1) intimations resolve themselves once you verify your TDS claims against Form 26AS and respond correctly.

## Prevention Tips

- Always reconcile your ITR data with Form 26AS and AIS before filing
- Declare all sources of income including interest from savings accounts and fixed deposits
- Do not claim deductions you cannot substantiate with documents
- Keep your registered mobile number and email ID updated on the e-filing portal
- File your return on time every year, even if no tax is due
- After filing, periodically check your portal for any pending notices or communications

## FAQs

### What happens if I ignore an income tax notice?

Ignoring a notice can lead to an ex-parte assessment where the AO decides your tax liability without your input. This is almost always unfavourable. You may also face penalties for non-compliance and interest on any resulting demand.

### Can a notice be challenged if it was served incorrectly?

Yes. If the notice was not served to your correct address or registered email, or if proper procedure was not followed, you can raise a jurisdictional objection. However, do not use this to delay a valid notice without addressing the substance.

### I received a scrutiny notice under 143(2). Should I be worried?

Not necessarily. Scrutiny notices are issued both randomly and based on specific triggers. As long as your income is correctly declared and you have supporting documents, a scrutiny assessment can be handled without significant additional liability.

### How long does the income tax department have to respond after I submit a reply?

There is no fixed statutory timeline for the AO to respond in all cases. However, assessment proceedings under Section 143(3) must be **completed within 12 months** from the end of the assessment year in which the return was filed.

### Can I appoint a representative to appear before the AO on my behalf?

Yes. You can authorise a Chartered Accountant or tax advocate to represent you before the AO by filing a Power of Attorney or Form 2848. This is common in scrutiny and reassessment cases.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 10(10D) of Income Tax Act – Life Insurance Tax Exemption Rules & Deductions
articles.excerpt: Life insurance policies serve a dual purpose of protection and savings. The tax treatment of proceeds from such policies is governed by Section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which provides exemption on maturity proceeds, subject to…
articles.content:
Life insurance policies serve a dual purpose of protection and savings. The tax treatment of proceeds from such policies is governed by Section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which provides exemption on maturity proceeds, subject to specific conditions. While the provision appears straightforward, its applicability depends on factors such as premium limits, policy type, and date of issuance.

What Section 10(10D) Covers Section 10(10D) exempts from tax:

- Maturity proceeds from life insurance policies
- Death benefits received by nominees
- Bonuses received under the policy

This exemption applies regardless of the amount, provided conditions are satisfied.

## Key Conditions for Exemption

The exemption is not automatic. It depends on the premium-to-sum assured ratio:

- For policies issued before 1 April 2012

Premium should not exceed 20% of sum assured

- For policies issued on or after 1 April 2012

Premium should not exceed 10% of sum assured

- For certain policies issued for persons with disability or specified illness The limit may extend to 15%

If these conditions are not met, the maturity proceeds may become taxable. Special Rule for High-Value Policies Recent amendments introduced additional conditions:

- If aggregate premium exceeds ₹5 lakh per year (for policies issued after 1 April

2023), maturity proceeds may become taxable

### However

- This restriction does not apply to death benefits, which remain fully exempt

How Tax Applies When Conditions Are Not Met If a policy does not satisfy the exemption conditions:

- Maturity proceeds are taxed as “Income from Other Sources”
- Only the net gain (after deducting premium paid) is taxable

This ensures that only the income portion is taxed.

## Practical Illustration

An individual holds a policy where annual premium exceeds the prescribed percentage of sum assured.

- On maturity, the proceeds become taxable
- Only the profit portion is taxed, not the total payout

In contrast, if the policy meets all conditions, the entire maturity amount remains tax-free. Distinction Between Maturity and Death Benefit

- Death benefit: Always exempt, regardless of premium size
- Maturity benefit: Exempt only if conditions are satisfied

This distinction is critical in understanding tax implications.

## Practical Interpretation

Section 10(10D) is designed to encourage genuine insurance protection rather than high-premium investment products. The introduction of premium limits ensures that tax exemption is aligned with the risk coverage aspect of insurance. For policyholders, this means evaluating not just returns but also tax treatment at maturity.

## Conclusion

Section 10(10D) provides significant tax relief on life insurance proceeds, but its applicability depends on compliance with specified conditions. Premium limits and policy structure play a decisive role in determining whether the proceeds are tax-free. A clear understanding of these rules ensures proper planning and avoids unexpected tax liability under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Income Tax Return (ITR) Processing Time
articles.excerpt: Once an Income Tax Return (ITR) is filed, it goes through a system-driven verification and computation process before being accepted or adjusted. The timeline for this process, commonly referred to as ITR processing time, determines when…
articles.content:
Once an Income Tax Return (ITR) is filed, it goes through a system-driven verification and computation process before being accepted or adjusted. The timeline for this process, commonly referred to as ITR processing time, determines when a taxpayer receives an intimation, refund, or demand under the Income Tax Act, 1961. Understanding how long processing takes and what affects it helps set realistic expectations and avoid unnecessary follow-ups.

What “Processing” Actually Means ITR processing is not manual scrutiny in most cases. It is a system-based validation carried out under Section 143(1), where the Income Tax Department:

- Verifies income details reported
- Matches TDS, TCS, and tax payments with Form 26AS/AIS
- Checks arithmetical accuracy
- Applies basic adjustments, if required

After this, an intimation is issued, confirming acceptance or highlighting differences. Typical Processing Timeline In recent years, processing has become faster due to automation:

- Most ITRs are processed within a few weeks to 1–3 months
- Refunds, if any, are issued after successful processing

However, timelines may vary depending on complexity and verification status.

## What Influences Processing Time

### Several factors affect how quickly a return is processed

- Verification status of the return (e-verification speeds up processing)
- Accuracy and completeness of data
- Matching of TDS details with Form 26AS
- Type of ITR filed (simple vs complex cases)
- Volume of returns during peak filing season

Returns with mismatches or incomplete data may take longer. Role of E-Verification A return is not processed until it is verified.

- E-verification through Aadhaar OTP, net banking, or other methods enables faster processing
- Physical verification (sending ITR-V) may delay processing

Timely verification is essential for starting the processing cycle. Refund Timeline If a refund is due:

- It is issued after processing is complete
- Typically credited within a few days to weeks after intimation

Delays in refund are often linked to delays in processing or verification issues.

## Practical Situations

A salaried individual with Form 16 and matching TDS records may see processing completed quickly. In contrast, a taxpayer with multiple income sources or discrepancies between reported income and AIS may experience delays due to additional validation.

## When Delays Occur

### Processing may take longer in cases such as

- Mismatch between return and Form 26AS/AIS
- Incorrect bank account details
- Large refunds triggering additional checks
- Pending verification

In such cases, reviewing and correcting data may be necessary.

## What Happens After Processing

Once processed, an intimation under Section 143(1) is issued, which may show:

- No change in return
- Refund due
- Tax payable (demand)

The taxpayer must review this carefully and take action if required.

## Conclusion

ITR processing time has reduced significantly with digitization, but it still depends on accuracy, verification, and data matching. A correctly filed and verified return is typically processed without delay. Ensuring consistency between reported income and tax records helps achieve faster processing under the Income Tax Act, 1961 and smooth receipt of refunds.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Dearness Allowance (DA) – Meaning, Types, Calculation & Taxation
articles.excerpt: Dearness Allowance (DA) is a component of salary paid to employees, primarily in the government and public sector, to offset the impact of inflation on cost of living. It forms an integral part of salary structure and directly affects…
articles.content:
Dearness Allowance (DA) is a component of salary paid to employees, primarily in the government and public sector, to offset the impact of inflation on cost of living. It forms an integral part of salary structure and directly affects taxable income under the Income Tax Act, 1961. DA is revised periodically based on inflation indices, ensuring that purchasing power remains relatively stable over time.

## What Dearness Allowance Represents

### DA is not a fixed amount. It is

- Calculated as a percentage of basic salary
- Revised periodically based on inflation trends
- Applicable mainly to government employees and pensioners, though some private sector organizations also adopt it

It is designed to compensate for price level changes in the economy. Types of Dearness Allowance Dearness Allowance is generally classified into: Industrial Dearness Allowance (IDA)

- Applicable to public sector employees
- Revised based on consumer price index for industrial workers Variable Dearness Allowance (VDA)
- Applicable in certain sectors and wage structures
- Adjusted periodically based on inflation metrics

For central government employees, DA revisions are typically announced twice a year.

## How DA is Calculated

### DA is computed as

- DA = Basic Salary × DA Rate (%)

The DA rate is determined by the government based on inflation indices. For example, if basic salary is ₹50,000 and DA rate is 40%:

- DA = ₹20,000

This amount becomes part of total salary. Tax Treatment of DA Under the Income Tax Act, 1961:

- DA is fully taxable as salary income
- It is included in gross salary for tax computation
- It is also considered for calculating retirement benefits in certain cases

There is no separate exemption available for DA. DA and HRA Interaction In certain situations:

- DA may be included as part of salary for calculating HRA exemption, if it forms part of retirement benefits

This can influence overall tax calculation indirectly.

## Practical Illustration

### An employee with

- Basic salary: ₹40,000
- DA rate: 30%

Receives ₹12,000 as DA. This amount is added to salary and taxed accordingly.

## Practical Interpretation

### DA plays a dual role

- It increases take-home salary
- It also increases taxable income

For government employees, periodic increases in DA can lead to higher tax liability unless offset by deductions.

## Conclusion

Dearness Allowance is a key salary component designed to address inflation, but it carries full tax implications. Its periodic revisions make it an important factor in both compensation planning and tax computation. A clear understanding of its calculation and treatment ensures accurate reporting under the Income Tax Act, 1961 and better financial planning.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Intimation Under Section 143(1) of Income Tax Act – ITR Intimation Password
articles.excerpt: After an Income Tax Return (ITR) is filed and processed, the Income Tax Department issues an intimation under Section 143(1) of the Income Tax Act, 1961. This is a system-generated document that confirms whether the return has been…
articles.content:
After an Income Tax Return (ITR) is filed and processed, the Income Tax Department issues an intimation under Section 143(1) of the Income Tax Act, 1961. This is a system-generated document that confirms whether the return has been accepted as filed or adjusted based on automated checks. It is not a scrutiny notice, but rather a summary of final computation after processing, and it plays a key role in confirming tax liability, refund, or demand.

What the Intimation Contains and How It Works The intimation reflects a comparison between the return filed by the taxpayer and the computation done by the tax department’s system. It typically includes:

- Returned income versus processed income
- Tax calculated by the taxpayer and by the department
- Details of any adjustments made
- Final outcome in terms of refund, demand, or no change The system processes the return by:
- Matching TDS, TCS, and tax payments with Form 26AS and AIS
- Checking for arithmetic errors
- Identifying inconsistencies in claims or disclosures

Based on this, the department either accepts the return or makes limited adjustments. Possible Outcomes and What They Mean The intimation can result in three broad outcomes. If there is no change, the return is accepted as filed and no further action is required. If a refund is due, it indicates that excess tax has been paid. The refund is processed after this intimation. If there is a tax demand, it means the department has recalculated the tax and found additional liability. This usually happens due to mismatches in TDS, incorrect claims, or calculation differences. Each of these outcomes should be reviewed carefully to ensure accuracy. Password Format and Accessing the Intimation The intimation is sent as a password-protected PDF to the registered email ID. The password format is standard:

- First 4 characters of PAN in uppercase
- Followed by Date of Birth in DDMMYYYY format

For example, if PAN is ABCDE1234F and date of birth is 10 March 1992, the password will be ABCD10031992. This format ensures secure access to sensitive tax information.

What Should Be Reviewed After Receiving It Once the intimation is accessed, it should not be treated as a formality. It is important to verify:

- Whether income figures match what was filed
- Whether TDS credit has been correctly considered
- Whether any deductions or exemptions have been modified
- Whether the final tax payable or refund is accurate

If everything aligns, no further action is needed. If there are discrepancies, corrective action becomes necessary. Handling Differences and Follow-Up Actions In case of mismatches, the response depends on the nature of the issue. If the adjustment is correct, any additional tax demand should be paid promptly. If the adjustment appears incorrect, a rectification request can be filed through the income tax portal. Supporting details should be provided to justify the claim. Timely response is important, as ignoring the intimation may lead to further notices or recovery actions.

## Practical Understanding

In most cases, differences arise due to TDS mismatches or reporting gaps. For example, if TDS claimed in the return is higher than what appears in Form 26AS, the system may reduce the credit and raise a demand. Similarly, minor errors in calculation or incorrect entries can trigger adjustments. The intimation effectively acts as a final checkpoint between return filing and closure of the tax assessment for that year.

## Conclusion

Intimation under Section 143(1) is a critical part of the return processing cycle. It confirms how the Income Tax Department has interpreted and processed the filed return. Reviewing it carefully and responding where necessary ensures accurate closure of tax liability under the Income Tax Act, 1961, while avoiding unnecessary follow-ups or disputes.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 80TTB Deduction for Senior Citizens
articles.excerpt: Section 80TTB of the Income Tax Act, 1961 provides a targeted tax benefit to senior citizens by allowing deduction on interest income. With many retirees relying on fixed deposits and savings instruments, this provision plays an important…
articles.content:
Section 80TTB of the Income Tax Act, 1961 provides a targeted tax benefit to senior citizens by allowing deduction on interest income. With many retirees relying on fixed deposits and savings instruments, this provision plays an important role in reducing taxable income from such sources. It is designed to offer broader relief compared to similar deductions available to non-senior taxpayers. Scope and What It Covers Section 80TTB allows deduction on interest income earned by senior citizens from specified sources. This includes:

- Interest from savings accounts
- Interest from fixed deposits (FDs)
- Interest from recurring deposits (RDs)
- Deposits held with banks, post offices, or co-operative banks

Unlike certain other provisions, the benefit is not limited to savings accounts alone. Deduction Limit and Eligibility The deduction is available subject to the following conditions:

- Maximum deduction: ₹50,000 per financial year
- Applicable only to resident individuals aged 60 years or above
- Available only under the old tax regime

If total interest income is less than ₹50,000, the entire amount can be deducted.

## How It Works in Practice

The deduction is applied after computing total income. For example:

- A senior citizen earns ₹60,000 as interest income
- Deduction allowed under Section 80TTB is ₹50,000
- Only ₹10,000 becomes taxable

This significantly reduces tax liability for retirees dependent on interest income. Difference from Section 80TTA Section 80TTB replaces Section 80TTA for senior citizens.

- Section 80TTA applies to non-senior individuals and is limited to ₹10,000, only for savings account interest
- Section 80TTB offers a higher limit of ₹50,000 and covers a wider range of deposits

A senior citizen cannot claim both; only Section 80TTB applies.

## Practical Considerations

Interest income is often subject to TDS by banks once it crosses prescribed thresholds. Even in such cases, deduction under Section 80TTB can still be claimed while filing the return. It is important to:

- Aggregate interest income across all accounts
- Reconcile with Form 26AS or AIS
- Ensure correct reporting before applying deduction Practical Interpretation

Section 80TTB acknowledges the financial reality of retirees, where interest income forms a major portion of earnings. By allowing a higher deduction, it reduces the tax burden on relatively stable but fixed income sources. However, the benefit is meaningful only when the taxpayer opts for the old tax regime, since it is not available under the new regime.

## Conclusion

Section 80TTB provides a focused tax relief for senior citizens by allowing deduction on interest income up to ₹50,000. Its broader coverage and higher limit make it a key provision for retirement income planning. A clear understanding of eligibility and correct reporting ensures effective utilization of this benefit under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How are Gifts Taxed? – Gift Tax Exemption, Relatives List
articles.excerpt: Gifts are commonly exchanged for personal, social, and financial reasons, but their tax treatment depends on the nature of the gift, relationship between parties, and value involved. Under the Income Tax Act, 1961, gifts are governed by…
articles.content:
Gifts are commonly exchanged for personal, social, and financial reasons, but their tax treatment depends on the nature of the gift, relationship between parties, and value involved. Under the Income Tax Act, 1961, gifts are governed by specific provisions that determine when they are taxable and when they are exempt. The law does not impose a separate “gift tax” today. Instead, certain gifts are taxed in the hands of the recipient under the head “Income from Other Sources.”

## When Gifts Become Taxable

### A gift is taxable when

- It is received without consideration, and
- The aggregate value exceeds ₹50,000 in a financial year, and
- It is received from a non-exempt person

If these conditions are met, the entire amount becomes taxable, not just the excess. Gifts can include:

- Cash
- Movable property such as shares, jewellery, or securities
- Immovable property such as land or building Situations Where Gifts Are Fully Exempt

Certain gifts are completely exempt regardless of amount: Gifts from specified relatives Gifts received on the occasion of marriage Gifts received through a will or inheritance Gifts received from local authorities or specified institutions Among these, the most commonly used exemption is for gifts from relatives.

Who Qualifies as “Relative” The definition of relative is specific and limited. It includes:

- Spouse
- Brother or sister
- Brother or sister of the spouse
- Brother or sister of either parent
- Any lineal ascendant or descendant (parents, grandparents, children, grandchildren)
- Spouse of the above persons

Gifts from these individuals are fully exempt, irrespective of value. Tax Treatment of Different Types of Gifts The nature of the asset determines how taxation is applied:

- Cash gifts: Fully taxable if above ₹50,000 (unless exempt)
- Movable property: Taxed based on fair market value
- Immovable property: Taxed if stamp duty value exceeds threshold or if received below fair value

The valuation rules ensure that undervaluation does not avoid taxation.

## Practical Illustration

### If an individual receives ₹1,00,000 from a friend

- Since the amount exceeds ₹50,000 and the friend is not a relative,
- The entire ₹1,00,000 becomes taxable

### If the same amount is received from a parent

- It is fully exempt, regardless of amount Important Considerations
- The ₹50,000 threshold applies to aggregate gifts during the year
- Gifts from multiple non-relatives must be combined
- Proper documentation is important, especially for large transfers
- Clubbing provisions may apply in certain cases involving spouses or minor children Practical Interpretation

The taxation of gifts is structured to prevent misuse of transfers as a means of tax avoidance. At the same time, it allows genuine transfers within families without tax implications. For taxpayers, the key is understanding the relationship-based exemption and maintaining clarity on the source and nature of funds.

## Conclusion

Gift taxation in India is based on defined rules that balance flexibility with control. While genuine personal transfers are largely exempt, unexplained or non-relative gifts beyond the threshold become taxable. Clear classification, documentation, and awareness of exemptions ensure compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961 while avoiding unintended tax liability.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: LTCG (Long-Term Capital Gains Tax): Rates, Calculation & Exemptions
articles.excerpt: Complete guide to LTCG tax in India after Budget 2024: the 12.5% equity rate, Rs. 1.25 lakh annual exemption, property indexation choice, Section 54/54EC/54F exemptions, and practical examples.
articles.content:
## Overview

Long-term capital gains tax is one of those topics that sounds straightforward until you actually sit down to file your returns. The rules changed significantly after the Union Budget 2024, and many investors are still working with outdated assumptions. This guide cuts through the confusion and gives you a clear, current picture of how LTCG works in India.

When you sell a capital asset after holding it for a specified period, the profit you make is classified as a long-term capital gain. The holding period threshold varies by asset type, and so does the tax rate. Getting this wrong can cost you significantly, either through overpayment or through penalties for underpayment.

The 2024 Budget brought in a flat **12.5% LTCG rate** on equity and equity-oriented assets, eliminated indexation on property sales (though a partial rollback followed), and raised the exemption limit on equity gains from Rs. 1 lakh to **Rs. 1.25 lakh**. Understanding these shifts is essential before making any sell decision.

## Tax Rates at a Glance

| Asset Type | Holding Period for LTCG | LTCG Tax Rate | Indexation Available? |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Listed Equity Shares | More than 12 months | 12.5% | No |
| Equity Mutual Funds | More than 12 months | 12.5% | No |
| Debt Mutual Funds (pre-Apr 2023) | More than 36 months | 20% | Yes |
| Debt Mutual Funds (post-Apr 2023) | Taxed as STCG (slab rate) | N/A | No |
| Real Estate (Residential) | More than 24 months | 12.5% without indexation (or 20% with indexation for older assets) | Limited |
| Gold (Physical) | More than 24 months | 12.5% | No (post-Budget 2024) |
| Unlisted Shares | More than 24 months | 12.5% | No |
| Bonds (Listed) | More than 12 months | 12.5% | No |

> [!NOTE] Cess on top of base rate
> Surcharge and health and education cess of 4% apply over the base tax rate. The effective rate for most investors becomes approximately 13%.

## How LTCG Is Calculated

The basic formula is:

**LTCG = Sale Price − Cost of Acquisition − Cost of Improvement − Transfer Expenses**

For equity assets, there is an additional **grandfathering rule**: gains accrued up to January 31, 2018 are exempt. This is handled through the Fair Market Value (FMV) concept.

### Calculation Example: LTCG on Listed Equity Shares

Ramesh bought 500 shares of a company at Rs. 200 each in March 2022. He sold them in October 2024 at Rs. 480 each.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Sale Value | Rs. 2,40,000 |
| Cost of Acquisition | Rs. 1,00,000 |
| LTCG | Rs. 1,40,000 |
| Exemption (up to Rs. 1,25,000) | Rs. 1,25,000 |
| Taxable LTCG | Rs. 15,000 |
| Tax at 12.5% | Rs. 1,875 |
| Add Cess at 4% | Rs. 75 |
| **Total Tax Payable** | **Rs. 1,950** |

This illustrates why timing your redemptions to stay within the Rs. 1.25 lakh annual threshold each year can save meaningful amounts over time.

## Exemptions Available on LTCG

### The Rs. 1.25 Lakh Annual Exemption (Equity)

Gains up to Rs. 1.25 lakh per financial year from listed equity shares and equity mutual funds are fully exempt. This applies **per taxpayer**, not per account.

### Section 54 (Reinvestment in Residential Property)

If LTCG arises from the sale of a residential house, reinvesting the gains in another residential property within the specified time frame exempts the gain.

### Section 54EC (Capital Gains Bonds)

Investing in government-notified bonds (NHAI, REC) within 6 months of the sale of any long-term asset can exempt gains up to Rs. 50 lakh.

### Section 54F (From Non-Residential Assets)

If you sell any asset other than a residential house, reinvesting the entire sale proceeds in a residential house can give you full exemption.

### Grandfathering for Equity (Pre-January 31, 2018 gains)

Unrealised gains in equity up to January 31, 2018 are not taxed. The higher of cost price or FMV on that date becomes the acquisition cost.

## Strategy Tips

1. **Harvest gains annually up to Rs. 1.25 lakh.** Sell and repurchase equity holdings each year to reset your cost base and utilise the exemption limit. This is called tax-loss and gain harvesting and is perfectly legal.
2. **Offset gains with losses.** LTCG can be set off against Long-Term Capital Losses (LTCL). Plan your portfolio exits to book losses in the same financial year as large gains.
3. **Hold for the long-term threshold.** Selling equity just before the 12-month mark means you pay 20% STCG instead of 12.5% LTCG. Waiting a few extra weeks can cut your tax bill significantly.
4. **Use Section 54EC for property sales.** If you have sold real estate with a large gain, park up to Rs. 50 lakh in capital gains bonds immediately. The lock-in is 5 years, but the tax saving often outweighs the illiquidity.
5. **Split holdings between spouses.** Each taxpayer gets the Rs. 1.25 lakh exemption separately. Investing in both spouses' names effectively doubles the annual tax-free threshold on equity gains.

> [!TIP] Plan before you sell, not after
> Most LTCG strategies must be set up before or within months of the sale. A tax consultation after you have already registered the transaction dramatically limits your options.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. If I make LTCG of Rs. 3 lakh from equity this year, can I carry forward the tax liability to next year?

No. LTCG tax is payable in the year the gains are realised. However, if you have long-term capital losses from the same or previous years (up to 8 years), you can set them off to reduce the taxable amount.

### Q2. Does the Rs. 1.25 lakh exemption apply separately to equity shares and equity mutual funds, or is it combined?

It is a **combined limit** across all equity and equity-oriented assets. If you earn Rs. 80,000 from shares and Rs. 70,000 from equity mutual funds in the same year, your total LTCG is Rs. 1.5 lakh. Only Rs. 1.25 lakh is exempt; Rs. 25,000 is taxable.

### Q3. Is LTCG from international equity mutual funds taxed at 12.5%?

No. International fund of funds are **not classified as equity-oriented funds** for tax purposes. They are taxed like debt funds — gains are added to your income and taxed at your applicable slab rate, regardless of holding period (post-April 2023).

### Q4. Can NRIs also claim the Rs. 1.25 lakh LTCG exemption on equity?

Yes, NRIs are entitled to the same basic LTCG exemption on equity investments as resident Indians. However, TDS is deducted at source by the broker or fund house, and they need to file a return to claim any refund.

### Q5. If I gift shares to a family member and they sell them, who pays LTCG?

The person who sells the shares pays LTCG on the gains. However, the cost of acquisition and the holding period of the original owner are considered for calculating the gain, which can be advantageous if the original purchase was long ago at a lower price.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Late Filing Penalty Under Section 234F: What It Is and How to Avoid It
articles.excerpt: Section 234F imposes an automatic fee of up to Rs. 10,000 for filing your ITR after the due date. This guide covers the full fee structure, all other consequences of late filing (loss carry-forward, refund delays), due dates to remember, and the Section 139(8A) updated return option.
articles.content:
## What Is Section 234F?

Section 234F of the Income Tax Act, introduced from Assessment Year 2018-19 onwards, prescribes a **mandatory fee for filing your income tax return after the due date**. This replaced the earlier discretionary penalty under Section 271F.

The key difference: the old Section 271F left it to the AO's discretion to impose a penalty. Section 234F is **automatic**. If you file late, the fee is levied without exception.

## Fee Structure Under Section 234F

| Filing Date | Total Income Above Rs. 5 Lakh | Total Income Rs. 5 Lakh or Below |
| --- | --- | --- |
| On or before the due date | No fee | No fee |
| After due date but by December 31 of AY | Rs. 5,000 | Rs. 1,000 |
| After December 31 but by March 31 of AY | Rs. 10,000 | Rs. 1,000 |
| Total income below basic exemption limit | No fee regardless of filing date | No fee regardless of filing date |

## Other Consequences of Late Filing

| Consequence | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| Interest under Section 234A | 1% per month on unpaid taxes from due date to actual filing date |
| No carry-forward of losses | Business losses and capital losses cannot be carried forward if return is filed late |
| Delayed refund | Refunds are processed only after filing; late filing delays your refund and reduces refund interest |
| Scrutiny risk | Late filers attract higher scrutiny probability |
| Prosecution in extreme cases | Wilful failure to file can lead to prosecution under Section 276CC |

## Due Dates to Remember

| Category of Taxpayer | Original Due Date | Belated Return (Sec 139(4)) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Individual / HUF with no audit | July 31 of AY | December 31 of AY |
| Business requiring tax audit | October 31 of AY | December 31 of AY |
| Transfer pricing cases | November 30 of AY | December 31 of AY |
| Revised return under Sec 139(5) | December 31 of AY | N/A |

## Example: Deepa, Freelance Designer — Pune

> [!WARN] The total extra cost of filing late
> Deepa's total annual income was Rs. 9.5 lakh (including freelance income). ITR due date: July 31, 2024. She filed on November 20, 2024.
> **Late filing fee (Section 234F):** Rs. 5,000 (income above Rs. 5 lakh, filed before December 31)
> **Interest (Section 234A):** ~Rs. 800 (assuming Rs. 40,000 tax was unpaid at filing)
> **Total extra cost: Rs. 5,800** — and if she had a business loss that year, she would also have **permanently lost the ability to carry it forward**.
> For a freelancer with business losses, filing late is doubly painful. You pay a fee *and* lose the tax shield that carry-forward losses provide.

## What About Updated Return Under Section 139(8A)?

If you missed even the December 31 deadline, there is one more option. Section 139(8A) allows you to file an **Updated Return (ITR-U)** within **two years** from the end of the relevant assessment year. However, this comes with an additional tax surcharge of **25% to 50%** of the aggregate tax and interest due.

> [!NOTE] ITR-U is a last resort, not a planning tool
> An updated return requires you to declare additional income and pay a significant surcharge. It is not a substitute for timely filing — it is an emergency exit.

## How to File After Missing the Due Date

1. **File the belated return under Section 139(4)** — as soon as possible to limit interest accrual
2. **Pay any outstanding tax before filing** — to stop the Section 234A interest meter
3. **The Section 234F fee** is payable at the time of filing and gets automatically included in your tax challan
4. **If income is below the basic exemption limit**, verify this before assuming you can skip filing

## Prevention Tips

- Set a calendar reminder for **July 31 every year** if you are a non-audit assessee
- Collect all Form 16, Form 16A, and bank statements by mid-June to allow preparation time
- Use the AIS and Form 26AS reconciliation tools on the income tax portal to pre-check your data
- If you have business losses to carry forward, make filing on time an **absolute priority**
- If a CA is handling the filing, confirm well in advance that all documents are submitted to them
- For complex returns involving multiple capital gains or business income, start the process by June

## FAQs

### If my total income is below the basic exemption limit, do I still pay Section 234F fees for late filing?

No. Section 234F explicitly states that no fee is leviable if the total income does not exceed the basic exemption limit — Rs. 2.5 lakh (below 60 years), Rs. 3 lakh (senior citizens), Rs. 5 lakh (super senior citizens).

### I filed late but I am due a refund. Will I still be charged interest under Section 234A?

Section 234A interest is charged only if there is tax payable at the time of filing. If you have excess TDS and your return shows a refund due, Section 234A interest generally does not apply. However, the Section 234F fee still applies if the income is above the exemption limit.

### Can I carry forward my long-term capital loss if I file late?

No. Losses under all heads except losses from house property **cannot be carried forward** if the return is filed after the original due date. This includes both short-term and long-term capital losses — one of the most overlooked costs of late filing.

### What is the difference between a belated return and an updated return?

A **belated return (Section 139(4))** can be filed up to December 31 of the assessment year at the cost of the Section 234F fee and interest. An **updated return (Section 139(8A))** can be filed up to 2 years after the end of the AY but requires declaring additional income and paying an extra surcharge of **25% to 50%**.

### Will filing a belated return affect my ability to get a home loan or visa?

Most lenders and visa authorities accept belated returns, though some premium loan products or consulates may require returns filed on time for the past 2 to 3 years. Consistently late filing can create practical difficulties even if there is no strict legal disqualification.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Tax Collected at Source (TCS) – Rates, Payment and Exemption
articles.excerpt: Tax Collected at Source (TCS) is a mechanism under the Income Tax Act, 1961 where the seller collects tax from the buyer at the time of sale of specified goods or services. Unlike TDS, where tax is deducted while making a payment, TCS…
articles.content:
Tax Collected at Source (TCS) is a mechanism under the Income Tax Act, 1961 where the seller collects tax from the buyer at the time of sale of specified goods or services. Unlike TDS, where tax is deducted while making a payment, TCS shifts the responsibility to the seller to collect tax as part of the transaction value. This system is designed to track high-value transactions and ensure early tax collection. Where TCS Applies TCS is applicable only on specified transactions, not on all sales. Common categories include:

- Sale of goods exceeding prescribed turnover thresholds
- Sale of motor vehicles above specified value
- Foreign remittances under the Liberalised Remittance Scheme (LRS)
- Sale of scrap, minerals, and certain other goods

The applicability depends on both nature of transaction and monetary limits.

## How TCS Works in Practice

### The mechanism is straightforward

- Seller collects tax as a percentage of sale value
- Buyer pays this amount along with the purchase price
- Seller deposits the collected tax with the government

The tax collected is reflected in the buyer’s Form 26AS, allowing them to claim credit while filing ITR. TCS Rates (Indicative) Rates vary based on transaction type:

- Sale of goods above threshold: 0.1%
- Motor vehicle above specified value: 1%
- Foreign remittances (LRS): varies depending on purpose
- Scrap and certain goods: higher prescribed rates

Rates are subject to change and specific conditions. Payment and Compliance The seller is responsible for:

- Collecting TCS at the time of receipt or sale
- Depositing it within prescribed timelines
- Filing periodic TCS returns
- Issuing TCS certificates to buyers

Non-compliance can lead to interest and penalties.

## Exemptions and Non-Applicability

### TCS may not apply in certain situations

- When buyer deducts TDS on the same transaction
- Transactions with government or notified entities
- Where declarations are submitted for specific purposes

The overlap between TDS and TCS is carefully managed to avoid double taxation.

## Practical Illustration

A seller with turnover exceeding the prescribed limit sells goods worth ₹10 lakh:

- TCS at 0.1% is collected from the buyer
- Buyer pays ₹10,00100
- Seller deposits ₹100 as TCS

The buyer can later claim this ₹100 as tax credit.

## Practical Interpretation

TCS is not an additional tax burden. It is an advance tax collection mechanism, where the amount collected is adjustable against final tax liability. For businesses, it introduces additional compliance, while for buyers, it ensures that transactions are captured within the tax system.

## Conclusion

Tax Collected at Source strengthens the tax framework by embedding tax collection within transactions. While it adds compliance responsibility for sellers, it improves transparency and traceability of high-value dealings. Understanding its applicability and correct reporting ensures smooth compliance under the Income Tax Act, 1961 and avoids unnecessary disputes.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: What Is a Nil Return? Who Needs to File It and Why
articles.excerpt: A nil return is an ITR with zero tax payable — but filing it may still be mandatory or highly advisable. This guide covers when nil return filing is compulsory, the triggers that override the income limit, five reasons to file even when not required, and a step-by-step filing process.
articles.content:
## Introduction

There is a widespread assumption among salaried individuals and small earners that if their tax liability is zero — either because their income is below the taxable limit or because deductions wipe out the tax — they have no obligation to file an Income Tax Return. This assumption is only **partially correct**, and acting on it without verification can create unnecessary complications. Enter the concept of a Nil Return.

## What Is a Nil Return?

A **Nil Return** is an Income Tax Return (ITR) filed by an individual whose net tax payable for the financial year is zero. The term "nil" refers to the tax liability, not necessarily the income. A person may have earned Rs. 8 lakh in a year, paid no tax because deductions and rebates reduced their liability to zero, and still file a nil return to inform the Income Tax Department of their income and financial activity.

> [!NOTE] A nil return is not a special form
> A nil return is a regular ITR filed using the applicable form (ITR-1, ITR-2, etc.), with the resulting tax payable shown as zero. It is not a separate or special type of return.

## Three Situations That Lead to a Nil Return

| Situation | Example | Result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Income below basic exemption limit | Income of Rs. 2.5 lakh under old regime | Tax = nil |
| Income taxable but offset by deductions | Rs. 7 lakh income, full deductions under 80C, 80D, etc. | Tax = nil |
| Tax offset by rebate under Section 87A | Rs. 7 lakh income under new regime | Tax = nil after rebate |
| Tax payable but fully covered by TDS already deducted | Rs. 5 lakh income, employer deducted Rs. 15,000 TDS | Net payable = nil |

## Is Filing a Nil Return Mandatory?

### When Nil Return Filing Is Mandatory

Under Section 139(1), ITR filing is compulsory if your **gross income** (before deductions) exceeds the basic exemption limit, regardless of whether tax is payable:

- **Old regime:** Gross income above Rs. 2.5 lakh (below 60 years), Rs. 3 lakh (60-80 years), Rs. 5 lakh (above 80 years)
- **New regime:** Gross income above Rs. 3 lakh (for FY 2024-25 onwards)

Additionally, filing is mandatory regardless of income level if any of these conditions are met:

| Trigger | Threshold |
| --- | --- |
| Deposited in savings account | More than Rs. 50 lakh in a year |
| Paid electricity bill | More than Rs. 1 lakh in a year |
| Spent on foreign travel | More than Rs. 2 lakh in a year |
| TDS/TCS deducted from income | More than Rs. 25,000 (Rs. 50,000 for senior citizens) |
| Owns foreign assets or has foreign income | Any amount |
| Is a company or firm | Always mandatory |

### When Filing Is Optional (But Still Advisable)

If your income is below the basic exemption limit and none of the above triggers apply, filing is technically voluntary. But experts almost universally recommend filing anyway.

## Why File a Nil Return Even When Not Mandatory?

### 1. Proof of Income

A filed ITR serves as official proof of income, particularly useful when applying for home loans, personal loans, credit cards, visa applications, and government contract tenders.

### 2. Carry Forward of Losses

If you incurred a capital loss or business loss during the year, you **cannot carry it forward** to offset against future gains unless you file the ITR by the due date. A nil return with losses reported preserves this benefit.

### 3. Claiming Refund of TDS

If your employer or bank has deducted TDS but your overall tax liability is nil, you can only claim a refund of that TDS by filing an ITR. **The refund is not automatic.**

### 4. Establishing Financial Track Record

Lenders, embassies, and government agencies consider filing history when evaluating trustworthiness. Consistent ITR filing builds a verifiable financial identity.

### 5. Avoiding Future Complications

The Income Tax Department increasingly uses data analytics and third-party reporting (from banks, registrars, and market intermediaries) to identify high-value transactions. If you have made significant financial moves but have not filed, you may receive a notice.

## How to File a Nil Return

The process is identical to any regular ITR filing:

1. **Collect all documents** — Form 16, bank interest certificates, investment proofs
2. **Log in to the Income Tax e-Filing Portal** — incometax.gov.in
3. **Select the applicable ITR form** — ITR-1 for most salaried individuals
4. **Select the correct Assessment Year**
5. **Fill in income details and deductions** — verify that tax payable = zero
6. **e-Verify** — using Aadhaar OTP, net banking, or DSC
7. **Keep the ITR-V acknowledgement** for your records

The entire process takes **15–30 minutes** for salaried individuals with simple income structures.

## Practical Example: Sanjay's Nil Return

Sanjay, age 32, is a junior sales executive. Gross salary for FY 2025-26: Rs. 5.5 lakh. He is under the old tax regime.

| Computation | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Gross salary | Rs. 5,50,000 |
| Less: Standard deduction | Rs. 50,000 |
| Less: 80C (PPF) | Rs. 1,50,000 |
| Less: 80D (health insurance) | Rs. 25,000 |
| Net taxable income | Rs. 3,25,000 |
| Tax on Rs. 3,25,000 at slab rates | Rs. 3,750 |
| Less: Section 87A rebate | Rs. 3,750 |
| **Tax payable** | **Rs. 0** |

Sanjay must still file because his gross income (Rs. 5.5 lakh) exceeds Rs. 2.5 lakh. He files ITR-1, confirms nil tax, and e-verifies.

> [!WARN] Late filing has real costs
> A nil return must be filed by **July 31** (for individuals, non-audit cases). Filing between July 31 and December 31 is a belated nil return and attracts a late fee of **Rs. 1,000** (income up to Rs. 5 lakh) or **Rs. 5,000** (income above Rs. 5 lakh) under Section 234F.
> Losses **cannot** be carried forward if the return is filed late — even if it is a nil return. This is one of the most financially damaging consequences of missing the deadline.

## FAQs

### Q1. My income is Rs. 2.2 lakh plus Rs. 12,000 bank interest. Do I need to file?

Your total income is Rs. 2.32 lakh — still below the basic exemption limit of Rs. 2.5 lakh (old regime) or Rs. 3 lakh (new regime). Mandatory filing is not triggered. You may still choose to file voluntarily for documentation purposes.

### Q2. I have a capital loss of Rs. 40,000 from selling mutual funds. My income is below the taxable limit. Should I file?

**Yes.** File a nil return and declare the capital loss within the return. Only losses declared in a timely ITR can be carried forward to future assessment years. If you skip filing, that Rs. 40,000 loss is **forfeited permanently** and cannot be used to offset future capital gains.

### Q3. The IT department sent me an intimation under Section 143(1) after my nil return. Should I be worried?

Not at all. An intimation under Section 143(1) is a routine communication from the CPC after your return is processed. If it says "no demand, no refund," it means the department has accepted your nil return as filed. Review it for any discrepancies and respond within 30 days if you disagree.

### Q4. Can I file a nil return with zero income — for example, if I was between jobs?

Yes. A nil return can be filed even with zero income. If you were employed earlier, had TDS deducted, and then had no income for part of the year, filing a nil return ensures that any excess TDS is refunded.

### Q5. My spouse has no income. Can they file a nil return for visa purposes?

Your spouse can file a nil return independently in their own name using their own PAN. Visa applications from embassies (UK, Schengen, US) often request ITR acknowledgements for all adult applicants. A nil return with zero income is valid as a supporting document.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Income from Other Sources — The Residual Head Explained
articles.excerpt: The fifth head of income under the Income Tax Act catches everything that doesn't fit elsewhere — FD interest, dividends, gifts, lottery winnings, online gaming prizes, subletting income, and more. This guide covers what's taxable, the gift tax rules, available deductions, and special flat rates.
articles.content:
## Introduction

Four heads of income exist to cover specific economic activity: employment, property ownership, business activity, and investment sales. But what about interest from a bank deposit? What about money received as a gift? What about the cash prize you won in a quiz contest? Or the dividend your shares paid last quarter?

All of these find a home under the fifth and final head: **Income from Other Sources (IFOS)**. It is the most versatile and often misunderstood of the five heads, because almost any income not covered elsewhere ends up here.

## Legal Basis

Income from Other Sources is governed by **Sections 56 to 59** of the Income Tax Act, 1961. Section 56 begins with a clear principle: income of every kind which is not chargeable to income tax under any of the heads specified in Section 14, items A to E, shall be chargeable under the head "Income from Other Sources."

In simpler terms: if it does not belong to Salaries, House Property, PGBP, or Capital Gains — it comes here.

## What Falls Under Income from Other Sources?

### Category A: Always Taxable Under IFOS

| Type of Income | Tax Rate |
| --- | --- |
| Interest on savings account | Slab rates (deduction u/s 80TTA up to Rs. 10,000) |
| Interest on fixed deposits (FDs) | Slab rates |
| Interest on recurring deposits (RDs) | Slab rates |
| Interest on NSC, bonds, debentures | Slab rates |
| Dividend from shares (domestic or foreign) | Slab rates (post FY 2020-21) |
| Family pension received after pensioner's death | Slab rates (1/3rd or Rs. 15,000 exempt, whichever is lower) |
| Income from subletting (not property owner) | Slab rates |
| Agricultural income from foreign country | Slab rates |
| Ground rent | Slab rates |

### Category B: Specifically Included Under Section 56(2)

These are incomes the law specifically brings under IFOS to prevent tax avoidance:

| Income Type | Section | Key Condition | Tax Rate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Gifts (cash or kind) from non-relatives | 56(2)(x) | Exceeds Rs. 50,000 in aggregate | Slab rates on full amount |
| Immovable property received without consideration | 56(2)(x) | Stamp value exceeds Rs. 50,000 | Slab rates on stamp value |
| Shares received at below fair market value | 56(2)(viib) | FMV exceeds issue price | Excess taxed in issuer's hands |
| Lottery, gambling, card games, horse races | 56(2)(ib) | Any amount | 30% flat |
| Online gaming winnings | 115BBJ | Applicable from FY 2023-24 | 30% flat |
| Interest from income tax refund | 56 | Any amount | Slab rates |
| Insurance maturity proceeds (taxable portion) | 56 | When Section 10(10D) exemption is not available | Slab rates |

## The Gift Tax Rules Explained

### What Is a "Gift" for Tax Purposes?

Any money, immovable property, or movable property received without consideration (or for inadequate consideration) is treated as a gift.

| Gift Amount (Aggregate in FY) | Tax Treatment |
| --- | --- |
| Up to Rs. 50,000 from all sources | Fully exempt |
| Above Rs. 50,000 (cash) | Full amount taxable (not just the excess) |
| Immovable property at below stamp duty value | Difference between stamp value and consideration is taxable |
| Movable property at below FMV | Difference between FMV and consideration is taxable |

> [!IMPORTANT] Full amount taxable — not just the excess
> If you receive Rs. 60,000 cash from a friend (non-relative), the **entire Rs. 60,000** is taxable — not just the Rs. 10,000 above the threshold. This surprises many taxpayers.

### Who Is a "Relative" (Gifts Between Relatives Are Exempt)?

- Spouse
- Siblings (brothers and sisters of the individual)
- Siblings of spouse
- Siblings of parents
- Lineal ascendants/descendants and their spouses (parents, grandparents, children, grandchildren)

> [!TIP] Example — Vikram's wedding gift
> Vikram receives Rs. 70,000 cash from his college friend (not a relative). The **entire Rs. 70,000 is taxable** under IFOS at slab rates.
> However, if the same Rs. 70,000 came from his maternal uncle, it would be **fully exempt** — because the maternal uncle is the sibling of a parent and hence a relative.

## Deductions Available Under Income from Other Sources

| Deduction | Applicable To | Limit |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Section 80TTA | Interest on savings bank account (individuals and HUFs) | Up to Rs. 10,000 |
| Section 80TTB | Interest on all deposits (senior citizens 60+) | Up to Rs. 50,000 |
| Commission or remuneration for collecting interest on securities | Individuals earning interest as business activity | Actual amount |
| Repairs, depreciation, insurance for plant/machinery let on hire | Owners who earn hire charges | Actual (reasonable) |

> [!WARN] No deductions on lottery, gaming, or gambling
> No deduction is available against lottery winnings, online gaming, or gambling income. These are taxed **gross at 30%** with no expense deduction allowed.

## Special Flat Tax Rates

| Income Type | Flat Rate | Surcharge / Cess Applicable? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Lottery / puzzle winnings | 30% | Yes |
| Horse racing income | 30% | Yes |
| Online gaming winnings (Section 115BBJ) | 30% | Yes |
| Card game / other gambling | 30% | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT] No basic exemption against special-rate income
> If your only income is Rs. 3 lakh from a lottery win and you are below 60, you **cannot** claim the Rs. 2.5 lakh basic exemption against lottery income. The Rs. 3 lakh is taxed at 30% flat on the gross amount.

## Practical Example: Sumit's Full Computation

Sumit, age 35, has the following income in FY 2025-26:

| Source | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Salary (after standard deduction) | Rs. 9,00,000 |
| Interest on FD (HDFC Bank) | Rs. 40,000 |
| Interest on savings account (SBI) | Rs. 12,000 |
| Dividend from Infosys shares | Rs. 8,000 |
| Cash received from friend as gift | Rs. 60,000 |
| **Total IFOS income** | **Rs. 1,20,000** |

**IFOS breakdown:**
- FD interest Rs. 40,000 — taxable at slab rates
- Savings interest Rs. 12,000 — Rs. 10,000 exempt under 80TTA; **net taxable: Rs. 2,000**
- Dividend Rs. 8,000 — taxable at slab rates
- Gift from friend Rs. 60,000 — full amount taxable (exceeds Rs. 50,000 threshold)

**Total IFOS taxable: Rs. 1,10,000** (after 80TTA deduction). This is added to his salary of Rs. 9 lakh, and the combined income is taxed at applicable slab rates.

> [!NOTE] Dividends and family pension
> Since FY 2020-21, dividends are taxable in the hands of the shareholder. Companies deduct TDS at 10% before paying dividends if the annual amount exceeds Rs. 5,000. Declare the gross dividend in your ITR and claim credit for TDS.
> Family pension (pension received by the family of a deceased government employee) is taxed under IFOS — **not under Salaries**. A standard deduction of one-third of the pension or Rs. 15,000, whichever is lower, is available.

## FAQs

### Q1. My parents gifted me Rs. 5 lakh for buying furniture. Is this taxable?

Gifts received from parents are **fully exempt** regardless of the amount, because parents are specifically listed as relatives under Section 56(2)(x). No disclosure is needed as taxable income in your ITR.

### Q2. I won Rs. 1 lakh in an online fantasy sports platform. How is this taxed?

Online gaming winnings, including fantasy sports, are taxed at **30% flat** under Section 115BBJ from FY 2023-24. The platform is required to deduct TDS at 30% on net winnings under Section 194BA. Declare the gross winnings in your ITR and claim TDS credit.

### Q3. I sublet a room to a tenant but I am a tenant myself, not the owner. Is this house property income?

No. Income from subletting is specifically excluded from the House Property head because you are not the owner. It is taxable under **Income from Other Sources** at your applicable slab rate. You may deduct the proportionate rent you pay for the subletting portion while computing this income.

### Q4. My PPF account matured and I received Rs. 12 lakh. Is this IFOS?

No. Maturity proceeds from a **Public Provident Fund (PPF)** account are fully exempt under Section 10(11). This amount does not appear in your ITR as income at all — neither under IFOS nor any other head.

### Q5. I received a gold chain worth Rs. 45,000 as a Diwali gift from my employer. IFOS or Salary?

Gifts from an employer are taxed as a **perquisite under the Salary head** (Section 17(2)), not under IFOS. However, there is an annual exemption of Rs. 5,000 for employer gifts. So Rs. 5,000 of the chain's value is exempt, and Rs. 40,000 would be added to your taxable salary as a perquisite.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: What Is ITR? Everything a Beginner Needs to Know
articles.excerpt: An ITR is your annual financial disclosure to the government — reporting income, claiming deductions, and settling the tax account. This beginner's guide covers why you must file, which ITR form to use, what goes into each schedule, step-by-step filing, and what happens after you submit.
articles.content:
## Introduction

Every year, the government of India asks its citizens a simple question: here is the tax system, here are the rules — how much did you earn, how much tax did you pay, and do we need to settle up? Your answer to that question is your **Income Tax Return**, commonly called ITR.

An ITR is not just a tax payment form. It is a comprehensive financial disclosure — a structured document where you report your income from all sources, claim deductions and exemptions, state the taxes already paid (through TDS, advance tax, or self-assessment), and compute whether you owe more tax or are owed a refund.

## ITR: The Full Picture

| Aspect | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Full Form | Income Tax Return |
| Filed With | Income Tax Department of India |
| Filing Portal | incometax.gov.in |
| Governing Law | Section 139 of the Income Tax Act, 1961 |
| Who Must File | Persons whose income exceeds the basic exemption limit and others as prescribed |
| Filing Period | For FY 2025-26: April 1, 2026 to July 31, 2026 (without late fee) |
| Assessment Year | AY 2026-27 for FY 2025-26 income |
| Verification Method | e-Verify via Aadhaar OTP, net banking, or physical ITR-V to CPC Bengaluru |

## Why Is Filing ITR Important?

### Legal Compliance

If your income exceeds the basic exemption limit, you are legally required to file. Non-filing can invite notices under Section 148 or even prosecution under Section 276CC for wilful default.

### Getting Your TDS Refund

If total TDS deducted exceeds your actual tax liability (common for people with deductions and rebates), the only way to reclaim the excess is by filing an ITR and requesting a refund.

### Loan and Credit Applications

Banks require ITRs (typically for 2-3 years) when processing home loans, business loans, and high-limit credit cards.

### Visa Applications

Countries like the USA, UK, Canada, Australia, and Schengen nations often ask for proof of financial stability. ITRs serve as the most credible evidence of your earnings.

### Carrying Forward Losses

Capital losses and business losses can only be carried forward to future years if you file the ITR **before the due date**.

## Types of ITR Forms

| Form | Who Should File |
| --- | --- |
| ITR-1 (Sahaj) | Resident individuals with salary, one house property, and other sources income up to Rs. 50 lakh |
| ITR-2 | Individuals/HUFs with capital gains, more than one property, or foreign assets/income (no business income) |
| ITR-3 | Individuals/HUFs with business or professional income (detailed books required) |
| ITR-4 (Sugam) | Individuals/HUFs/firms opting for presumptive taxation under 44AD/44ADA/44AE |
| ITR-5 | Firms, LLPs, AOPs, BOIs |
| ITR-6 | Companies (other than those claiming exemption under Section 11) |
| ITR-7 | Trusts, political parties, charitable institutions, research associations |

### Choosing the Right Form — Common Scenarios

| Your Situation | Correct Form |
| --- | --- |
| Salaried, single property, FD interest | ITR-1 |
| Salaried with capital gains from stocks | ITR-2 |
| Freelancer, doctor in private practice | ITR-3 or ITR-4 (if opting for presumptive) |
| Small business owner with turnover under Rs. 3 crore | ITR-4 |
| Partnership firm | ITR-5 |

## What Information Goes Into an ITR?

| Schedule | What You Disclose |
| --- | --- |
| Schedule S | Salary income breakup |
| Schedule HP | House property income or loss |
| Schedule PGBP | Business or professional income |
| Schedule CG | Capital gains (short-term and long-term) |
| Schedule OS | Income from other sources |
| Schedule VIA | Deductions under Chapter VI-A (80C, 80D, 80G, etc.) |
| Schedule TI | Total income computation |
| Schedule TTI | Tax on total income computation |
| Schedule TDS | TDS details (from Form 26AS / AIS) |
| Schedule IT | Advance tax and self-assessment tax paid |
| Schedule FA | Foreign assets (if any) |
| Schedule AL | Assets and liabilities (if income above Rs. 50 lakh) |

## Step-by-Step: How to File an ITR

1. **Collect documents** — Form 16, Form 26AS, AIS, bank statements, investment proofs
2. **Log in to incometax.gov.in** with your PAN
3. **Navigate to "File Income Tax Return"**
4. **Select AY and ITR form**
5. **Review pre-filled data** — from Form 26AS and AIS; do not accept blindly
6. **Add any income not pre-filled** — FD interest, rent, capital gains
7. **Claim all applicable deductions**
8. **Verify tax computation** — check for refund or balance payable
9. **Pay any balance tax** via Challan 280 (self-assessment tax)
10. **e-Verify the return** within 30 days of filing

## Practical Example: Ramesh's ITR at a Glance

Ramesh, 40, is a salaried engineer in Pune with some investments.

| Item | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Gross salary | Rs. 14,00,000 |
| Less: Standard deduction | Rs. 75,000 |
| Net salary | Rs. 13,25,000 |
| Interest on FD | Rs. 35,000 |
| Total income before deductions | Rs. 13,60,000 |
| Less: 80C (PPF + ELSS + PF) | Rs. 1,50,000 |
| Less: 80D (health insurance) | Rs. 25,000 |
| Net taxable income | Rs. 11,85,000 |
| Tax computed at slab rates | Rs. 1,72,500 (approx.) |
| Health and education cess @ 4% | Rs. 6,900 |
| Total tax | Rs. 1,79,400 |
| TDS deducted by employer | Rs. 1,70,000 |
| TDS by bank on FD | Rs. 3,500 |
| **Self-assessment tax payable** | **Rs. 5,900** |

Ramesh pays Rs. 5,900 via Challan 280 before filing, then files ITR-1 and e-verifies.

## What Happens After You File?

| Event | Timeline |
| --- | --- |
| Acknowledgement generated (ITR-V) | Immediately after submission |
| e-Verification required | Within 30 days of filing |
| Processing by CPC | Typically 15-45 days after e-verification |
| Intimation under Section 143(1) | Issued after processing |
| Refund credited (if applicable) | Usually within 30-60 days of processing |
| Scrutiny notice under 143(2) | Within 3 months of filing date (rare) |

> [!IMPORTANT] An unverified ITR is treated as never filed
> e-Verification is not optional — it is a mandatory step. If you cannot e-verify online, send the signed physical ITR-V acknowledgement by **ordinary post** (not courier) to CPC, Bengaluru within 30 days.

> [!WARN] Do not blindly accept pre-filled data
> Pre-filled data on the portal (from Form 26AS, AIS, and TIS) may contain errors. Cross-check with your actual Form 16, bank statements, and broker reports before accepting. Accepting incorrect pre-filled data is preventable — but fixing a mismatch notice after the fact is not.

## FAQs

### Q1. I filed my ITR but forgot to include my FD interest. Can I correct this?

Yes. File a **revised return under Section 139(5)** to correct any omission. The revised return must be filed by **December 31 of the Assessment Year**. So if you filed for AY 2026-27 in July 2026 and later realised you missed FD interest, you can revise anytime up to December 31, 2026.

### Q2. My FD is still running. Do I need to report interest I have not received yet?

Yes. Interest accrued on an FD is taxable on an **accrual basis** each year, even if you do not withdraw it. Your bank issues Form 16A reflecting the accrued interest and TDS deducted. Use this to fill your IFOS schedule.

### Q3. Does India have joint returns like the USA?

India does **not** have joint income tax returns. Every individual files independently. A homemaker with no income has no mandatory filing obligation (unless specific high-value transaction thresholds are triggered).

### Q4. I filed but did not e-verify. It has been 35 days. What happens?

If 30 days have passed without e-verification, your return is technically treated as invalid. e-Verify immediately, even if late — the department typically accepts late verification but treats the return as filed on the **date of verification** (not the original filing date), which affects loss carry-forward eligibility.

### Q5. I received an intimation under 143(1) showing a demand of Rs. 2,500 that I disagree with. What should I do?

Do not ignore it. Log in to the e-filing portal, go to "Pending Actions," and respond within **30 days**. If you believe the demand is incorrect, file a rectification request under Section 154. If you agree, pay via Challan 280. Ignoring the demand leads to interest under Section 220(2) and possible recovery action.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Income Tax Slabs for FY 2025-26 — New Regime vs. Old Regime
articles.excerpt: Budget 2025 revised the new regime slabs significantly. This guide covers the complete slab tables for both regimes, surcharge rates, a break-even comparison at multiple income levels, the Rs. 12.75 lakh zero-tax benefit for salaried individuals, and which deductions still work under the new regime.
articles.content:
## Introduction

Tax slab announcements in the Union Budget are one of the most widely watched events in India's financial calendar. For FY 2025-26, Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman introduced significant changes to the new tax regime, making it even more attractive for a majority of individual taxpayers — while the old regime continues to exist for those who prefer to claim deductions.

If you have been filing under the old regime because of your home loan, PPF, and insurance deductions, this may be the year to run the numbers again.

## Two Parallel Tax Regimes

India currently operates two parallel income tax regimes for individuals:

| Feature | Old Regime | New Regime (Default) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Tax slabs | Fewer slabs, higher rates at lower income levels | More slabs, lower rates across the board |
| Deductions (80C, 80D, HRA, etc.) | Available | Not available (except specific ones) |
| Standard deduction | Rs. 50,000 (salaried) | Rs. 75,000 (salaried, from FY 2024-25) |
| Default status | Opt-in required | Default for all taxpayers |
| Surcharge on income above Rs. 5 crore | 37% | 25% (capped) |
| Best for | High deduction claimers | Those with minimal deductions |

## New Tax Regime Slabs for FY 2025-26 (AY 2026-27)

Budget 2025 revised the new regime slabs significantly:

| Income Slab | Tax Rate |
| --- | --- |
| Up to Rs. 4,00,000 | Nil |
| Rs. 4,00,001 to Rs. 8,00,000 | 5% |
| Rs. 8,00,001 to Rs. 12,00,000 | 10% |
| Rs. 12,00,001 to Rs. 16,00,000 | 15% |
| Rs. 16,00,001 to Rs. 20,00,000 | 20% |
| Rs. 20,00,001 to Rs. 24,00,000 | 25% |
| Above Rs. 24,00,000 | 30% |

> [!IMPORTANT] Zero tax up to Rs. 12.75 lakh for salaried individuals
> Under Section 87A, individuals with taxable income up to **Rs. 12 lakh** effectively pay zero tax under the new regime. Salaried individuals with gross income up to **Rs. 12.75 lakh** (Rs. 12 lakh + Rs. 75,000 standard deduction) also pay zero tax.

## Old Tax Regime Slabs for FY 2025-26

The old regime slabs remain unchanged:

| Income Slab | Tax Rate (Below 60 Years) |
| --- | --- |
| Up to Rs. 2,50,000 | Nil |
| Rs. 2,50,001 to Rs. 5,00,000 | 5% |
| Rs. 5,00,001 to Rs. 10,00,000 | 20% |
| Above Rs. 10,00,000 | 30% |

- **Senior citizens (60 to 79 years):** basic exemption is Rs. 3 lakh
- **Super senior citizens (80 years and above):** basic exemption is Rs. 5 lakh

## Surcharge on High Income

| Income Range | Old Regime Surcharge | New Regime Surcharge |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Rs. 50 lakh to Rs. 1 crore | 10% | 10% |
| Rs. 1 crore to Rs. 2 crore | 15% | 15% |
| Rs. 2 crore to Rs. 5 crore | 25% | 25% |
| Above Rs. 5 crore | 37% | **25% (capped)** |

**Health and Education Cess: 4%** on (tax + surcharge) — applicable under both regimes.

## Which Regime Is Better? Break-Even Analysis

| Annual Income | Old Regime Tax (with Rs. 3L deductions) | New Regime Tax | Verdict |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Rs. 6 lakh | ~Rs. 0 (87A rebate) | Rs. 0 (87A rebate) | Tie |
| Rs. 10 lakh | ~Rs. 54,600 | ~Rs. 52,000 | New regime slightly better |
| Rs. 12 lakh | ~Rs. 78,000 | Rs. 0 (87A rebate, salaried) | New regime wins decisively |
| Rs. 15 lakh | ~Rs. 1,29,480 | ~Rs. 1,05,000 | New regime wins |
| Rs. 20 lakh | ~Rs. 3,37,400 | ~Rs. 2,73,000 | New regime better |
| Rs. 30 lakh | ~Rs. 6,78,600 (with Rs. 4L deductions) | ~Rs. 4,75,000 | New regime better |

> [!NOTE] When does the old regime still win?
> The old regime typically wins only when deductions (80C + 80D + HRA + home loan interest + NPS) are very high — often **Rs. 4.5 lakh to Rs. 5 lakh and above** for income in the Rs. 15-20 lakh range.

## Practical Example: Neha at Rs. 15 Lakh Income

Neha, 35, works at a private firm. Gross salary Rs. 15 lakh.

**Under New Regime:**
Taxable income after Rs. 75,000 standard deduction = Rs. 14,25,000
Tax = Nil on Rs. 4L + 5% on Rs. 4L (Rs. 20,000) + 10% on Rs. 4L (Rs. 40,000) + 15% on Rs. 2.25L (Rs. 33,750) = Rs. 93,750 + 4% cess = **Rs. 97,500**

**Under Old Regime** (with Rs. 3.5L deductions: 80C Rs. 1.5L, 80D Rs. 25K, HRA Rs. 1.5L, standard deduction Rs. 50K):
Taxable income = Rs. 15L − Rs. 3.5L = Rs. 11.5L
Total (approximate with cess) = **Rs. 1,29,000**

**New regime saves Neha Rs. 31,500 — she should switch.**

## Deductions Still Available Under the New Regime

| Deduction / Exemption | Available in New Regime? |
| --- | --- |
| Standard deduction (Rs. 75,000) | Yes |
| NPS employer contribution (Section 80CCD(2)) | Yes (up to 14% for govt employees, 10% for others) |
| Gratuity exemption (Section 10(10)) | Yes |
| Leave encashment exemption (Section 10(10AA)) | Yes |
| HRA exemption | No |
| Section 80C (PPF, ELSS, LIC, PF) | No |
| Section 80D (health insurance) | No |
| Home loan interest (Section 24b) | No (except let-out property) |
| Section 80G (donations) | No |

> [!IMPORTANT] Switching rules differ for salaried vs. self-employed
> **Salaried employees** can switch between old and new regime every year.
> **Self-employed individuals and business owners** can switch only once (from new to old), and if they switch back to new, they cannot switch again (with limited exceptions).
> The new regime is the **default** from FY 2024-25. To use the old regime, you must explicitly opt for it via Form 10-IEA while filing your ITR.

## FAQs

### Q1. I have a home loan with Rs. 2 lakh interest and invest Rs. 1.5 lakh in PPF. Should I shift to new regime?

With Rs. 1.5L (80C) + Rs. 2L (home loan) + Rs. 75K (standard deduction under old) = Rs. 4.25 lakh in deductions, the old regime might still be competitive for incomes around Rs. 12-18 lakh. For income of Rs. 12 lakh or below, the Section 87A rebate makes tax zero under the new regime regardless — the old regime rarely wins there.

### Q2. My employer is deducting TDS under the new regime by default. Can I claim old regime benefits while filing?

Yes. You can opt for the old regime at the time of filing your ITR, even if your employer defaulted to the new regime for TDS. Any excess TDS deducted will be refunded. For efficiency, inform your employer at the start of the year and submit Form 10-IEA or a declaration.

### Q3. I am a retired government employee receiving pension. Which regime applies to me?

Pension from a former employer is taxed as salary — retired individuals can choose either regime. Since retirees often do not have large 80C investments or HRA, the new regime frequently works out better. However, factor in the Rs. 50,000 deduction under 80TTB for interest income if you are above 60.

### Q4. I earned Rs. 12 lakh salary plus Rs. 50,000 FD interest. Does Section 87A still apply?

Total income = Rs. 12.5 lakh. After standard deduction of Rs. 75,000, taxable income = Rs. 11.75 lakh. Since this is below Rs. 12 lakh, the **full 87A rebate applies and tax is nil** under the new regime.

### Q5. Are NRI taxpayers eligible for the new tax regime?

Yes, NRIs can opt for the new tax regime. However, the **Section 87A rebate is not available to NRIs**, which is a significant difference from resident taxpayers. Certain exemptions specifically applicable to residents (like 80TTA/80TTB) also do not apply to NRIs regardless of regime.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: GST on Event Management Services: Rate, SAC Code & Impact
articles.excerpt: Understand GST on event management services in India: 18% rate, SAC codes, composite vs mixed supply rules, real-world examples, and ITC implications for organisers and clients.
articles.content:
## Introduction

India's events industry has grown into a multi-billion-dollar sector, encompassing everything from corporate conferences and product launches to weddings and music festivals. With GST replacing the old service tax regime, event management companies now operate under a unified tax structure that affects both their cost structures and client billing.

Whether you are an event organiser, a client commissioning a corporate event, or a vendor supplying services to the events ecosystem, understanding GST on event management services is essential for accurate billing, compliance, and budgeting.

## GST Rate Table for Event Management Services

| Service Type | GST Rate | SAC Code | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Event Management Services (General) | 18% | 998596 | Covers planning, execution, and coordination |
| Entertainment Events (Cultural/Sports) | 18% | 998596 | Including ticketed events |
| Exhibition and Trade Fair Organisation | 18% | 998596 | B2B exhibitions and expos |
| Event Ticketing (Online Platforms) | 18% | 998599 | Platform fee on ticket sales |
| Supply of Sound/Light Equipment (Rental) | 18% | 997319 | Equipment hiring for events |
| Catering at Events | 5% / 18% | 996331 | 5% if no ITC; 18% with ITC |
| Hotel Banquet Halls (Room Rental) | 18% | 996311 | For event venue booking |
| Photography and Videography at Events | 18% | 998387 | Creative services |
| Fireworks Display Services | 18% | 998596 | Classified under event support |

## SAC Codes for Event Management

| SAC Code | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 998596 | Event management and planning services |
| 998599 | Other recreational and amusement services |
