| 998387 | Videography and photography services |
| 997319 | Equipment rental services (AV/tech) |
| 996331 | Catering and food supply services |
| 996311 | Accommodation and venue rental |

## Applicability

- Any registered event management company providing services to clients (B2B or B2C) must charge **18% GST**.
- Freelance event planners with aggregate turnover above Rs. 20 lakh (Rs. 10 lakh in special category states) must register and collect GST.
- Companies organising events outside India must evaluate place of supply rules under IGST.
- **Composite supplies** (e.g., venue + catering + decor as a package) are taxed at the rate of the principal supply.
- **Mixed supplies** are taxed at the highest GST rate among all components.

> [!IMPORTANT] Composite vs. Mixed Supply — get clarity upfront
> If a wedding planner bundles venue, catering, and decor as a single package, the entire package is taxed at 18% (the principal supply rate). If items are independently priced but packaged together, the highest rate in the mix applies. Misclassification can significantly change your tax liability.

## Real-World Examples

### Example A: Corporate Conference

A Mumbai-based event management firm organises a two-day corporate conference. The bill includes:

| Component | Amount | GST Rate |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Venue booking | Rs. 2,00,000 | 18% |
| AV equipment rental | Rs. 80,000 | 18% |
| Catering (no ITC option) | Rs. 1,20,000 | 5% |
| Coordination fees | Rs. 1,00,000 | 18% |

Each component is billed separately with its applicable GST rate. B2B clients can claim ITC on venue, AV, and coordination invoices.

### Example B: Wedding Planning Package

A Jaipur-based wedding planner charges Rs. 5,00,000 as a comprehensive package. Since this is a **composite supply** where event planning is the principal supply, the entire package is taxed at 18%. The client pays Rs. 5,90,000 inclusive of GST.

## Industry Impact

- **Increased tax burden on end clients:** The 18% GST rate is higher than the erstwhile service tax rate of 14.5%, marginally increasing costs for clients.
- **ITC benefits for businesses:** B2B clients can claim input tax credit on event management invoices, reducing their net tax cost.
- **Compliance complexity:** Event companies managing multi-vendor events must ensure all vendors are GST-registered to maintain a clean ITC chain.
- **Improved transparency:** GST has eliminated the cascading effect of multiple state-level taxes on events spanning multiple locations.
- **Freelancer registration burden:** Independent event coordinators who cross the threshold must register, file returns, and maintain records.

## Key Insights

- Always obtain GST-registered invoices from sub-contractors to claim ITC
- For interstate events, IGST applies — always verify the place of supply carefully
- Reverse Charge Mechanism (RCM) may apply when hiring unregistered service providers
- TDS under GST (Section 51) applies if the event is organised for a government or specified entity

## FAQs

### Q1. Is GST applicable on weddings managed by private planners?

Yes. If the wedding planner is GST-registered, they must charge 18% GST on their service fees. Individual families hosting personal events are not liable to register for GST, but the service provider must be compliant.

### Q2. Can a company claim ITC on expenses incurred for a business event?

Yes, subject to Section 17(5) restrictions. ITC is available on venue, AV equipment, and coordination services. However, ITC on food and beverages served at events is generally blocked unless it is a mandatory part of the service offering.

### Q3. What is the GST treatment for a ticketed public concert?

Ticketed entertainment events attract 18% GST on the ticket value. The event organiser must collect and remit this tax. If the event is organised by a state government or local authority, an exemption may apply.

### Q4. Do event management companies need to issue SAC-wise invoices?

Yes. Businesses with turnover above Rs. 5 crore must report 6-digit SAC codes on all invoices. Smaller businesses may use 4-digit codes. Using the correct SAC code is critical for accurate filing and avoiding GST notices.

### Q5. Is there GST on virtual events and webinars?

Yes. Virtual events and webinars are classified as electronically supplied services and attract 18% GST. If the event is provided to overseas clients with no establishment in India, it may be treated as an export of service — making it zero-rated subject to conditions.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: GST Rates in India 2026: Updated List
articles.excerpt: A comprehensive guide to updated GST rates in India for 2026, covering all slabs for goods and services, composition scheme rates, and practical examples for businesses and individuals.
articles.content:
## Introduction

India's Goods and Services Tax framework operates on a multi-slab rate structure, designed to ensure that essential commodities are taxed minimally while luxury goods and services carry a higher burden. As of 2026, the GST Council has made several revisions to align rates with economic realities, reduce litigation, and improve compliance.

This comprehensive guide covers updated GST rates across goods and services, the rationale behind the slab system, key changes implemented in recent council meetings, and practical examples to help businesses and individuals understand their tax obligations.

## The GST Rate Structure: An Overview

India follows a four-tier rate structure for goods and a broadly uniform rate for services. There is also a zero-rated category for exports and a special composition scheme for small businesses.

| GST Slab | Applicable To | Examples |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 0% (Exempt) | Essential commodities and basic necessities | Fresh vegetables, milk, eggs, contraceptives |
| 5% | Items of common use | Packaged food, coal, household goods |
| 12% | Processed goods and mid-range services | Frozen meat, butter, ayurvedic medicines |
| 18% | Standard rate for most goods and services | Electronics, financial services, IT services |
| 28% | Luxury, sin goods and demerit goods | Cars above 1500cc, tobacco, aerated drinks |
| 28% + Cess | High-end luxury and sin goods | SUVs, pan masala, cigarettes |

## GST Rates: Key Goods (2026)

| Category | Item | GST Rate | HSN Code |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Food and Agriculture | Fresh vegetables and fruits | 0% | Chapter 7 and 8 |
| Food and Agriculture | Milk (non-branded or branded) | 0% | 0401 |
| Food and Agriculture | Butter and ghee | 12% | 0405 |
| Food and Agriculture | Ice cream | 18% | 2105 |
| Beverages | Aerated drinks (cold drinks) | 28% + 12% Cess | 2202 |
| Beverages | Packaged drinking water (above 20L) | Nil | 2201 |
| Beverages | Fruit juices | 12% | 2009 |
| Pharma | Life-saving medicines | 5% | 3004 |
| Pharma | Cosmetics and toiletries | 18% | 3304 |
| Textiles | Cotton fabrics | 5% | Chapter 52 |
| Textiles | Man-made fibre | 12% | Chapter 54 |
| Automobiles | Small cars below 1200cc (petrol) | 28% + 1% Cess | 8703 |
| Automobiles | SUVs above 1500cc, over 4m, 170mm GC | 28% + 22% Cess | 8703 |
| Electronics | Mobile phones | 18% | 8517 |
| Electronics | Solar panels | 12% | 8541 |
| Fuel | Coal | 5% | 2701 |
| Real Estate | Under-construction property | 5% (no ITC) | 9954 |
| Real Estate | Affordable housing | 1% (no ITC) | 9954 |

## GST Rates: Key Services (2026)

| Service Category | GST Rate | SAC Code |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Healthcare (hospitals, doctors) | 0% | 9993 |
| Education (schools, colleges) | 0% | 9992 |
| Transportation of goods by road (GTA) | 5% (no ITC) or 12% (with ITC) | 9965 |
| Air travel (economy class) | 5% | 9964 |
| Air travel (business class) | 12% | 9964 |
| Hotel accommodation (tariff below Rs. 7,500) | 12% | 9963 |
| Hotel accommodation (tariff Rs. 7,500 and above) | 18% | 9963 |
| IT and software services | 18% | 9983 |
| Financial services | 18% | 9971 |
| Legal services | 18% | 9982 |
| Restaurant services (AC) | 5% (no ITC) | 9963 |
| Event management services | 18% | 998596 |
| Insurance premium | 18% | 9971 |
| Telecom services | 18% | 9984 |
| Construction (works contract) | 12% or 18% | 9954 |

## Composition Scheme Rates (2026)

| Business Type | Composition Rate | Turnover Limit |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Manufacturers | 1% of turnover | Up to Rs. 1.5 crore |
| Restaurants (not serving alcohol) | 5% of turnover | Up to Rs. 1.5 crore |
| Traders and others | 1% of turnover | Up to Rs. 1.5 crore |
| Service providers (special category) | 6% of turnover | Up to Rs. 50 lakh |

## Real-World Examples

### Example A: Manufacturing Company

A textile manufacturer in Surat sells cotton fabric at Rs. 1,00,000. GST at 5% applies, resulting in a tax of Rs. 5,000. The buyer, a garment exporter, claims ITC on this input — making the effective cost Rs. 1,00,000 upon export refund.

### Example B: Service Provider

A Bengaluru-based software firm raises an invoice of Rs. 10,00,000 for software development services to a Delhi client. IGST at 18% applies, amounting to Rs. 1,80,000. The Delhi client claims this as ITC.

## Industry Impact

GST rate rationalisation has been a recurring agenda at Council meetings. The move towards a two-rate structure (merging 12% and 18%) has been discussed but not yet implemented. Sector-specific rate reductions in insurance, cement, and real estate are under active consideration for 2026. The cess on luxury goods remains a contentious issue as states push for higher devolution of cess revenue.

> [!IMPORTANT] Always verify rates before filing
> The GST Council periodically revises rates. Always verify the latest notification on the GSTN portal before filing. Incorrect HSN/SAC classification attracts penalties and interest.

## Key Insights

- Exports and SEZ supplies are zero-rated, allowing full ITC refunds
- Small businesses should evaluate if the Composition Scheme offers a lower compliance and tax burden
- Transitional issues such as mismatched ITC claims remain a major audit trigger
- Mixed supplies are taxed at the highest GST rate among all components

## FAQs

### Q1. Is the 28% GST slab being phased out?

Not entirely. While the GST Council has consistently moved goods out of the 28% bracket, the slab continues to apply to luxury automobiles, tobacco products, and aerated drinks. A complete phase-out is not on the current agenda.

### Q2. What is the GST rate on gold and jewellery?

Gold attracts GST at **3%**, while making charges on jewellery are taxed at **5%**. This was a deliberate policy choice to keep gold accessible while still bringing it under the tax net.

### Q3. How are mixed supplies taxed under GST?

A mixed supply is taxed at the highest GST rate among all components. For example, if a gift hamper contains items taxed at 5%, 12%, and 18%, the entire hamper is taxed at **18%**.

### Q4. Is there GST on rent for residential properties?

Rent on residential properties used for residential purposes is exempt from GST. However, if a company rents a residential apartment for its employees, GST at 18% may apply under RCM if the landlord is unregistered.

### Q5. Do petroleum products fall under GST?

No. Crude oil, petrol, diesel, aviation turbine fuel, and natural gas are currently outside the GST framework. They are taxed under central and state excise duties and VAT. Bringing petroleum under GST has been debated but deferred repeatedly.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: GST on Chemicals in India: Rates, HSN Codes & Industry Impact
articles.excerpt: Comprehensive guide to GST on chemicals in India: HSN chapters, rates for fertilisers, dyes, agrochemicals, plastics, and specialty chemicals, with real-world examples and ITC implications.
articles.content:
## Introduction

The Indian chemical industry is one of the largest in the world, contributing approximately 7% to the country's GDP and employing millions across sectors ranging from agrochemicals and petrochemicals to specialty chemicals and dyes. GST has brought this fragmented sector under a uniform tax regime, replacing a complex web of central excise, VAT, and entry taxes.

Chemical taxation under GST depends on the specific HSN chapter, end use, and whether the chemical is an input, intermediate, or finished product. This guide provides a structured reference for manufacturers, traders, and importers.

## GST Rate Table: Key Chemical Categories

| Chemical Category | GST Rate | HSN Chapter | Examples |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Basic inorganic chemicals | 18% | Chapter 28 | Sulphuric acid, hydrochloric acid, caustic soda |
| Basic organic chemicals | 18% | Chapter 29 | Methanol, acetic acid, benzene |
| Fertilisers | 5% | Chapter 31 | Urea, DAP, complex fertilisers |
| Dyes and pigments | 18% | Chapter 32 | Reactive dyes, azo dyes, titanium dioxide |
| Essential oils and cosmetic chemicals | 18% | Chapter 33 | Perfume compounds, cosmetic bases |
| Soaps and detergents | 18% | Chapter 34 | Sodium lauryl sulphate, washing powder base |
| Explosives | 18% | Chapter 36 | Industrial explosives (mining use) |
| Pesticides and agrochemicals | 18% | Chapter 38 | Insecticides, fungicides, herbicides |
| Plastics (raw material) | 18% | Chapter 39 | Polypropylene, PVC, polyethylene |
| Rubber (synthetic) | 18% | Chapter 40 | SBR rubber, nitrile rubber |
| Speciality chemicals (pharma intermediates) | 18% | Chapter 29 | API precursors, synthesis intermediates |
| Chemical waste / by-products | 18% | Chapter 38 | Glycerol, tall oil |

## HSN Code Reference

| HSN Chapter | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 28 | Inorganic chemicals, compounds of precious metals |
| 29 | Organic chemicals |
| 30 | Pharmaceutical products |
| 31 | Fertilisers |
| 32 | Tanning or dyeing extracts, dyes, pigments, paints, varnishes |
| 33 | Essential oils, perfumery, cosmetic preparations |
| 34 | Soap, washing preparations, waxes |
| 35 | Albuminoidal substances, starches, enzymes |
| 36 | Explosives, pyrotechnic products, matches |
| 38 | Miscellaneous chemical products |
| 39 | Plastics and articles thereof |

## Applicability

- Chemicals **exported** are zero-rated; full ITC refund is available
- Import of chemicals attracts Basic Customs Duty plus IGST at applicable rates
- **Job work** on chemicals (contract manufacturing) attracts 18% GST
- Fertilisers attract only **5% GST**, making them the lowest-taxed chemical category
- Hazardous chemicals require additional transport documentation but the GST treatment mirrors non-hazardous equivalents in the same HSN chapter

## Real-World Examples

### Example A: Fertiliser Manufacturer

A DAP manufacturer in Gujarat sells 1,000 tonnes to agricultural cooperatives. Invoice value: Rs. 60,00,000. GST at 5% = Rs. 3,00,000. The cooperative can claim ITC if registered. Farmers purchasing directly do not claim ITC.

### Example B: Specialty Dye Exporter

A reactive dye manufacturer in Surat exports dyes worth Rs. 2,00,00,000. As exports are zero-rated, no GST is charged on the export invoice. The manufacturer claims a refund of ITC paid on raw materials (18% on base chemicals), resulting in significant cash flow benefit.

### Example C: Petrochemical Trader

A trader selling polypropylene granules to a packaging manufacturer charges IGST at 18%. The packaging manufacturer claims the full ITC, making the effective cost of polypropylene equal to the pre-tax rate.

## Industry Impact

- **Elimination of cascading:** The pre-GST era had significant tax-on-tax issues in chemical supply chains. GST's seamless ITC chain has largely eliminated this, reducing embedded costs by an estimated 2–4% in many sub-sectors.
- **Export competitiveness:** Prompt ITC refunds on exports have improved working capital for chemical exporters, though delays in refund processing remain an issue.
- **Classification disputes:** The chemical industry has seen numerous advance rulings and litigation on HSN classification, particularly for products straddling multiple chapters.
- **Agrochemical sub-sector:** The 18% GST on pesticides has been criticised as too high for products used by farmers. Industry bodies have repeatedly lobbied for a reduction to 12% or 5%.

> [!TIP] Seek advance rulings for complex products
> HSN classification disputes in chemicals are common. Seek advance rulings from GST authorities for novel or complex products. Maintaining detailed batch records and chemical specifications is essential to support classification in case of audits.

## Key Insights

- ITC on capital goods (reactors, distillation columns) is fully available — a significant benefit for greenfield chemical plants
- Job work provisions allow principal manufacturers to send chemicals for processing without GST if returned within the prescribed time
- Exports are zero-rated: full ITC refund is available on all input taxes

## FAQs

### Q1. Is there GST on laboratory-grade chemicals used for research?

Yes. Laboratory chemicals used for R&D attract the same GST rates as commercial-grade chemicals based on their HSN classification. If a research institution is notified under the IGST Act as an eligible recipient, it may claim refunds on taxes paid.

### Q2. What is the GST treatment for chemical imports?

Imported chemicals attract Basic Customs Duty, Social Welfare Surcharge, and IGST at the rate applicable to the domestic equivalent. The IGST paid on imports can be claimed as ITC by registered importers.

### Q3. How are chemical mixtures classified under GST?

Chemical mixtures are classified based on the essential character of the mixture or the component that gives the product its identity. If the mixture cannot be classified under a specific chapter, it falls under **Chapter 38** (miscellaneous chemical products). Advance ruling is recommended for ambiguous cases.

### Q4. Is there GST on industrial gases like oxygen, nitrogen, and argon?

Yes. Industrial gases classified under Chapter 28 attract **12% GST**. Liquefied gases for industrial use similarly attract 12%. However, medical oxygen supplied to hospitals has been periodically exempt or concessionally taxed during health emergencies.

### Q5. Do chemical distributors need to issue HSN codes on invoices?

Yes. All registered suppliers with turnover above Rs. 5 crore must mention the 6-digit HSN code on invoices. Businesses between Rs. 1.5 crore and Rs. 5 crore must use 4-digit codes. Businesses below Rs. 1.5 crore need not mention HSN codes, though doing so is best practice.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Dividend Tax \u2014 How It Works for Indian Investors
articles.excerpt: Complete guide to dividend taxation in India post-DDT abolition: TDS rules, Forms 15G/15H, REIT distributions, Section 80M, US stock dividends with FTC, and strategies for income investors.
articles.content:
## Overview

Few tax rules have been as disruptive for income-oriented investors as the 2020 abolition of the **Dividend Distribution Tax (DDT)**. Before that change, companies paid a 15% DDT on dividends before distributing them, and shareholders received the dividend tax-free. Since April 1, 2020, that system is gone. Today, dividends flow to shareholders in full and are taxed in the hands of the recipient at their applicable slab rate.

This shift was particularly jarring for high-income investors who had built portfolios around tax-efficient dividend income. Someone in the 30% bracket now pays 30% plus cess on every rupee of dividend income, compared to an effective tax of around 20% under the old DDT structure.

This guide covers dividend taxation across stocks, mutual funds, and REITs, and outlines how to approach dividend income strategically in the current framework.

## Dividend Tax Rates at a Glance

| Source of Dividend | Tax Treatment | TDS Rate | Who Deducts TDS |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Indian Listed Equity Shares | Slab rate | 10% (if dividend exceeds Rs. 5,000 p.a. per company) | Company / Registrar |
| Equity Mutual Funds (IDCW option) | Slab rate | 10% (if payout exceeds Rs. 5,000 p.a.) | Mutual Fund AMC |
| Debt Mutual Funds (IDCW option) | Slab rate | 10% (if payout exceeds Rs. 5,000 p.a.) | Mutual Fund AMC |
| US Stocks (Indian resident) | Slab rate (after FTC) | 15% (US withholds, DTAA rate) | US company |
| REITs | Varies: interest taxed; dividend exempt if DDT-paid | 10% on interest component | REIT |
| NRI shareholders (Indian company) | 20% (flat) | 20% | Company |

> [!NOTE] TDS is not the final tax
> TDS is only an advance deduction. The actual tax depends on the investor's total income and applicable slab. File your ITR to claim refund if TDS exceeded your actual liability, or pay the shortfall as self-assessment tax.

## Understanding TDS on Dividends

TDS of 10% is deducted when dividends from a **single company** exceed Rs. 5,000 in a financial year. For mutual funds, it is Rs. 5,000 from a single AMC. If your actual tax rate is lower than 10% (for instance, if your total income is within the basic exemption), you can claim a refund by filing your ITR.

Conversely, if your actual rate is 30%, you must pay the remaining 20% (plus cess and surcharge) through self-assessment.

### Form 15G / 15H

If your total income is below the taxable threshold and you do not want TDS deducted, you can submit **Form 15G** (for individuals below 60) or **Form 15H** (for senior citizens) to the company or fund house. This prevents TDS deduction at source.

## Calculation Example

**Scenario:** Portfolio of dividend-paying stocks.

Sunita holds shares across 5 companies. In FY 2024–25, she receives the following dividends:

| Company | Dividend Received | TDS (10%) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Infosys | Rs. 18,000 | Rs. 1,800 |
| HDFC Bank | Rs. 12,000 | Rs. 1,200 |
| ITC | Rs. 8,500 | Rs. 850 |
| L&T | Rs. 4,200 | Nil (below Rs. 5,000) |
| Reliance | Rs. 6,000 | Rs. 600 |
| **Total** | **Rs. 48,700** | **Rs. 4,450** |

Sunita's other income: Rs. 9,00,000. Total income including dividends: Rs. 9,48,700. She falls in the 20% slab on this portion.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Tax on dividends at 20% | Rs. 9,740 |
| Less: TDS already deducted | Rs. 4,450 |
| Additional tax to pay | Rs. 5,290 |
| Add Cess at 4% | Rs. 210 |
| **Net additional tax liability** | **~Rs. 5,500** |

## REIT Dividend Taxation (A Special Case)

REITs distribute income in three components: interest, dividend, and capital gains. Each is taxed differently.

| REIT Distribution Type | Tax Treatment |
| --- | --- |
| Interest component | Taxed at slab rate |
| Dividend (from SPVs that have paid tax) | Exempt from tax |
| Capital gains on REIT units sold | Treated as equity (LTCG at 12.5% after 12 months) |
| Return of capital | Reduces cost of acquisition (not immediately taxed) |

This makes REIT distributions considerably more complex to track, but the dividend component being tax-exempt is a genuine advantage for income investors.

## Exemptions

### Section 80M — Deduction for Companies Receiving Dividends

If a domestic company receives dividends from another domestic company, it can deduct the dividend income it further distributes to its own shareholders. This prevents cascading taxation at the corporate level.

### Form 15G / 15H

Prevents TDS deduction if your total income is below the taxable threshold. Not a tax exemption per se, but prevents unnecessary withholding.

### Foreign Tax Credit

For dividends from US stocks where withholding tax has been deducted at source in the US, claim the credit in India using Form 67 to avoid double taxation.

## Strategy Tips

1. **Prefer growth option over IDCW in mutual funds.** The IDCW option creates an immediate tax liability at your slab rate each time a payout is made. The growth option defers taxation to redemption. For long-term wealth creation, growth is almost always more tax-efficient, particularly for investors in higher brackets.
2. **Consolidate dividend income to avoid crossing TDS thresholds unnecessarily.** Since TDS is triggered per company above Rs. 5,000, consider consolidating stock holdings to reduce administrative complexity, although this does not reduce the actual tax liability.
3. **Use dividend income to fund expenses rather than reinvest.** If you are in retirement or need income, dividend-paying stocks serve a purpose. But for accumulation-phase investors, the growth option delivers higher after-tax compounding.
4. **Track inter-company dividends for corporate investors.** Companies holding equity of other companies must carefully track Section 80M to avoid paying tax on dividends they have already received and are distributing onward.
5. **Factor dividends into advance tax calculations.** Dividend income is unpredictable in timing and amount. When a large dividend is announced (often announced weeks before the record date), revise your advance tax estimate for the quarter accordingly to avoid interest penalties.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. I received Rs. 3,500 as dividend from a company. No TDS was deducted. Do I still need to declare it?

Yes. **All dividend income is taxable regardless of amount**, even if TDS is not deducted because the amount falls below the Rs. 5,000 threshold. You must include it under Income from Other Sources in your ITR and pay tax at your applicable slab rate.

### Q2. Can dividend income from stocks be used to offset capital losses in my portfolio?

No. Dividend income is classified as Income from Other Sources, while capital losses arise under Capital Gains. You cannot set off one head of income against losses from another. Capital losses can only be set off against capital gains.

### Q3. My company declared a dividend in March 2025 but credited it to my account in April 2025. In which year is it taxable?

Dividend income is taxable in the **year of receipt, not declaration**. Since the dividend was credited in April 2025 (FY 2025–26), it will be taxed in that financial year, not in FY 2024–25. TDS would have been deducted at the time of payment and would reflect in FY 2025–26 Form 26AS.

### Q4. Are dividends from foreign mutual funds or ETFs held directly taxed the same way?

Dividends from foreign funds are taxed as income from other sources at your slab rate. If foreign tax was withheld at source, you can claim a Foreign Tax Credit in India using Form 67. You must also report the investment in Schedule FA (Foreign Assets) of your ITR.

### Q5. A relative gifted me 1,000 shares of a blue-chip company. I received dividends on them this year. Is the dividend taxable in my hands or in my relative's hands?

Since the shares were gifted (not from a spouse or minor child where clubbing rules apply), the dividend is taxable entirely in **your hands** as the current beneficial owner. Gifts from non-relatives that exceed Rs. 50,000 in value may themselves be taxable as income in the year of receipt, but dividend income thereafter belongs to you.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: Section 80C: The Complete Guide to India's Most Popular Tax Deduction
articles.excerpt: Complete guide to Section 80C deductions in India: the Rs. 1.5 lakh limit, eligible instruments (EPF, PPF, ELSS, life insurance, tuition fees, home loan principal), and tips to maximise savings.
articles.content:
## Overview

Section 80C is the cornerstone of tax planning for most Indian taxpayers. It allows individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) to reduce their taxable income by investing in or spending on specific government-approved instruments. For millions of salaried professionals, 80C is the first stop on the tax-saving journey every financial year.

The deduction is available under the Income Tax Act, 1961, and covers a wide range of options — from provident funds and insurance premiums to school tuition fees and home loan principal repayment. The sheer variety of qualifying instruments makes 80C flexible enough to suit almost every income bracket and risk appetite.

What makes 80C particularly powerful is that it is not just about investments. **Mandatory contributions like EPF deductions from your salary, or your child's school tuition fees, already count toward this limit** without you doing anything extra. Many taxpayers unknowingly exhaust a portion of this limit before they even begin active tax planning.

## Eligibility

Section 80C is available to:

- **Individual** taxpayers (residents and non-residents)
- **Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs)**

It is **not** available to:

- Partnership firms
- Companies
- LLPs

> [!IMPORTANT] Old regime only
> Taxpayers opting for the new tax regime under Section 115BAC cannot claim deductions under Section 80C. This is one of the most critical trade-offs to evaluate before choosing your tax regime for the year.

## Deduction Limits

| Parameter | Details |
| --- | --- |
| Maximum deduction | Rs. 1,50,000 per financial year |
| Combined ceiling with 80CCC and 80CCD(1) | Rs. 1,50,000 |
| Tax regime eligibility | Old regime only |
| Applicable to | Individuals and HUFs |

> [!NOTE] Shared ceiling with 80CCC and 80CCD(1)
> 80CCC (pension plan premium) and 80CCD(1) (NPS employee contribution) share the Rs. 1.5 lakh ceiling with 80C. If you invest Rs. 50,000 in NPS under 80CCD(1), your effective 80C room reduces to Rs. 1,00,000.

## Qualifying Instruments Under Section 80C

| Instrument | Lock-in Period | Risk Level |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Employee Provident Fund (EPF) | Till retirement | Low |
| Public Provident Fund (PPF) | 15 years | Low |
| National Savings Certificate (NSC) | 5 years | Low |
| ELSS Mutual Funds | 3 years | Market-linked |
| Tax-saving Fixed Deposits | 5 years | Low |
| Life Insurance Premium (LIC / others) | Policy term | Low |
| Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana (SSY) | Till girl child turns 21 | Low |
| Senior Citizens Savings Scheme (SCSS) | 5 years | Low |
| Home Loan Principal Repayment | As per loan tenure | N/A |
| Children's Tuition Fees | N/A (expense) | N/A |
| National Pension System — 80CCD(1) | Till age 60 | Market-linked |
| Unit Linked Insurance Plans (ULIPs) | 5 years | Market-linked |

## Example Calculation

Rajan is a salaried professional earning Rs. 12,00,000 per year (gross) under the old tax regime.

### Existing 80C contributions

| Item | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| EPF deduction from salary | Rs. 36,000 (auto-deducted) |
| LIC premium | Rs. 24,000 |
| **Total so far** | **Rs. 60,000** |
| Remaining 80C room | Rs. 90,000 |

He then invests Rs. 90,000 in ELSS mutual funds to fill the limit.

| Component | Amount |
| --- | --- |
| Total taxable income reduction | Rs. 1,50,000 |
| Tax saved at 20% slab | Rs. 30,000 |
| Additional saving from 4% cess | Rs. 1,200 |
| **Total tax saved** | **Rs. 31,200** |

> [!TIP] Map existing contributions first
> If Rajan had not tracked his EPF and LIC contributions, he might have over-invested in ELSS, wasting money in locked-in instruments unnecessarily.

## Optimization Tips

1. **Always map your existing contributions first.** EPF, LIC premiums, and tuition fees count before you invest a rupee extra.
2. **Prefer ELSS if you want growth potential.** Among all 80C options, ELSS has the shortest lock-in (3 years) and market-linked returns, making it the most efficient from a wealth-creation angle.
3. **Use PPF for long-term, risk-free compounding.** The interest is tax-free (not just tax-deferred), giving it a significant edge over NSC or tax-saver FDs.
4. **Do not buy insurance purely for tax saving.** Mixing insurance and investment rarely ends well. Buy term insurance separately, and use dedicated instruments for 80C.
5. **Time your investments.** ELSS investments via SIP throughout the year avoid the last-minute February-March rush and give you rupee cost averaging benefits.
6. **Check spouse or HUF capacity.** Each filer gets a separate Rs. 1.5 lakh limit.

## Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Assuming the 80C limit is Rs. 1.5 lakh on top of EPF.** EPF counts within the Rs. 1.5 lakh ceiling. Many people over-invest not realising this.
2. **Buying endowment or money-back policies for 80C benefits.** The returns are poor, the lock-in is long, and the insurance coverage is inadequate. This is perhaps the most common and costly 80C mistake.
3. **Investing in 80C instruments after switching to the new tax regime.** Contributions made after opting for the new regime do not yield any 80C benefit.
4. **Ignoring NSC interest reinvestment.** The interest accrued on NSC after the first year is treated as a fresh 80C-eligible investment each year. This is a hidden benefit many miss.
5. **Forgetting to claim tuition fees for children.** You can claim fees paid to any school, college, or university for up to two children. This is often overlooked in salary-class households.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. Can I claim 80C deduction for principal repayment on a home loan taken for a second property?

Yes, you can claim principal repayment under 80C for a second home loan as well. However, if you sell the property within 5 years of purchase, the deductions claimed will be added back to your income in the year of sale.

### Q2. Is there any way to go beyond the Rs. 1.5 lakh 80C limit?

Yes. Section 80CCD(1B) allows an **additional Rs. 50,000 deduction** for NPS contributions, over and above the 80C ceiling. This brings your total deduction potential to Rs. 2 lakh on retirement savings alone.

### Q3. My employer contributes to NPS on my behalf. Does that count under 80C?

No. Employer contributions to NPS are deductible under **Section 80CCD(2)**, which has its own separate limit and does not eat into your 80C ceiling.

### Q4. Can a HUF invest in ELSS and claim 80C deduction?

Yes. HUFs are eligible for 80C deductions and can invest in ELSS in the HUF's name to claim the benefit.

### Q5. Can I claim 80C for a life insurance policy I took for my spouse or children?

Yes. Premium paid for life insurance on the life of your spouse or children qualifies for 80C deduction, subject to the overall Rs. 1.5 lakh limit.
articles.author: Mridhuv Associates

articles.title: How to Save Tax at Different Income Levels: Strategies for Rs. 10L to Rs. 1 Crore+
articles.excerpt: Practical tax-saving strategies for Indian salaried professionals at Rs. 10L, 20L, 30L, 50L, and 1 crore+ income levels: regime comparison, deductions, surcharge thresholds, and HNI-specific tools.
articles.content:
## Overview

Tax planning is not a one-size-fits-all exercise. The right strategy for someone earning Rs. 10 lakh per year is very different from what makes sense at Rs. 50 lakh or Rs. 1 crore. As income rises, the marginal tax rate changes, the instruments that offer meaningful savings change, and the trade-offs between the old and new tax regimes shift considerably.

This guide walks you through practical, income-level-specific tax saving strategies under the Indian tax system, grounded in the FY 2024-25 / AY 2025-26 framework. All calculations assume the individual is salaried unless noted otherwise.

## Tax Slab Reference

### Old Regime (FY 2024-25)

| Income Slab | Tax Rate |
| --- | --- |
| Up to Rs. 2,50,000 | Nil |
| Rs. 2,50,001 to Rs. 5,00,000 | 5% |
| Rs. 5,00,001 to Rs. 10,00,000 | 20% |
| Above Rs. 10,00,000 | 30% |

### New Regime (FY 2024-25)

| Income Slab | Tax Rate |
| --- | --- |
| Up to Rs. 3,00,000 | Nil |
| Rs. 3,00,001 to Rs. 7,00,000 | 5% |
| Rs. 7,00,001 to Rs. 10,00,000 | 10% |
| Rs. 10,00,001 to Rs. 12,00,000 | 15% |
| Rs. 12,00,001 to Rs. 15,00,000 | 20% |
| Above Rs. 15,00,000 | 30% |

> [!NOTE] Standard deduction
> Rs. 75,000 standard deduction is available under the new regime as well (enhanced in Budget 2024).

## Strategy for Rs. 10 Lakh Income

At Rs. 10 lakh gross, the **new tax regime often wins** for those with minimal investments.

### Without investments

| Regime | Taxable Income | Approx Tax |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Old (SD Rs. 50K + 80C Rs. 1.5L) | Rs. 8,00,000 | Rs. 92,500 |
| New (SD Rs. 75K) | Rs. 9,25,000 | Rs. 54,600 |

New regime saves roughly **Rs. 37,900** without any investment.

### With home loan and full 80C

| Regime | Taxable Income | Approx Tax |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Old (SD + 80C Rs. 1.5L + 24b Rs. 2L) | Rs. 6,00,000 | ~Rs. 32,500 |

The old regime wins decisively with a home loan.

**Key tools at Rs. 10 lakh:** 80C, 80D (health insurance), HRA exemption (if renting), and 24(b) if you have a home loan.

## Strategy for Rs. 20 Lakh Income

At Rs. 20 lakh, you are firmly in the 30% slab in both regimes. The value of each deduction is higher.

### Maximum deductions available

| Deduction | Section | Amount |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Standard deduction | Salary | Rs. 50,000 |
| Investments (EPF, ELSS, PPF, insurance) | 80C | Rs. 1,50,000 |
| NPS voluntary contribution | 80CCD(1B) | Rs. 50,000 |
| Health insurance (self + parents) | 80D | Rs. 50,000 to Rs. 75,000 |
| Home loan interest | 24(b) | Rs. 2,00,000 |
| HRA exemption (if applicable) | 10(13A) | Varies |

**Potential total deductions:** Rs. 5,00,000 to Rs. 5,75,000

At 30% slab, Rs. 5 lakh in deductions saves Rs. 1,50,000 + cess. The **old regime** is typically better at Rs. 20 lakh for individuals who are home buyers or have significant health insurance outgo.

## Strategy for Rs. 30 Lakh Income

At Rs. 30 lakh, you are deep in the 30% bracket. Tax planning becomes multi-dimensional.

### Additional tools beyond the standard stack

- **NPS via employer (80CCD(2)):** If your employer contributes to NPS, up to 10% of basic + DA is deductible in **both regimes**. This is the only deduction available in the new regime beyond the standard deduction.
- **House property loss:** If you have a second or rented property, the loss from house property (interest minus rent received) can offset up to Rs. 2 lakh of other income.
- **Capital gains optimisation:** At Rs. 30 lakh, managing short-term vs long-term capital gains becomes important. Shift profit-booking to long-term wherever possible.

### Estimated tax saved through full optimisation (old regime)

| Deduction | Amount | Tax Saved at 30% |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 80C | Rs. 1,50,000 | Rs. 45,000 |
| 80CCD(1B) | Rs. 50,000 | Rs. 15,000 |
| 80D | Rs. 75,000 | Rs. 22,500 |
| 24(b) — home loan | Rs. 2,00,000 | Rs. 60,000 |
| **Total** | **Rs. 4,75,000** | **Rs. 1,42,500** |

## Strategy for Rs. 50 Lakh Income

At Rs. 50 lakh, **surcharge of 10% applies** under both regimes. This increases effective tax rate significantly.

### Key instruments that remain relevant

1. All standard deductions (80C, 80CCD(1B), 80D, 24(b)) in the old regime.
2. **Business income restructuring:** High earners often shift to a professional structure, allowing deduction of actual business expenses.
3. **Section 54 / 54F (capital gains exemption):** If you sell property or assets and reinvest in residential property, gains can be exempt.
4. **Charitable donations under 80G:** 100% or 50% deductions on donations to eligible institutions (not political parties).
5. **HUF creation:** Creating a separate HUF entity allows a second set of basic exemption limits and investments.

> [!TIP] Focus shifts at Rs. 50 lakh+
> The goal moves from "maximise deductions" to "income restructuring and deferral."

## Strategy for Rs. 1 Crore+ Income

At income above Rs. 1 crore, the **surcharge is 15%** under both regimes (for income between Rs. 1 crore and Rs. 2 crore). The effective tax rate can exceed 42%.

### Strategies relevant at this level

1. **Employer NPS under 80CCD(2)** — available even in the new regime. At 30% + 15% surcharge + cess, Rs. 10 lakh contributed by employer to NPS saves approximately Rs. 4.6 lakh in tax.
2. **Family income splitting** — paying salary to family members for genuine work in a business lowers overall tax by utilising their lower slabs.
3. **Gratuity, VRS, and leave encashment planning** — for those nearing retirement, these provide significant exempt payouts.
4. **LTCG exemption harvesting** — selling equity holdings strategically to realise LTCG within the Rs. 1.25 lakh annual exemption each year.
5. **Trusts and foundations** — ultra-HNIs may use charitable trusts to redirect income in a tax-efficient and socially impactful manner.

## Optimization Tips

1. **Determine your regime first, then invest.** Too many taxpayers invest in 80C instruments and then realise they should have been in the new regime. Run the comparison annually before making any commitment.
2. **As income rises, focus on tax-free income sources.** PPF interest, LTCG from equity up to Rs. 1.25 lakh, and maturity from life insurance (subject to conditions) are all tax-free.
3. **Employer NPS works at every income level in the new regime.** This is the one universal deduction available under the new regime.
4. **Do not ignore 80D at higher incomes.** Rs. 75,000 of health insurance premiums (self + senior citizen parents) saves Rs. 22,500 at 30% + surcharge. This is pure insurance you need anyway.
5. **Review tax strategy every year**, not just in March. Market conditions, salary changes, and regulation changes make an annual review essential.

## Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Defaulting to the old regime out of habit at lower income levels.** For incomes under Rs. 12–13 lakh with limited deductions, the new regime is often better.
2. **Treating all deductions as equally valuable.** A deduction worth Rs. 1 lakh saves Rs. 20,000 at 20% slab and Rs. 30,000 at 30% slab. Not all instruments are equally worth the lock-in.
3. **Ignoring surcharge thresholds.** At Rs. 50 lakh and Rs. 1 crore, surcharge kicks in abruptly. Sometimes earning slightly below the threshold or structuring income slightly differently can save a disproportionate amount of tax.
4. **Not using Section 87A rebate at lower incomes.** Under the new regime, if taxable income is up to Rs. 7 lakh, the rebate under 87A brings the tax to zero. This is an enormous benefit that many taxpayers under Rs. 7 lakh income miss by defaulting to the old regime unnecessarily.
5. **Waiting till February to plan.** Investments made in March create rushed decisions. The best tax planning begins in April at the start of the financial year.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### Q1. At what income level does the old tax regime clearly beat the new one?

Generally, if your total deductions (80C + 80D + 24(b) + HRA + NPS) exceed **Rs. 3.75 lakh**, the old regime becomes more beneficial. This threshold shifts depending on your specific income level and deduction mix.

### Q2. Can I switch between old and new tax regime every year?

Salaried individuals can switch every year. Self-employed individuals and business owners who opt for the new regime cannot switch back and forth freely; once they opt out of the new regime, they can only return under specific conditions.

### Q3. Does the surcharge on high incomes apply under both regimes?

Yes. Surcharge applies under both regimes based on total income. However, the **new regime caps the surcharge at 25% for incomes above Rs. 5 crore**, whereas the old regime levies 37% surcharge. This makes the new regime more attractive for very high earners.

### Q4. What is the most effective single tax-saving instrument across all income levels?

**NPS through employer contribution under Section 80CCD(2)** is the most universally effective, because it works under both regimes, has no ceiling relative to the employer's contribution capacity, and benefits retirement savings simultaneously.

### Q5. Are there any legal ways to reduce tax below zero (get a refund) through planning?

Tax refunds are possible if TDS deducted by your employer or others exceeds your actual tax liability. This can happen if you have significant deductions that were not accounted for during TDS. Planning your declarations to your employer accurately ensures correct TDS and avoids the need to wait for a refund.
